chapters.summary: Council staff and Committee on Finance Chair Linda Lee call the joint hearing to order, silencing electronic devices and outlining the schedule for the day's budget reviews.
chapters.title: Committee on Finance Convenes Joint Executive Budget Hearing

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to today's New York City Council Executive Budget Hearing for the Committee on Finance.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Join with the Committee on Children and Youth, and join with the Committee on Immigration.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, please silence all electronic devices, and no one may approach a dais at any time.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chairs, we are ready to begin.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Wait, oh gotta gavel, always forget that part.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee welcomes attendees to day three of the FY27 Executive Budget Hearings and provides an overview of the Administration for Children's Services (ACS) proposed $3.51 billion budget, highlighting headcount vacancies and the Raise the Age Act.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Delivers Opening Statement on the ACS Budget

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to day three of the fiscal FY27 Executive Budget Hearings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee, and today's hearings will begin with the Administration for Children's Services, followed by Department of Youth and Community Development, and the Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm pleased to be joined by my colleague and dear friend, Councilmember Althea Stevens, Chair of the Committee on Children and Youth.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And today we have been joined by Councilmember Morte, Councilmember Shulman, Councilmember Epstein, Councilmember Joseph, Councilmember Wong, and Councilmember Deputy Speaker Williams is on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Welcome, Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Week two, or day seven, rather, day six to you and your team, and of course your amazing team.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12th, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for fiscal 2026 to 2030 with a proposed FY27 budget of 124.7 billion dollars.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The administration for children's services proposed fiscal FY27 budget of 3.51 billion dollars represents 2.7% of the administration's proposed FY27 budget in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this is an increase of $89.6 million dollars or 2.6% from the $3.42 billion originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This increase results from several actions, including additional funding added for child care vouchers, foster care, staffing across the agency, and a baselining of the Promise NYC program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 2026, ACS's headcount was 562 less than their FY26 budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the council's preliminary budget response, we called on the mayor to apply for the state waiver that would allow the city to apply for reimbursement of its expenditures across fiscal 2019 to 2026, related to the implementation of the state's Raise the Age Act.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee continues her opening statement, calling on the administration to address the 25,000-child waitlist for child care vouchers and expressing support for the baselining of the Promise NYC program.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Highlights Child Care Vouchers and Promise NYC

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Further, in our response, we called on the administration to continue to invest in child care vouchers by baseline the Promise NYC program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In our preliminary budget hearing, we encourage the administration to explore ways to end the waiting list practice for child care vouchers, which now stands at approximately 25,000 children.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: While we were pleased to see the Promise New York City baselined and some additional funding added for child care vouchers, I hope to see progress made in reducing the voucher wait list and ensuring these families are able to receive the vital child care services that they need, whether through ACS or other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I look forward to hearing from our new commissioner on how ACS plans to address the urgent challenge of providing child care and continue to equitably serve families amid citywide savings requirements.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: My questions today will largely focus on areas in which ACS was able to realize savings, the agency's process and methodology in identifying these savings, and updates on the nearly 200 million dollars in state funding designated for at-risk youth.

chapters.summary: Committee on Children and Youth Chair Althea Stevens delivers her opening statement, focusing on the impact of citywide savings and vacancy reduction benchmarks on vulnerable youth and families served by ACS.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Delivers Opening Statement on ACS Savings Targets

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I now want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Councilmember Stevens, for her opening statement.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Good morning, and thank you, Chairley.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: My name is Althea Stevens, and I'm serves as the chair of Committee on Children and Youth for this first part of our hearing this morning.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I look forward to discussing changes to ACS budget since the preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: The executive budget included investments in several areas, including child care vouchers, detention facilities, and staffing across the agency.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: New needs of $55.7 million were added to the fiscal 2026 in the executive plan, growing to $104.6 million in fiscal 2027 and dropping to $80.7 million in fiscal 2028 and in the outer years.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Additionally, other adjustments in fiscal 2026 totaled 547.6 million, dropping to 2 million in fiscal 2027 and in the out years.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: While the city's budget now has been balanced without property taxes increase, all the agencies were required to meet savings and vacancy reduction benchmarks.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: As agencies providing frontline services, some of the city's most vulnerable residents, understanding the impact of the savings targeted ACS is critical.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens continues her opening remarks, urging ACS to maximize state funding for youth services and Raise the Age implementation, and thanks the fiscal staff for preparing for the hearing.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Calls for Hardship Application and State Funding Accountability

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm also interested in understanding how the ADC is working with the state partners to maximize the funding and vital services such as child care vouchers, preventative preventative services and programming.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm also really curious to hear about the plan for the money that was allocated to the from the governor, because in our preliminary hearing, that plan still had not been discussed with us and want to make sure that the money that was allocated for youth services and the raise the age that we are thinking about.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And also would like to hear if we're going to put in a hardship application.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And the council's response responsibility to ensure that the city budget is fair, transparent, and accountable to New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: As the chair of Children and Youth Committee, I will continue to push for accountability and transparency in the ACS budget.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Amidst the needs to eliminate budget gaps and meet saving requirements, our responsibility is enacting a budget that reflects the needs and interests of our city's youth, taking on even higher importance.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Like the mayor has often repeated, this budget must not be balanced on the back of working people or young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm adding the or because nobody keeps talking about the young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So today, through and throughout the examination of AC budget, we ensure that this is the case.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Before we get started, I would like to take a quick moment to thank both my staff and the entire council fiscal division for their efforts in preparing for this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Margaret, Julia, Richard, Rhea, Elizabeth, and my deputy chief of staff, Greer, and the entire A Team back in the suite 16.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: At this time, I'll pass it back to Chair Lee.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee thanks the finance team, reminds attendees that public testimony will occur on June 10th, and directs the committee counsel to swear in the ACS witnesses.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Outlines Public Testimony Schedule and Swears in ACS Witnesses

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Before we get started, uh, just wanted to take a moment to thank the entire council finance team and division for preparing today's hearing, especially Julia Haramis and Margaret Barnsley for uh today's hearing, and of course, Brian Sarfo and all the finance analysts and support staff who make it all work.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just as a reminder, uh, for this year's executive budget hearings, because we're on a tighter timeline than we normally are, uh, we're going to be doing all of the public testimony on Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 9 30.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I see some of our partners here from our nonprofits that are um here with us, which I'm glad actually, because I think it's always important to be in the room and listen to the feedback, and then also hopefully it'll help inform the questions you do have when we hopefully we'll see you back on the 10th, June 10th, Wednesday, starting at 9 30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Um, so I will turn it now over to our committee counsel to swear in the witnesses for their testimony.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel administers the oath to ACS Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston and her deputy commissioners, who affirm to tell the truth before the committees.
chapters.title: ACS Witnesses Affirmed

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Um, do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Commissioner Jones Gaston.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Gandalf.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Mendez.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Linadas.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Walker.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And Deputy Commissioner Ginsberg.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: ACS Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston introduces her leadership team, thanks interim leadership, and shares her personal background and philosophy regarding safety, connection, and equity in child welfare.
chapters.title: ACS Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston Shares Personal Connection to Child Welfare

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'd like to thank Chair Stevens, Chair Lee, and the members of the Children and Youth and Finance Committees for convening today's hearing on the executive budget for City Fiscal Year 2027.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I am joined by my colleagues, Deputy Commissioner for Financial Services, Margaret Plentikoff, Deputy Commissioner for External Affairs, Stephanie Glendell, Deputy Commissioner for the Division of Youth and Family Justice, Nancy Ginsburg, Deputy Commissioner for the Family Services Division, Luisa Lenares, Deputy Commissioner for Family Permanency Services, Ina Mendez, and Deputy Commissioner for the Division of Child and Family Wellbeing, Elizabeth Volcamoore.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Before I begin, I want to acknowledge Melissa Hester for her steady and compassionate leadership as interim commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: During a period of transition, Melissa guided this agency with care, integrity, and deep commitment to children and families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I want to thank her and congratulate her on her new role as Executive Deputy Commissioner of People, Operations and Technology.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I often say, I often say that I was born into the system.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Child welfare is not simply the work I do, it is personal to me.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It has shaped my life, my sense of purpose, and my understanding of what children, youth, and families need not only to survive, but to heal, grow, and thrive.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Because I know personally what systems can mean in the life of a child, I approach this role with both urgency and hope.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Across every role, every system, and every community, one lesson has remained constant.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Meaningful and lasting change only happens when we remain grounded in the voices, experiences, strengths, and hopes of the people most impacted by our decisions.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That belief will guide my leadership at ACS.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I also recognize that this work does not happen in isolation.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The well-being of children and families depends on strong partnership across systems and sectors, including providers, advocates, community leaders, and our partners in the judiciary.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Lasting progress requires all of us working together with shared accountability and shared commitment to safety, healing, fairness, and opportunity.

chapters.summary: ACS Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston continues her testimony, outlining her commitment to preventive services, juvenile justice reform, and supporting a skilled and well-resourced workforce.
chapters.title: Commissioner Jones Gaston Emphasizes Workforce Investment and System Transformation

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe deeply that safety and connection must coexist.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Children should be safe, and families should have the support they need to remain together whenever possible.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Communities should be seen not simply through the lens of need, but through the lens of strength, wisdom, and possibility.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Children are not cases.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Families are not problems to solve.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Communities are not deficits to manage.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: They are people with stories, strengths, hopes, and potential.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And our responsibility is to shape systems that recognize humanity and respond with accountability, compassion, and intention.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As commissioner, I'm committed to working, continuing the work of strengthening child welfare system that is more responsive, more equitable, more preventive, and more humane.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That means ensuring child protective intention is focused on where children are truly unsafe.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: While continuing to invest in the community-based and family-centered supports that help families thrive before crisis occurs, it means listening closely to young people and families with lived experiences and recognizing them as partners in shaping the solutions.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And it means continuing to transform how we support young people involved in the juvenile justice system, seeing them not only for the worst moment in their lives, but for their potential, resilience, and future.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe in stewardship, I believe in partnership, I believe in accountability rooted in compassion, and I believe in systems change happens when we are willing to listen, to learn, and to act with informed intentionality.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I also believe none of this work is possible without a skilled, supported, and well-resourced workforce.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Every day, ACS staff provide ACF staff provide our partners, attorneys, clinicians, case planners, direct care staff, and so many others carry enormous responsibility on behalf of children and families, often under incredibly complex circumstances.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: If we want stronger outcomes for children and for families, we must continue investing in the people who make these outcomes possible.

chapters.summary: ACS Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston provides a high-level overview of the $3.51 billion FY27 Executive Budget, highlighting early childhood education agreements and the baselining of Promise NYC at $25 million.
chapters.title: ACS Commissioner Outlines FY27 Budget and Child Care Voucher Funding

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Today I'll provide an overview of the FY 2027 executive budget, which is projected to be $3.51 billion, including $1.4 billion in city funds.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As you know, the Mamdani administration asked each agency to identify a chief savings officer to carefully examine opportunities for savings and efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I want to thank ACS's Chief Savings Officer, Margaret Plentikoff for leading this work thoughtfully with continued focus on minimizing impacts on children, families, and providers who depend on our services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Affordable child care is essential for families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I am extremely pleased that the Mom Dani administration has prioritized expanding across the quality childcare throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS's budget furthers the commitment in a number of ways.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Due to the advocacy from the Mom Dani Administration, the City Council, and many others, the state and city have come to a number of budgetary agreements that will enable New York City to expand and maintain access to early childhood education.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As it relates to ACS, the funding from the city and the state in ACS's budget will enable us to continue to recertify the vast majority of families receiving low-income child care vouchers from ACS.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The funding will also allow the city to support the growing need for child care vouchers among families on cash assistance who are served by the Human Resources Administration.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition, we are extremely pleased to share that the FY27 executive budget restores and baselines Promise NYC at $25 million.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This funding will enable ACS to maintain city funded community based childcare.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This funding supports approximately 1,000 slots.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Historically, Promise NYC has funded was funded for one year at a time.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Baselining the funding for this crucial program creates certainty and stability.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I have dedicated my career to identifying ways to support and uplift families and communities so that they can be safe and thrive.

chapters.summary: ACS Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston discusses community partnerships, family services, and the difficult decision to close four prevention programs to realize $2.7 million in city tax levy savings.
chapters.title: ACS Commissioner Details Community Partnerships and Preventive Services

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As I take on this role, I remain committed to lifting up family-centered, prevention-driven solutions that improve outcomes and transform how systems support children, families, and communities.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I am committed to keeping children safe by providing ACS child protective staff with the tools, supervision, and training they need to make sound decisions.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I am committed to ensuring ACS helps to provide families and communities with the support they need to succeed.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS will soon announce the providers recommended for the 11 community partnerships across the city.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Community partnerships are neighborhood coalitions that enhance child and family well-being through strategies responsive to each community's specific wants and needs.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: By listening to the voices and ideas of community members, the community partnerships work to increase coordination among service providers, connect families to resources, and fill programming gaps.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The new contracts will expand the role of community ambassadors, trusted community residents who build relationships with and connect families to information and resources.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'm looking forward to the implementation of these new contracts and the positive impact the partnerships will continue to have.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS currently contracts with 44 provider agencies operating 127 programs for family services, previously referred to as prevention services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Across the system, ACS contracts for 10,277 slots, meaning more than 10,000 families can receive services at any one point in time.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In calendar year 2025, a little over 30,000 children in 14,000 families receive child welfare family support and therapeutic treatment services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS and our providers have been intentional about supporting communities, engaging families, and connecting them to resources.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Through this work, we've increased community referrals from 12% of all families served in January 2023 to 27% of them in March of 2026.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: These include referrals from schools, healthcare providers, and families themselves.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As part of our efforts to look closely at our budget and possible efficiencies, this budget includes closing four programs totaling 360 slots for savings of 2.7 million CTL and 7.1 million gross.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We will be looking working closely with the four providers as well as the providers in the same catchment area to ensure that no family loses services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We will also continue to monitor community needs and make sure families continue to have access to services.

chapters.summary: ACS Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston details foster care budget adjustments, the expansion of Fair Futures, and the launch of the Career Choice program for youth pursuing vocational training.
chapters.title: ACS Commissioner Outlines Foster Care Savings and Career Choice Program

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: When ACS was created 30 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Our work at ACS centers on continuing to reduce the number of children in care, both by preventing children from entering in the first place and from intensifying efforts to reunify children more quickly.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I am committed to working with the ACS team and our providers to continue this reduction.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: For those children and youth in our care, it is essential that we provide them with the resources and skills that they need to become successful and thriving New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This budget includes important actions that will enable us to do this.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes a number of budgetary reductions in the foster care area that will have no impact on children, youth families, or providers, as the savings are from underspending or surpluses in specific budget codes, which I'll explain in more detail.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As you recall, last fiscal year, ACS's budget included the Youth Safety and Success Initiative, which was a $40 million investment in programs for youth and foster care and the justice system.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Specifically as it relates to foster care, the initiative funded an expansion of the number of youth who could be served by Fair Futures coaches and tutors, the number of youth participating in the College Choice program, and the creation of a new program, Career Choice.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As a result of this investment, we see more youth getting connected to Fair Futures coaches, and there are over 470 youth enrolled in college choice, up from 430 last year.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition, we've created and launched Career Choice, a new program that builds off the success of college choice by connecting young people in foster care to similar financial, social, and academic support when they elect to attend a vocational program or a job readiness program instead of college.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The program was launched in October, and the first 10 youth are now enrolled in career choice programming.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition, we are on track for 136 youth to participate in the Department of Youth and Community Development's Advance and Earn program this fiscal year, which is also a part of Career Choice.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget better aligns anticipated spending levels of both Fair Futures and Career Choice with no impact on services to better match programmatic ramp up and allocated funding.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Specifically for Fair Futures, we plan for the program to grow in budget and youth served in both FY27 and 28, while saving about 2 million CTL in FY26 and 27 and 1.1 million in FY28.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We still anticipate full ramp up to 42.7 million and 54 youth by FY29.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Similarly, there is a 405,000 CTL and 1.1 million gross reduction in career choice in the current fiscal year 2026 due to the savings from the initial startup time needed for the program.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: All residential and specialized family foster care providers receive $3,500 for each youth ages 14 to 21 in their care for independent living services, referred to as preparing youth for adulthood.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: These services help youth develop life skills through workshops and programs such as financial literacy, resume writing, college applications, grocery shopping, banking, and driver's education.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes an annual reduction of 1.4 million CTL, 3.75 million gross due to a surplus in the budget code.

chapters.summary: ACS Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston explains the $97.5 million city funding addition to address the federal Title IV-E reimbursement gap and details $1.6 million allocated for immigration legal services for youth in foster care.
chapters.title: ACS Commissioner Details Title IV-E Funding Gap and Immigration Legal Services

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: There will be no impact on providers or youth.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Providers will continue to receive the $3,500 for each youth aged 14 to 21.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget also includes 233,000 CTL, 1.1 million gross reduction for adoption recruitment contracts, which ACS no longer maintains and has not since 2015.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Adoption and foster parent recruitment is now managed by our foster care providers, and their current contracts include recruitment funding.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thus, there is no interruption in service and we do not anticipate any impact on providers or families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes $97.5 million in CTL funding to address the Title IVE budget gap the ACS has been carrying due to the way the federal government reimburses for foster care.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Specifically, while federal Title IVE funding for foster care is a federal entitlement, it is only an entitlement for those children and youth who are federally eligible.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: While there are a number of eligibility requirements, family income is the one that causes most children to be ineligible for federal reimbursement.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: To be eligible, current income of the child's family must be such that they would have been eligible for AFDC, which was the predecessor of TANF in 1996.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This has never been adjusted to inflation, nor the current public assistance program.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: There was a period of time where the gap was being filled by federal government funds through the 4E waiver, but that ended in 2018.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This addition additional federal funding, excuse me.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This additional funding fills the baselines the gap ACS has been carrying.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget also includes $1.6 million for immigration legal services and fees for children and youth in foster care.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Since at least 2006, ACS has been working with legal service providers to assist youth in care by obtaining special immigrant juvenile status.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS is extremely grateful to these providers who have helped countless youth in care get green cards all pro bono.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Given the increasing demands for immigration legal assistance, ACS will now be able to provide attorneys handling these matters with $2,000 per child.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition, the Trump administration recently created new fees and increased existing fees for various immigration relief applications, and this additional funding will provide reimbursement for those fees.

chapters.summary: ACS Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston concludes her testimony by detailing capital and operational investments at the Horizon and Crossroads detention facilities and emphasizing the importance of interagency partnership.
chapters.title: ACS Commissioner Details Juvenile Justice Facility Investments and Closing Remarks

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS continues to focus on meeting the needs of the increased number of youth across our juvenile justice continuum.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As of May 13, 2026, there were 362 youth in secure detention, 66 youth in non secure detention, and 128 youth in close to home.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes funding to support operations, maintenance, and capacity needs at our facilities.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The budget includes $6.3 million baselined for juvenile justice facility needs associated with the creation of the exercise pavilion at Horizon, including the additional funding needed for maintenance repairs and operations of the existing space.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This funding will also be used for additional supplies needed for the population of youth in detention, such as uniforms, toiletries, bedding and food services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Finally, a portion of this initiative is additional funding needed to support the conversion of a six-bed non-secure placement facility to a nine-bed non-secure placement facility to help with the increased close to home census.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget also includes additional capital needs for our juvenile justice programs, including funding for the Horizon Annex, HVACs at Horizon and Crosswoods, Crossroads, as well as our other repairs and IT equipment at Horizon and Crossroads.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So in closing, I believe this executive budget reflects an important investment in children, youth, and families and communities across New York City, and it represents an opportunity to continue shaping systems that are not only effective but also compassionate, responsive, and grounded in partnership.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As I begin in this role, I do so with humility, urgency, and hope.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Any one agency or system alone.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It requires partnership across government with providers, advocates, communities, and with our partners in the judiciary, and most importantly, with young people and families whose lived experience must continue to inform and shape our work.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: At the same time, we are navigating a period of significant change and uncertainty nationally.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Federal policy shifts and broader pressures on social service systems continue to impact child welfare and youth serving systems across the country.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: But even as systems adapt, transform, and respond to those pressures, our responsibility must remain clear.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Children, youth, and families must be at the center of every decision we make.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This moment calls not only for responsiveness, but for intentional transformation rooted in equity, community partnership, accountability, and the belief that every child and family deserves dignity, support, stability, and possibility.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe deeply in the power of connection and intentional action.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe children, youth, and families deserve systems that see their humanity, honor their strengths, and create real pathways to healing, stability, opportunity, and belonging.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I believe our collective responsibility is not simply to respond to crisis, but to help create conditions where children and families and communities can truly thrive.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That's the work ahead of us, and I look forward to doing it in partnership with all of you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you, and I welcome your questions.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks ACS Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston to explain the data-driven approach and metrics used to identify recurring savings opportunities for the FY27 Executive Plan.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions ACS on Savings Methodology

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Before I go into my questions, just want to recognize we've been joined by Councilmember Morano on Zoom, Councilmembers Ose, Councilmember Felder, Councilmember Jay Sanchez, and Councilmember Mealy.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And thank you so much, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We have two former nonprofit leaders up here, which really greatly appreciate the work and the portfolio that ACS has.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I know that you are in charge of a lot and there's a lot on your plate.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so one of the things I hate is our silos in government, and so whatever we can do on the council, we've always tried to champion ways to make sure that the agencies are also talking to each other and ways that we can play a helpful role in that as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just wanted to, I have a few questions before I pass it on to the chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So, in terms of the savings exercises, I know that every agency had a target goal for the FY27 executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How did ACS determine which savings to include in the executive plan and what was the process in terms of what metrics were used to make the assessment?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Sure, good morning.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So, as part of the Executive Order 12, all agencies were required to identify recurring savings opportunities across their entire budget and review agency operations using a data-driven approach focused on operational efficiencies, program performance, and long-term sustainability.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: At ACS, we focused on aligning the budget to reflect our projected revenues, took savings from sunsetting programs and contracts, and we also looked across the entire agency budget and evaluated contracts based on performance and agency need.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Our focus throughout the process was to find savings and efficiencies that would not eliminate services for children, youth, and families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay, because I know that when we talked about savings on the council side, and I know the speaker has said this as well, we really wanted to emphasize that we don't want to make we want to make sure that services are not cut.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks whether any direct services or programs will see a decrease in funding due to the savings exercise, and Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston details the sunsetting of administrative and prevention contracts.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Inquires About Direct Service Impacts of Savings Measures

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And so, you know, just wanting to make sure that that was actually the case because you said sunsetting contracts and looking at other programs, and so uh which programs and services, if at all, were included in the savings measures, and will any direct services or programs see a decrease in funding from the savings exercise?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um sure.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So the the majority of our savings initiatives actually came out of maximizing revenue, so um more than half came through um realizing additional fringe that we were able to receive as well as additional miscellaneous revenues out of audit.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, in terms of um programs that were sunsetted, we sunsetted some administrative contracts.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Uh we made reductions for IT savings based on underspending in uh our Xerox contract.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um we had some programs that had already sunset, including Esperanza.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um we also looked at uh looking at surpluses in our committee on special education funding.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um we slowed down the ramp up of some programs that we had received funding as of uh last exec for our youth safety and success initiative, and that includes identifying accrualed savings accrued savings in FY26 for the career choice program as well as slowing the ramp up of our Fair Futures program.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And then we also had savings through prevention contracts.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um we are ending four contracts for prevention.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And so that all of the family and children will continue to be served.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And a lot of the slowing of the ramp up was because of capacity hiring processes, that sort of thing, like to onboard.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I know that my colleagues probably have more questions about some of those, so I'll save that for them.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks about the total loss in state and federal funding from the savings measures and whether ACS met its overall savings target of 1.5 percent.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions ACS on State and Federal Funding Reductions

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And several savings measures included a reduction of state and or federal funding.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And what is the total loss in funding from each state and federal source from savings?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And can any of this funding be shifted to different programs?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yeah, so the the revenue that was taken down is representative of the as if every CTL dollar we spend, we're able to draw down revenue.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So the revenue can't really be transferred because we need the CTL dollar to draw it down.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Give me one second, I'll tell you what the total value of take down is.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Please do, I don't know if I don't want to disturb please do come back to it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I'm bad at multitasking, so I you know never assume.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Um so were you able to hit the target of the savings rate this year for the targeted amount?

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks about the average timeline for contract registration and invoice reimbursement to human services providers, and Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston highlights ACS's strong performance in contract registration.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions ACS on Contracting and Reimbursement Timelines

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It was more than that right the savings that we achieved was was at the target not at the target actually okay um and the total revenue takedown for FY27 is 29 million dollars 29 million okay and uh the second part of the question was just about whether any of the funding could be shifted to different programs and no okay okay um I'll probably have follow up later on that but moving on to contracting and procurement my favorite topic uh contracting is an issue across the board for a lot of city agencies as we know and um what is the average timeline for reimbursements to providers at ACS so the average processing time for um health and human service invoices is 7.7 days which is much lower than the average across the city for health and human services agencies which at a at the close of the year was um 17 7.7 days from this time they submit the invoice but then if there's questions about the invoicing and there's back and forth like is that how how long does that and this is not meaning the contracts are registered we we also lead we also lead agencies in in timely contract resent registration so we're very good there um we don't actually I I don't have a way to measure um if there's back and forth um in in terms of uh questions that might come up if something is submitted incorrectly and it has to be resubmitted or sent back to the provider.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, we don't have a way of measuring that.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: If if there's some way to measure that, that'd be great, only because we've been hearing, and I say this also as someone that used to contract with DYCD, DIFTA, um, DOHMH, and a lot of other city agencies, but I know that the the timeline it takes usually for the reimbursement once you submit the voucher is usually a bit longer.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So um just wanted to know if if you have any information or on that, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Not to brag, but ACS is really good at this.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Oh, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So you guys are like A plus.7 days from when in total.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That's why I'm skeptical a little bit because from my our nonprofit background, it takes a lot longer.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay, that's good.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We want to know why other agencies aren't going to be.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yeah, exactly.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, and how has ACS dealt with and responded to challenges related to um if there are any contracting issues or procurement issues on the process?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, and what are major concerns or areas of concerns for providers in terms of the contracting process?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Have you heard of any and gotten feedback?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: My understanding is that we have close to 100%, if not 100% of all of our contracts registered on time on the human service side.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I don't think we've been hearing those types of things.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay, so then once it gets awarded, Schedule C comes out and the contracts get procured, usually in a couple months, it's usually registered with ACS.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks for an update on conversations with OMB regarding the state hardship waiver for Raise the Age reimbursement, and Commissioner Rebecca Jones Gaston shares estimated agency expenditures since FY19.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Inquires About the Raise the Age Hardship Waiver

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um okay, moving on to raise the age waiver.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Uh at the prelim budget hearing, um, we urged ACS to apply for the state for the hardship waiver because this is money that has been owed to the city, I think, since going back to 2019.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, so since the preliminary budget hearing, have you had any conversations with OMB regarding applying for the waiver or pursuing state reimbursement?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yes, we we have had those conversations.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um we appreciate the council's commitment and interest in New York City getting the funding from the state that it should get from raise the age.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Back in the day, both Nancy and I advocated for raise the age and for the city to get reimbursement.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And we've had those conversations with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The decision about whether or not to seek a hardship waiver would not be up to ACS, it would be up to City Hall and O and B.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And the funding for raise the age would be bigger than just ACS.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So okay, that's kind of leading to my next question, which is are you able to share information regarding how the expenses I know like you just mentioned it's it wouldn't be just with ACS, but um would you be able to share any information regarding where the expenses um would be directed?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So in theory, ACS, um probation, mock J are all agencies and probably a couple others and YPD and law.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay, and do you know if the mayor's office or if ACS would be able to share any of that information in terms of the funding?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Sure, we shared a chart which I can send to you as part of our response on the to the preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And it's an in-depth chart, so I can send that to you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay, and can you say for the record um just how much you're you estimate the agency has spent on the implementation of raise the age from fiscal 2019 to date, which I'm assuming is quite a bit?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um yeah, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yes, we estimate that um we have spent um going back to FY19, $549 million dollars in expense and $133 million dollars in capital.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay, perfect, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, and for at-risk youth funding, um, we had asked at the preliminary budget if there if you had details regarding the 194.5 million in baseline state funding provided, which was part of the total of 300 million added across several agencies that the state had given.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So since the prelim budget hearing, have you received any further guidance or clarification from the state?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We have not, although we're hoping it will be imminent because I believe the governor is probably signing the budget while we're sitting here.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: But it did finally pass legislature last night, so hopefully we'll be able to get more details soon.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Perfect.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks for details on how the $194.5 million in baseline state funding for at-risk youth will be utilized by ACS in FY27 and beyond.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions ACS on At-Risk Youth Funding

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And if you could let us know how which programs and services it'll fund, that would be great if for follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And the 300 million is definitely a significant apportion of funding for the new fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um and have you developed any plans for how it will utilize the funding for FY27 and beyond?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Have have those conversations happened?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Not that's my awareness.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay, so for follow-up, if you could let us know which programs you're thinking of expanding, reworking, adjusting, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: My understanding about the $300 million is it's not meant for expansion, but meant as revenue.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Revenue, okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: If you could, yeah, either way, that would be great if you could follow up.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And before and I think that's it for now, but I'm gonna try pass it over to Chair Stevens.

chapters.summary: Council Member Chi Ossé asks about the administration's plans to fund non-mandated child care vouchers for low-income working families and options for families currently on the waitlist.
chapters.title: Council Member Ossé Questions ACS on Child Care Vouchers

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um before I go, um Councilmember O say asked if you could ask a question, um, because he had to run, so if you ask your question.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Uh good morning, nice to see you.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Um there are members of my district who are concerned about the issue of non-mandated child care vouchers for working parents.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Uh these are vouchers for low-income families who do not qualify for benefits but do not earn enough to afford child care.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Because they were told the city has no more funding for these vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Parents and families in my district are concerned because these vouchers are crucial for them accessing child care.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: I would like to know what are the administration's plans for funding these vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: And if there is a funding gap, what would be the alternative for these families?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Well, first of all, I'll just acknowledge that child care is absolutely a critical priority for the Mamdani administration and for us.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I would hope so.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That would be crazy if you said otherwise, right?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I would actually like to turn it over to uh Deputy Commissioner Volcomore to be able to give some details about what the plans are.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Um so you're right, and as Stephanie said, we understand the budget is being finalized as we speak, so we're eager to see what is in the state budget because it is state funding that supports those low-income vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Um it's true that currently we are not able to issue new vouchers, though we continue to recertify families that have vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: So the vast majority of families using vouchers are able to hang on to them.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: As soon as the budget um is finalized, we will analyze what that means for how we can move forward in terms of when we may be able to move the wait list that we are maintaining.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Families, depending on age, have a few options.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: New York City public schools for children zero to five do have open seats, and of course, that depends on location and what families' preferences look like.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Um, and DYCD also has after-school programming, and we are regularly reaching out to families that are on our wait list to make sure that they are aware of those opportunities and can avail themselves of those.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens expresses alarm over the 362 youth in secure detention and asks about interagency task forces working to reduce these numbers.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions ACS on Secure Detention Census

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm just thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And I I, as I'm like with listening to your testimony and going through some of the things, one of the things that probably to me is is very glaring as of May 13th, 2020 6, that we have 362 young people in secure detention.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: This is like really alarming for me.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so I think for me, my my question is, and and this is why I say it's so important for us to invest in our young people on the front end, because now we have 362 young people in secure detention.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And that's a huge alarming number for me.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I I want to ask, and specifically, is there some type of interagency um task force or working group that's going on now to think about how do we reduce these numbers?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because at this point, I've I've been screaming at the top of my lungs for the last two years about this, and I know everyone is up in arms about child care, but I want us to be just as up in arms about 362 young people being in secure detention.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: This is alarming and honestly unacceptable to me.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I would love to hear is there a plan and as we're going through this budget exercise and thinking about you know where savings are, are we thinking about how to get these young people from not ending up here?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Um, and what would that look like?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Stevens.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, just to note that we absolutely share the value of and the importance of our young people in their development and their well-being, and um absolutely crucial in um focusing on um what we're doing, both from a prevention of foster care um involvement to also um uh uh prevention of involvement in the juvenile juvenile system.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'll turn it over to Deputy Commissioner Ginsburg to give some more specific details.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: As you know, we share your concerns.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: But it's just frustrating because you guys get them at the end, and so that's why I said is there like an interagency conversation on like how we're gonna reduce these numbers?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because you can't do this on your own, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Like, would like how are we working with DOE and DYCD and all these agencies that work with young people to get this number down because honestly, when we came in, we were at I believe it was like 150, and no one is talking about these numbers constantly increasing, and it's really driving me crazy.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: And so if we aren't doing that, I want to just stay on record that that is something that I need and want to see really quick soon.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: So the good news is is that since the change of administration, we have been actively in conversation with Mock J.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: We are speaking with DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: We have always been speaking with DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: As you are aware, DYCD funds many of our programs in the facilities and also funds programs in the communities.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: So when young people leave us, they can continue with some of that programming as supports in the communities in order to prevent them from coming back to the city.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Oh, I know we're seeing them later at one, so they'll be getting the same question.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Um that's why I have you guys all here because I want us to make sure we're looking at this in a holistic perspective, because this is a this is a huge concern of mine.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So um definitely want us to keep talking and thinking about how are we looking and being intentional about getting these numbers down because this to me is unacceptable.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens questions the decision to expand a non-secure detention facility to a nine-bed placement facility after previously reducing close-to-home capacity during the RFP process.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Close to Home Facility Expansion

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Another thing in the testimony that was kind of glaring to me and was like something that I think is also connected to even the savings.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I see now that some of this money is going to be around securing a nine uh nine-bed non-secure placement facility for close to homes.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: This is also super glaring and frustrating to me because when we did the RFP, we were right sizing this program, and I remember providers screaming from the top of their lungs that we are not right sizing and that these numbers are going up, and we shouldn't be closing any of them.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And they also said that it was going to be really hard, like once they close, that they would now have to say to get a facility back up and running.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: It takes a lot, and we know that it's not something that can be done overnight.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So this is frustrating that now we're building another facility.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And this is a case where we should be listening to providers, and I get it.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yes, we have to have savings, but it cannot be in areas that young people are impacted.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so I need to understand how do we get back to a place if we were right sizing now having to build a new facility.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: I want to just say, first of all, you are correct.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Um, the history is that um we reduce close-to-home capacity as part of the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Um, I do want to mention that it's not building a new facility, it's a current six-bed non-secure detention that we're gonna turn into a nine-bed non-secure placement.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: So it's already a site part of our juvenile justice continuum.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Okay, I just wanted to say again that the I know that doesn't get to the heart of your matter.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, it doesn't, but you know, I just have to say the things and it needs to be set on record, and it needs to be set because there's something in a place where providers said, hey, don't do this because we're going to need it.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And I feel like I'm gonna go full circle to now where we're at with the savings and prevent it preventive care.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens questions the justification for finding savings in preventive services, arguing that these services are critical to keeping youth out of secure detention facilities.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Savings Targets in Preventive Services

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And this is also frustrating because for the last two years, because I've only had ACS for two years.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: The last two years, it was said that the reason that these numbers are down in these facilities is because of the work of preventive care and that that was necessary.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I don't want to get y'all in trouble because I've been doing whatever, and I don't know how um council member Lee does her thing.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, no, I'll be clapping and so I'm sorry, my people don't know how to behave, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Um but it gets me very frustrated because preventative services has been totted as a reason for not having so many people um in the facility.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I think in your testimony you said that we're down um like 13 um 13,000 from a decade ago, and like these numbers are consistently going down, and it has been a continuous effort to get these numbers down and present preventive services has been like the thing that has been pointed to of saying we're gonna get these things down.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so, how do we now sit here and say and justify that it's services that this is a place that services should have savings?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because I know we don't like to use cuts, I know that's not what we're saying, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: But how do we justify that now when that has been the thing that we've pointed to?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so what is the plan to ensure that that the continuity stays?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because one of the things that I think for me is frustrating is that I've heard things like, well, there's still slots, and so if I'm a young person, well, if I'm a family and there's slots in uh the Bronx, and I live in Queens, and I already don't like the system, because let's be clear, and I already have issues, I'm not going.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I'm trying to understand what the justification is, what's the plan, and how this is gonna look in actual actual time for these families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I'll I'll stop chatting.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I'll start out with um, and this might be my own artificial uh separation.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um the close to home uh related to the work of trying to um support young people that are involved with juvenile justice um and um those placements and then the prevention services um connected to preventing foster care placement um are kind of aligned but different sort of yeah, they're all different, right?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: But this the point I was just trying to make was like we need to listen to providers because they said this, and now again we need to listen to the process.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Absolutely, and the per the partnership of providers is critical.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, and you heard that in my testimony.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um and prevention services are critical as well.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The um entire continuum.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We also um as an agency have um to have responsibility for you know fiscal responsibility and aligning um resources in um in ways so that we are investing um and utilizing the the resources we do have, and the decisions to um eliminate slots is a really difficult decision, and I'm happy to turn it over to Deputy Commissioner Linares to talk a little bit more about the prevention programs that I think you're referring to.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Chair Stevens.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks ACS to detail the specific metrics, such as utilization and performance indicators, used to select the four preventive service programs slated for closure.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Inquires About Metrics for Preventive Program Closures

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I share your sentiment.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It was a very difficult decision for us, but we really had to maintain our fiscal responsibility and ensure that we are maintaining a strong prevention program as possible.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have looked at different indicators to make the recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: One of the first things that we considered when thinking about this savings was our families going to continue to have services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So you're right, we don't want a family from Brooklyn to be thinking about a service in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And a couple of the programs actually were in Brooklyn that's being cut correct.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So can you do that?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Did you talk about the four programs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can you tell what's the total number of contracts that are under this program that are being eliminated and like what where they're located?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, so before I answer that, I wanted to um mentioned that when we look at things like families in the Bronx versus Brooklyn, we were very intentional in thinking about where our capacity was.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we try to have a birth eye view of our entire system before we think about where to make a recommendation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Once we did that, then we landed in the catchments that we did, which are three programs in Brooklyn and one in Queens.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Once we determined, we looked at things like are there other programs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: There was one in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm sorry, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Three in Brooklyn and one in Queens.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Once we looked at the catchment and determined that these were the areas where we have the more capacity, where we had less SER costs compared to other areas, where we have other prevention providers available in those areas.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Once we did that, then we narrow down and narrow down to those areas.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Then we said, okay, so now let's look at the providers that are in the areas and let's think about their own performance against the key performance indicators that we had identified.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And those are utilization, the number of families that they served, and we didn't look at a point in time, so we didn't look just at today how we were doing, but we were intentional in looking at fiscal year 24, fiscal year 25, and we also looked at the remaining uh uh what's passed in fiscal year 26.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we wanted to have a very comprehensive understanding of whether the how those programs were faring against those KPIs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The other thing that we had to consider was whether there was uh those programs were uh serving uh special population.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Once we did that, then we brought it down to those four providers that were put forward as recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How many clients are served by those programs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How many?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so in family support programs we have we serve uh citywide a total of five four thousand five hundred and twelve.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um as of April, we had a total of three thousand three hundred seats that were receiving uh participated in services of family support.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: For these four programs, we have a total of 360 slots currently active in those uh active families that are actually receiving services is 199.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about the transition plan for families currently enrolled in the closing preventive programs and the geographical distance to alternative services.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Transition Plan for Families Affected by Closures

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In those programs, in those um has there been an analysis on like where's the closest one, like if those families are not being served there, where's the closest facility or place they they would have to go?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, so that was part of uh part of the uh metrics that we looked.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh we're making a decision uh or a recommendation because we wanted to make sure that families can have services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That again, that was our first beginning point.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Will families continue to have services?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
