utterances.text: Not only the family that are currently in the world.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Well, I know they'll be able to still have services, but I'm trying to understand like what is like the distance, like how like especially like prime example.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You said there's three in Brooklyn, where those are in are those in the same area, and then were there other programs like nearby.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, because again, like the we know that these are vulnerable families.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We know that these are a lot of black and brown families.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I literally have a resolution about the harm that AC has done to black and brown families because we know of the past harm.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so this to me is a disinvestment in those communities because we know we don't want to, you know, criminalize poverty.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And a lot of these cases are often around poverty.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so, like, if I'm already struggling and now I have to like travel and and have a burden, like I'm trying to get an assessment of like what's the closest facility that they are gonna have to go to.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Have you guys already thought about like what that transition of plan is?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Like a lot of these families are vulnerable.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I don't I barely wanted to talk to this social worker.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Now you think I'm gonna talk to a new social worker.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm not doing it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I'm trying to get a better understanding of what that's going to look like and understanding like the bigger impact of like how like, and again, I want to be clear.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I know that no one wants to ever have programs cut, and I know that you guys don't want to have things cut, but I just need us to make sure as a city we're prioritizing different things.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And for me, prioritization is young people, and this is a place and families of like there's going whether we like to say it or not, there's going to be harm.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And for me, it's like, how are we mitigating this and how are we moving thoughtful?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I'm not saying that you're not, but what I'm saying is this is my concern, this is why I'm focusing on this because there is no way that families who are already vulnerable, families who already don't trust the system are now going to say, all right, well, it's slots over here, so I'm gonna go.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I just am trying to have more of a concrete of like what does this transition look like?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What is the plan, and and how how are we going to ensure that we are mitigating the harm of all these families?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I like to tell you that um unfortunately for me, I have lots of experience on ending contracts because of the end of contracts in 2020 when we transitioned the system to the no RFB.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: During that time, we had about 5,000 families or so that we were transitioning and that we did so successfully.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I like to say that we have very carefully thought about what would it mean for each individual family.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You should know that as part of the end of contract, immediately as the uh providers are notified, we have a team specifically dedicated to reviewing each and every case family that is involved actively in prevention.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The decision about the next steps are made together with the provider ACS and the family that is that if the family decides that this is a time when a case can close and we can determine that that's an appropriate decision then we go ahead and make a case closure.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it is not it it's it's a process that is very well thought out and one that I'm personally very proud of despite the fact that it means that we are ending a contract.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens expresses concern over the 90-day timeline for contract termination, highlighting the impact on human services workers who will lose their jobs.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Raises Concerns Over 90-Day Contract Termination Timeline

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What were these contracts in so um I'm gonna ask Margaret to help me with that question um because I um saving for projected for FY27 um we know that we need at least 90 days to uh continue the case management to transfer cases and so we're saying 90 days we know that we need 90 days from the from the beginning of the implementation in order to transfer all cases I should say that we transfer 5,000 families in a six month period during COVID and we did so successfully so while 90 days seems like a a very short time I do believe that we have a dedicated team that will make sure um that every family uh and providers get the support that they need to make this transition.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I mean, I just, yes, the families is also a a factor in this too, but 90 days for folks who are now out of a job is also a huge factor.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So we can't agree pretend like that's not part of the conversation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Like I don't, I agree.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It kind of felt cold.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it's not.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm sorry, guys.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: This ain't my hearing.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Don't worry, when we go back crossing by crazy, but sorry, guys.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: But that's kind of that's kind of upsetting.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And you know, this budget isn't complete.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So my hope is that we come up with a better solution than 90 days of thinking about the transition of families and figuring out a better way to have savings because again, everybody's talking about an affordable city on both sides.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And that don't sound like we are thinking about workers, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I've I feel like we've passed multiple bills around workers in this council.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So my hope is that my colleagues get along board for us to fight to make sure that these workers are also protected because the human service sector keeps getting hit.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So, you know, I hope other you know, folks get on board with this because that is kind of heartbreaking to think that people will be losing their jobs in 90 days.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks for the rationale behind the $4.9 million savings reduction in Fair Futures and how it aligns with the agency's stated goal to expand the program.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Fair Futures Funding Reductions

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Um I have I'm gonna transition to some other questions um around um fair futures.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm gonna do some questions and I'll go to colleagues and then we'll come back.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Um, the executive plan included baseline savings of 4.9 million from the city and state funding, stating that fiscal 2026 related to fair futures programs after speaking with um OMB understood that the savings should only been reflected in fiscal 2026 through 2028 that expected to restore the funding in fiscal 2029 and 2030 in the future fiscal plan.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: ACS has publicly stated that they desire to expand fair Futures programs.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Giving this, what is the rationale for the funding's reduction within fair Futures?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And this is, and I bring this up again.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: This is another program that ACS has been raising the flag about and saying how great it is and all the things.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I get that we are in savings, but it is mind-boggling that the programs that we've talked about for the last two years since I've been here and pointed to the success of getting these numbers down and foster care and transitioning, that this is where we're finding savings.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I just gotta say it.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I'll just start and then turn over to Deputy Commissioner Mendez.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: You know, I think these programs are exactly what you're referring to when we're talking about focusing on youth and well-being and our responsibility to helping them develop and become successful adults and understand how to navigate the world as best as possible.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And our commitment to that continues, and you'll hear from the deputy commissioner that it actually is not a loss in services, and the investment is intended to be used in full.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Thank you for your questions.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So regarding fair futures, um, you may recall in fiscal year, I believe it was 23, we baseline 30 million dollars, the city baseline 30 million dollars in the program.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We were allowed to expand um up to 26, serving children in care and out of care.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And then with the Youth Safety and Success Initiative, we received additional funding because we wanted to make sure there were additional resources because as we support the kids in care, even though that number is going down, as children are leaving, we wanted to continue.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So there was a plan, a robust plan to take these additional dollars and have the agencies quickly ramp up to serve.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I think the original plan was 5300 children starting either in fiscal year 27 or fiscal year 28.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So what we're doing is we're still expanding, and by fiscal year 29, 28, one of those fiscal years we'll get 29, we'll get to 5400, but we are doing this incrementally, and we're allowing the providers to have a little bit more time to ramp up, and that will help them build the infrastructure, do the onboarding, and hire the additional staff.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So, in fact, in fiscal year 27, they are receiving more money per slot for fair futures, and we are looking for them to grow.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So the target, I don't have my glasses on, but there was a target for this year, you know how I am.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And then there's going to be a target for fiscal year 27.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we they are going to be growing, we're reaching more young people.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: The same thing for fiscal year 28, and then by fiscal year 29, they should have their infrastructure and all of their additional staff in place to reach that 5400 number at a minimum.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: What I also will say is if providers come to us and say, Look, I can do more than what you're you know asking us to do.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: For example, we have the capacity to look at resource and shift so that no one will, if there's additional children that need fair futures, we're committed to figuring out how they can get the supports because we are committed to the program.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we are growing, we're just kind of creating a more of a runway to that to that growth.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks how ACS communicates budget flexibility to providers and tracks Fair Futures enrollment targets across different fiscal years.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Inquires About Fair Futures Targets and Communication

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And and even to your point there around, like providers can come if they need additional growth because I know this was a conversation we were having with the last commissioner where providers weren't coming and saying that they needed additional um funding or supports or whatever.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: How are we communicating that to providers?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Because that is one of the things that providers are saying that they didn't know even was possible.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So within the division of family permanency, we have we have a contract management unit and they work with the providers around their budgets.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So the providers are very vocal when they're having questions or they want to figure out the budget.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So that is that conference, that conversation, and we work on their budget, we look at their budget narrative.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So I will make sure the team communicates to each agency because we go over each of their budgets that if you feel you need more for fair futures or for anything, let's talk so that we can make sure that they can soundly do the work that they need to do.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And sorry, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: That is one of the commitments I've made.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: That's good to hear.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: What are some of the targets around, because I know you spoke about the like the different target areas around the fair futures?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Um, what are those different target areas?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And also thinking about, like, I know as the commissioner's coming in and you're saying you're gonna want to talk with them.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Are you do you guys like have benchmarks of like triggers like, okay, we see that you're increasing, and so like how do we then you know have that turnaround and what and just go over the target?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So for fiscal year 27, the the target would be about 4800.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: For fiscal year 28, we want to be at 5,000, a little over 5,000, and again in fiscal year 29 at that 5400.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: What we we are very we're in regular conversation with the Center for Fair Futures because they do a lot of the training and the help the agencies implement the model.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: They have a database, our data team is able to look at data.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And what we really do want to focus on now as we're moving into this second piece of the expansion is setting up some metrics and holding agencies accountable.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We were able to pull some data, and so we're able to say that right now of the there's about 4300 young people involved with Fair Futures this fiscal year so far.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: It looks like I don't want to jinx anything that we might be on track to surpass what we did last year, but 87% of the those kids are in care, 13% are um no longer in care, and we're reaching them, and then we know about two-thirds of young people that are eligible are getting fair futures.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So how do we make that number?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: How do we get to 75%?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: How do we get to 80%?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And I think that's working with the Center for Fair Futures and also the agencies and having specific metrics that we can touch base on.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have a team in the Office of Youth Safety and Success.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we were gonna have a conversation and kind of co-design that with the providers so that we can make sure everyone is reaching these targets that we've outlined.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about the frequency of contract management meetings and how ACS is working to ensure youth are aware of available Fair Futures supports.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions ACS on Youth Outreach and Contract Management

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, um, and and I and I would love for us to like continue to think about what that looks like, even around like the targets, because again, and and thank you guys for coming to Shadow Day for our foster care youth here.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you for having us.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, they were really excited to have you guys in the information, but even some of that, and I'm not sure they didn't ask you guys a lot of questions, but some of the conversations I had with them in passing, there's still a big inconsistency of what information is being shared according to agencies, and so definitely want to make sure that we're working on that, especially with a program like this, and how do we reach those targets?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because if young people don't know, they don't know.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Um is lower is a lower number of young people than originally planned for being served by the pro and no.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: What's the second steps is ACS taking to ensure providers are fully utilizing the funding in this program so that all eligible young people are receiving those vital supports?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So again, we have our contract management unit, and in addition to they're not just how often do they like meet with the providers, the contract management unit?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I wouldn't need to get back to you with an exact um often, but like you know, invoices come in monthly or you know, within a certain like a few times a quarter, and there's a lot of conversations with um those, you know, the finance teams at the agencies.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: At now, agencies have their allocations, at least for enhanced family foster care and specialized family foster care.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We're gonna be looking for them to submit the budget, so there'll be a lot of conversations now.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Um so I'll get an exact number I can get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I think I forgot your question though.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: What did you ask me?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I know your original questions.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We're just asking about like the utilization of the program.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So they submit, you know, and so we're able to look at the tracking and like basically on what they're submitting.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So let's say you know they're budgeted for X, and then so far they've submitted um a number, you know, much lower.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We're gonna have conversations about what's happening.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Is there like a trigger where if it's like okay, you don't see a number, and you guys can reach out to say, like, what supports do you need?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So that's the role of the contract management team is to look at like, hey, you haven't submitted invoices, are there vacancies?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Are there staff turnovers?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: There are difficulties.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Um, I think we want to refine and hone in more around the qualitative piece, not just look at like the monetary keys, but like the qualitative piece and how are you leveraging um that information?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And then I think our partnership with the Center for Fair Futures will help intensify because they're working with the coaches regularly, so it's really just about expanding and making sure new staff come on and they say, Oh, you're this age, you should go to Fair Futures.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Let me make a referral, that type of thing.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um Chair Stevens, I just to add, um, you know, one of one of the things that I have throughout my career enjoyed is being able to spend time with young people and hear directly from them.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And so um as as I'm learning all of the things parts and pieces that we have available in ACS, um, certainly wanting to ensure that we've got pathways for young people to engage and give us feedback directly about what's what they need and and how communication um and information is best communicated to them from um from the various agencies that are serving them, and so I'm looking forward to um engaging with the young people around that issue as well.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And thank you for having a shadow day.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I think it's critical.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Well, if you know me and everybody knows this, I only like kids, and so and I get all the information from them, which is why when I come back, I'm so passionate because I am translating the I do, I don't really like y'all adult, but the young people are what I'm really focused on, and so a lot of the things that I'm coming back with is like direct conversations that I have with them, um, because you know I think it's so important that they have access to government and have conversations with them.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I look forward to going out and hanging out with kids and changing the system to actually work for them.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I still have a ton of questions, but I'm gonna pass it to Lee um Chair Lee so that our colleagues could ask some questions and come back to me on a second round.

chapters.summary: Council Member Phil Wong asks for an update on the relocation of ACS headquarters from 150 William Street to 110 William Street and the benefits of the new space.
chapters.title: Council Member Wong Questions ACS on Headquarters Relocation

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Uh one recognize we've been joined by um Councilmember Wrestler, Majority Leader Brayu, Councilmember Gutierrez, and we are going to start off with Council Member Wong, followed by Councilmember Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Um I would like to start about the relocation of your headquarters.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Um when did the process begin and what is do you have an update on that?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As the last sole employee at 150, um, I'll try to answer that question.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um we are by the end of May, all staff um, or by the end of the day today, all staff will have moved out of 150 William Street.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I got special exemption to stay through the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um and um by the end of June we'll be fully out of 150 William Street.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Our new headquarters is at 110 William Street.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It's not fully complete.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you explain about the move?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Like what's is there something?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is there issues with the current headquarters?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Um talk, please talk about it.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So we had been in the current headquarters for about 30 years.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um the new headquarters is a much more family, it has been um intentionally developed to be more family friendly and to better meet the needs of the work we do.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So there are a lot of family meeting rooms, there are um like a beautiful new um playroom for kids, the new office of advocacy is much more family friendly.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It was just developed in a different time.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, and so I don't know all the details.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I wasn't personally involved in the details to relocate, but um have been part of some of the like not it wasn't ULERP, but the uh city planning process.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I know a bit about um sort of the idea was that this new location would better meet meet our needs for staff and for families.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Phil Wong asks for the current census of Horizon and Crossroads detention centers and questions the high school absenteeism rates reported by the state comptroller.
chapters.title: Council Member Wong Questions ACS on Detention Center Census and Absenteeism

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Um I would like to ask about the um the two detention centers.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Um I've been reading the mayor's executive budget, I've been hearing and reading the the statement.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I just don't see any uh numbers uh regarding the number of uh kids in the two sites at uh Brownsville site, 17 Bristol Street, and and the one in Matthaven, 560 Brook Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you talk about like sure Nancy can give you we have numbers in the testimony, but Nancy can give you what we have today.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Um as of this morning, we had 181 um young men in horizon.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Um in Crossroads we had 186 um young people, eleven are young women in that facility.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Um last year there's a report from uh the state compont at Napoli regarding both sites, and I'm gonna read that to you.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Uh the uh the report was data in April 2025, and uh it mentions that the the absence rate, school absence absenteeism rates for uh ranges anywhere from 13 to 73 percent days absent.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Um can you talk about what's been done to improve that?

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Yes, I can talk about that.

chapters.summary: Council Member Phil Wong asks about the average length of stay for youth in secure detention and the number of high school graduations achieved within the facilities.
chapters.title: Council Member Wong Inquires About Length of Stay and Graduation Rates

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: That report covered the period of COVID.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: It was a period of five years, mostly that predated the Adams administration.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: That's what it was looking at.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: The current attendance rate, according to our DOE partners in both sites, is about 72 to 74%, which is close to the attendance rate in the community.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: Can you talk about the number of days the child spends in a detention center and average before they get to leave me one minute?

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: The average length of stay in secure detention for 2025 was about 97.4 days.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: So graduation rates are difficult.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: It's not a school because young people don't start on the first day of school.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: They come whenever the court mandates them into our facility.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: We have graduated about 150 young people over the last three and a half years.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: And two years ago we signed an MOU with CUNY, and we have young people who have either graduated with a diploma or have achieved their GED engaged in college programming in both facilities through Hostos and BMCC.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lynn Schulman raises concerns about the proposed cuts to preventive services, highlighting the impact on Forestale in Queens and the loss of matching state funds.
chapters.title: Council Member Schulman Questions Cuts to Preventive Services

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I've been reminded we got to try to stick to time as best as possible, but we're gonna do round two questions as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Um, Councilmember Shulman, followed by Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Good morning, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Um, and welcome to New York.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I have to say your background is quite impressive, and we're lucky to have you as the ACS Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Um since 1999, the number of children removed from families by ACS has dropped more than 80 percent thanks to preventive services programs such as the one in my district at Forestale, a hundred and thirty-five-year-old organization that is the largest and most effective social services agency in the borough of Queens.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: However, the families help there are now at risk because of these proposed cuts.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to ask you uh, well, first of all, I know that a lot of your background is in preventive services, and you've made some great achievements in many states and in the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So with that, I want to know, you know, this the the for two things.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: One is the cuts that are being made to preventive services also have a match that you're losing, a state match that you're losing as part of that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's number one.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Number two is that it represents a very small piece of the ACS budget, so I'm wondering if there are other areas that can be cut instead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And and the and the next question after that is Forrestdale's getting the biggest cut, or it looks like it is.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: They're gonna have to lay off some um work from some employees.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And I have visited personally with the families there because I give them a lot of money uh for my budget, uh, and they have thanked me for keeping, you know, it's important to keep the families together and keep the children safe, which is your mantra.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So, is there a way that we can do these cuts differently?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you for the welcome.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, it is uh a pleasure to be here and an honor to honor to serve.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um I'll just say, yes, prevention services are absolutely and continue and will be a priority.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And you've heard in testimony today around some of the process and consideration that went into the decision making.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I haven't had a chance to visit any of the providers yet on day six.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: But look forward to getting to get out and meet with the providers, their teams, the community members, and the families that are being served.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: For the specific question around match, I'm gonna turn to Deputy Commissioner Plenty Plentnikov.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And then if there's more questions about process for decision making, happy to um pull in uh Deputy Commissioner Linares as well.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lynn Schulman asks if alternative areas of the ACS budget can be cut instead of preventive services, emphasizing that prevention saves the city money in the long run.
chapters.title: Council Member Schulman Urges ACS to Reconsider Preventive Service Cuts

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Sure, thank you, Chairmember.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Um so the cuts are actually pretty even across all um four contracts.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: So it's approximately the same at all providers.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Um the reimbursement is 62% that we received from the child or services state funding.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yeah, and I'm asking because that the total amount of the cut for the preventive services is a small piece of ACS's budget.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So were there any other areas that be especially since it is so important to have preventive services and we've seen the outcomes from preventive services?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's number one.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And number two is that if you cut and then you have to do remediation on the other end, that's even more expensive.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So that's what I'm asking here if there's a way to do it differently.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I know the count the the admin has made a decision.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I get that.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, you know, the mayor ran on working with vulnerable families and also those that um you know and and the family unit, and so I think it's just important to really push and ask that question.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um yes, and and I understand your concern.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um we did look across the entire agency, look to maximize revenues, look to find areas of underspending, and really kept our focus on the least impact to children, youth, and families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um we want to we want to note that there are uh over a thousand vacant slots in this program.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I understand.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I understand there are reasons for vacant slots depending when the contract gets paid, depending on a whole bunch of stuff.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, Commissioner, you were a social worker, you know what in what's involved sometimes with families and everything else.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I get that, but I'm I'm imploring that this be looked at again.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I don't want to take up too much more time because I know that my time is up.

chapters.summary: Council Member Rita Joseph asks how the city plans to expand the 'Every Child and Family is Known' initiative while cutting the New Visions portal that the program relies on.
chapters.title: Council Member Joseph Questions Every Child and Family is Known Initiative

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And as a fellow social worker who ran a nonprofit, I totally just want to reiterate what Chair Stevens was saying about um how prevention is so key, it actually saves the city money in the long run.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I think it's smart to actually invest in more save in more preventative services, and then also just wanted to reiterate that it really does impact um just the planning.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I say this with like almost past trauma in my voice, but you know, there it affects the planning of organization, staffing, budgets, all of that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So you know, we'll definitely be in continued conversation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know there's gonna be more questions about it, but just wanted to reiterate that point.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Um, and we have council member Joseph, followed by Council Member Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you, both chairs, and I'd love to associate my your with your comments as well.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Welcome, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How are you?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The Every Child and Family is Known initiative, a collaborative between ACS and DOE, which received additional funding in the same budget to expand its work with students living in shelters and other families facing crisis relies on the new visions portal to track and respond to families' needs.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: This school year, over 50,000 check-ins have been logged by carrying adults in the portable.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Please walk me through how does the city with how does the city plan on expanding every child and family is known initiative why simultaneously cutting the platform it this it depends on to do the work.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Can you share if these cuts are sustained?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How will it impact your work at ACS to support our youth who needs this initiative the most?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you for the question and thank you for the welcome.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'm gonna turn it over to Deputy Commissioner Mendez to be able to answer some of the specifics that you ask.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: But just again, reiterating you know, our our role when we're working with children and families is to ensure that they have their needs met and that we're um finding ways to resource um their needs based on what they need and not just what we have, and um look forward to continuing to have conversations with you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Our numbers are tremendous in New York City Public School students in temporary housing.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It's over 155 students, 155,000 students.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So and you know I know that work.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Good morning, Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So I'm happy to report ACS is working very closely with New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Um, the foster care office, as you know, we have a close collaboration with them and new visions to explore how to introduce the the access to that portal to foster care agencies.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So as you can imagine, there's a lot of things we have to do on the back end.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have to formalize an MOU.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Then we have to look at, I'm not the computer person, but I think it's called the cloud service agreement.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we're going through some of those like bureaucratic things to put in place, but we're also starting to talk to the foster care providers.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we're thinking about doing this as a pilot first.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And we're talking to the foster care providers about like what should this look like?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: What's what makes sense in all of those things while we're doing the MOU pieces.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And so we're hoping that that will continue to move forward so that in the next school year, this can move if is you know it will move forward.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So the timeline is for September this coming.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So you'll spend the summer training and transitioning this platform.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Is that what that's looking like?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Timeline, I think you know, part of the um, and I I can get back to you with actual specific milestones.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Like I mentioned, we have to create this MOU.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So sometimes there's um back and forth.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I think um Councilmember Lee talked about back and forth.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So there'll be some back and forth until you get it right.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Right, until we get it right.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: The cloud service agreement.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Um, I know New York City Public Schools is uh currently using New Vision, so that should not be a problem for ACS.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: They should pass all those cybersecurity tests, but I don't want to speak, I don't want to get ahead of myself.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I do know we're trying to launch in the next um school year.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So I'll continue to work.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We get around to, right, Chair Lee?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Welcome.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I have more questions.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And and I'll just commit that we can follow up um as it's evolving with you directly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Council Member Harvey Epstein asks about ACS's commitment to conducting outreach in communities to ensure vacant prevention slots are filled before cutting program capacity.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein Questions Outreach for Vacant Prevention Slots

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Councilmember Epstein, followed by Councilmember Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Is he here?

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: He's somewhere.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We can go get him if you want.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: But uh Commissioner, welcome.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Um, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Uh uh just uh disclose I was uh foster care worker back in the day, so I appreciate your commitment to this issue.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I do want to focus on the prevention.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Uh you know, as someone who worked in foster care, you know, we're hearing a lot of about the spaces uh you know v vacant slots in prevention, but I want to talk about maybe the the need to do additional outreach to prevent that that to happen.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And maybe there's slots that are vacant is maybe related to the agency not doing enough a good enough job doing the outreach in communities to get people into those services.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm wondering, obviously you're new into this, kind of what commitment you're gonna make to ensure that there's outreach into our community to make sure people get know about those prevention services so they can get into those slots.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you for the question and the welcome.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yeah, as I am getting my feet underneath me and um uh uh learning all the acronyms.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um I one of the things I'm committed to is looking at all of the programs and processes that we have and really um find ways that we can continue to improve and outreach and how people know about what's available and our relationships with providers and community are really really critical to that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, I I would encourage you to do that and to to work with us uh to ensure that happens because I do worry that when you reduce prevention slots, then we will, you know, obviously we've heard a lot about this, the the negative impact that it'll have on families and you know the cost that'll you know obviously the human cost to the city and well the human cost to those families who aren't getting the support that they need to to prevent bad things to happen to their families.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Absolutely, and I look forward to working with uh sister agencies also um as collectively thinking about how are we serving kids and families as a whole.

chapters.summary: Council Member Harvey Epstein asks about the plan to support youth staying at the Children's Center and efforts to reduce their length of stay through kinship placements.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein Inquires About Children's Center Services

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yeah, I want to flag the children's center for you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um uh because this has been something, you know, before I came to this lovely body, I was an assembly member for eight years and dealt with lots of issues there and insufficient programming and services available to the young people who are some people are cycling in in a day, but some people are there for months on end.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I'm wondering what the plan is to ensure that the young people who are there getting getting the support that they need while they're at the children's center.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'm learning, and um one of the things I know is I'm uh agree with you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We absolutely, if children are in our care, we have a responsibility to make sure that we're meeting their needs and um working with uh deputy first deputy commissioner, titles right, first deputy commissioner Saunders, um, who isn't here today, but um with um understanding kind of what's happening at the children's center where our needs are and where there's opportunity for um improvement.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Ultimately, um I would love that um there's less and less need and utilization of the children's center, um, and that we have the ability to both keep children at home with their families and meet their needs, utilize kinship placements, identify um uh family home placements outside of having um to use the children's center, but the quality of care is critical as well.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Um I'm gonna turn it over to Deputy Commissioner Mendez if she has anything else to add.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So just to piggyback on your question regarding services, I won't go into detail, we can definitely share, but there's a lot of robust service, mental health and well being services.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: JCCA provides mental health support, children's villages working with us on our youth that may be trafficked.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have um different organizations providing mentorship programs.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have an eight-staff ACS staff mental health team that's based in the Children's Center.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So there's a lot of programming that Commissioner Jones gaston mentioned that first deputy commissioner Wynnette Saunders introduced.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I'm happy to report today there are 39 young people at the Children's Center, and we have brought down considerably the length of time children stay there.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: There are some, yes, that stay older, our teens.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: But we're working, you know, with our foster care providers and also working with their families to see if they can safely be returned home.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we've made a lot of strides.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Um I could go on, but you know, yeah, I'm happy to talk offline about that more because it's something that we've seen as an ongoing concern and problem.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yes, and we work very closely with the 13% in the community district in that area on a regular basis.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: So I think those partnerships and the commitment, while we don't want children staying at their children a long time while they are there, we want them to be supported and have their needs met.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I just wanted to add that um first deputy commissioner Wynnette Saunders really wanted to be here today, and unfortunately had a medical appointment she couldn't change, and so I just wanted to put that out for the record because I know she really would have wanted to share updated information about the children's center with you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As you know, we've both been long-standing members of the Children's Center and Community Advisory Board, and um I'd be happy to set up a time for the three of us to meet so we can update you more fully at four of us.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler requests a briefing on the relocation of the Brooklyn Child Advocacy Center (CAC) and questions the 25,600-child waitlist for child care vouchers.
chapters.title: Council Member Restler Questions Brooklyn CAC Relocation and Voucher Waitlist

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Uh, we've been joined also by Councilmember Brewer, and we'll go on to Councilmember Ressler, followed by Gutierrez.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Um Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Congratulations, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's good to see the ACS team this morning.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Um, I will just note uh it building on Councilmember Epstein for a second, the CAC in Brooklyn is moving.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We've received no communication about that, so would appreciate a briefing on what's planned and where that's going.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's in our district, it's an important location, and we really want to make sure that that is handled thoughtfully.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And with that, I'm gonna switch the topic that your team knows I'm gonna ask about, which is vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um building on Councilmember Ose's questions, uh, just to affirm there are 25,000 children currently on the voucher waiting list.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: So first of all, absolutely um commitment to follow-up related to the CAC um question that you have, and then I'll turn it over to this.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Yes, based on the report from last Friday, 25,600.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 25,600 children desperately in need of child care who do not have it today and with no plans right now to provide any funding to the families on the wait list.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I want to be clear, I obviously share your concern and want to get care to as many families as possible.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: However, there are families on the wait list that are actively engaged in care, especially with New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We keep those children on the wait list because if and when we are able to offer them a voucher, we want them to have the choice of type of care.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So you mentioned earlier that your team and others across the administration are doing outreach to folks on the wait list to connect them to 2K seats and after school programs.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Have you quantified how many families have been successfully connected from the wait list to other child care options?

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler asks if the historic state budget investment provides funding for additional child care vouchers in FY27 to help move families off the waitlist.
chapters.title: Council Member Restler Questions Funding for Additional Vouchers

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: No, we don't have a way to quantify that, but I can say that there are about 6,000 children that are enrolled in CCBG funded New York City public school seats.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There may be children in other New York City public school seats, meaning other meaning funded by other funding streams, and we don't have currently the ability to do a data match with DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So without without a data match, we know that there's tens of thousands of children who are in desperate need of support, and while we know this is a major commitment for Catherine Governor Hochel and Mayor Mamdani, um we're in the lurch, we're left, these families are left in the lurch.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So just to understand, it certainly seems that there is with the historic investment in the state budget, there are resources available for this fiscal year to pay for additional vouchers without concern about future years.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can you confirm that's accurate?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: As you know, the budget is either being signed or signed potentially while we're checking out.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: We all know what those numbers are, and we've known them for five months.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: The mayor and the governor did an announcement on like this fifth day in January, and the numbers have not moved.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: So just to affirm a simple question, for this fiscal year coming up, FY27, do we have funding for additional vouchers?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Not for future years.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I understand there's a major liability there.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Well, well, I do want to say that I haven't seen the I haven't had a chance to look at the final bills.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: So I'll take your word for it, but the language is complex, and we do need to look at that, and we would be remiss if we didn't to ensure that it is exactly the same as the executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: However, we are always looking at where we are on spending and enrollments across the three agencies that use this funding.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: HRA, ACS, as you know, and New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And so now that the state budget is final, we will need to look at and analyze how and when we will be able to move the wait list.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I can't confirm today what that time window looks like.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler suggests utilizing the SCCF voucher model to fund child care for a single year as a way to reduce the waitlist without committing to long-term liabilities.
chapters.title: Council Member Restler Proposes SCCF Voucher Model

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: So I where I'm going with this, and if chair chairs, who I adore, who I didn't say anything nice about you at the beginning of my comments, I apologize.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Two two quick ones.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Um I do adore them.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: That was very sincere.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: The um I'd say that to all the tiers, but I really mean it today.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: The um uh so just to ask I I I understand that we have a an enormous responsibility.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: We significantly increased enrollment for these non non-mandated low-income childcare vouchers during COVID with an expectation of federal funds lasting forever, and we're not there, and we've been successful for two years in a row with tremendous advocacy in Albany to maintain that every family who has a voucher is able to keep it.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: That's an enormous achievement.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: You guys deserve credit, everybody involved deserves credit, God bless.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: But we do have a 25,000-person waiting list.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: The suggestion I want to make to you on the record here is that we do SCCF vouchers again.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: This was the model we crafted during the de Blasio administration, it can be funded for a single year.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: We don't make a in perpetuity commitment that this child is gonna have a voucher until they're 13 and age out, but that they have that we add child care capacity for this coming fiscal year with the resources we know we have today.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: And I think that is the approach we should take in this moment to help reduce the waiting list and take an approach and help more families connect to care now.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: And if we can get more money from Albany in the future, then let's enroll people in the vouchers in the future.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: But let's do more today.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler questions ACS on OCFS regulations that restrict child care vouchers to the precise hours parents are working, warning of massive disruptions to providers.
chapters.title: Council Member Restler Raises Concerns Over OCFS Work Hour Regulations

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Last question.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Um, if you want to respond to that, you're welcome.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Anything you want to add?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: We know your recommendation.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I I'm not shy.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Um, last thing, I am uh deeply disturbed by one policy that didn't make it into the state budget, which has to do with OCFS's policy around uh uh hours that parents work um and access to childcare vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Basically, OCFS has regulations that require parents to that that only allow for a child to have a voucher during the precise hours that a parent is working.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So if a parent works nights, then they don't get a daytime child care voucher.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If a parent works Mondays and Tuesdays, then the kid can only go into the child care center Mondays and Tuesdays.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If this policy is going to be imposed on the city, it would be profoundly disruptive.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It would lead to child center closures.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Because how can a hundred how can a child care center that has a hundred kids have a different policy of what hours every hundred kid is coming in?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Um you can't hire teachers that way.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We will see enormous, enormous disruptions to our child care system.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I am hopeful that that that the governor is able to guide her agency to do the right thing and that OCFS no longer um OCFS sees that this would be a disastrous policy for New York City child care.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Um do you have any updates on your conversations on this?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Uh is OCFS giving any indication to you that they're recognize how harmful and disruptive a policy this would be.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: First I'll say you know, but for the good of all, you know that ACS has been advocating.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Yes, no, no, no, I'm not criticizing you.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I'm not sure.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: No, I just I just want what you guys have been great understanding.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I'll say that on the right position of the agency and the position of this administration, which is that we agree that this would be bad for providers, bad for families, bad for child development.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Um we have been um advocating at the OCFS level and having conversations there.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: The administration has been speaking with chamber about this.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I don't have any updates, but we are holding on to the same hope that you are that there is a pathway so that we can ensure that families who are juggling more than just their work schedule, as we know, um have the opportunity to have the case for the action.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I think four or five working days left in session.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Um, whether we pass the heavy C Brisbard bill again, which is one way to fix this, or OCFS changes its regulations, which is another way to fix this, we just need it fixed because the families in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Sorry, I'm done.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Bye.

chapters.summary: Council Member Jennifer Gutiérrez expresses concern over the impact of preventive cuts on organizations like CAMBA and Forestale, and asks about the percentage of families unable to recertify child care vouchers.
chapters.title: Council Member Gutiérrez Questions Impact of Preventive Cuts and Voucher Recertification

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Councilmember Gutierrez, followed by uh Council uh Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I don't know if he's here or Brayou, but we'll move on, if not.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Good to see everyone.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Welcome, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Councilmember Shulman about the preventative service.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I understand the role of the chief savings officer.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Obviously, I think we're all in agreement.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: If we want to be able to fund programs that we have money for and fund effective programs, I do want to say that families in my district, I know the folks from Canva and Forestale are here.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: They will be testifying.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: But I really think that putting something out or making a proposal that cuts severely cuts this program without much of like a safety, without an explanation of what's going to happen to those jobs, without explaining what's gonna happen to those families impacted is deeply, deeply concerning.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: And you heard from uh Chair Lee about just the the very simple math of the cost the eventual cost savings to the city when we do invest in preventative care.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: So I just want to uplift uh council member Lee.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I of course am also asking some questions about the child care vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: It's a hot topic.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Um, and mainly because obviously we care deeply about early child care, as I think this administration does, but we have families that are saying, What about me?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: When the mayor is talking about child care, he's not talking about me, he's not talking about the voucher that I'm desperately waiting on.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: So I just have some data if you can if you have them specifically, and I'll I'll sign up for round two if I need.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Um, but can you share what percentage as I understand it, every family was able to recertify uh within this fiscal year?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: The vast majority of families have the opportunity to recertify, provided they submit a recertification application, it's timely, it's complete.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Um there is a small percentage, it's about, and not to minimize how important childcare is to this population, but there are about six to seven percent of families that we serve that are not able to continue with their voucher because when we have limited funding, we are required by federal rules to serve three populations first families that are unhoused, families with a child with special needs, and families that are very low income, which is under 300% of the state income standard.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay, and so that's around six six percent of those folks unable to kind of maintain their voucher.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Council Member Jennifer Gutiérrez asks about the challenges faced by families who recertify but cannot add younger siblings to their vouchers, and when ACS expects to begin moving the waitlist.
chapters.title: Council Member Gutiérrez Questions Voucher Eligibility for Younger Siblings

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Do you have a sense of of those that were able to recertify how many of them potentially have siblings, younger siblings that were not able to qualify?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Because I think that puts parents in basically back to square one where they have one child who is utilizing a voucher, but they technically can't go to back to work because they have a younger child.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Do you have a sense of that?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I don't have a sense of magnitude.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: We we know it's an issue.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: I mean, we do have families reaching out to us telling us that they are trying to add a child to their case, and that child might be on the wait list if the family is still eligible serving other children.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Um, we can look into whether or not those analytics are possible and get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Um, just so you know, we we a few of my colleagues and myself jointly sent a letter to some of our our folks um at the state.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Um, knowing full and well the budget would not have been passed by April 1st.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Obviously, we don't have anything.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think what you've shared today is just nothing substantial.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: My last question, um, and I'll stand up for round two is are there any um specific circumstances outside of seeing this miraculously in the state budget that would allow for ACS to begin offering vouchers to eligible families?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Well, I want to be clear that we will be looking at what is in the state budget, which is a substantial increase in funding that is allowing us at baseline to continue to say that we've got to allow people that have to restart it by.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Which is not nothing.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: Which is not nothing.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: And we will be looking at where enrollment stands across all three agencies and spending to assess if there is a time in the next months that we may be able to begin moving the wait list.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Wolkomir
utterances.text: But we don't know that yet because we need to assess what's in the budget, what attrition in our system looks like, what spending across ACS HRA and New York City Public Schools looks like.

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Nantasha Williams asks ACS to outline their short- and mid-term goals in relation to the city's preliminary racial equity plan.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams Questions ACS on Racial Equity Goals

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay, um, Deputy Speaker Williams, followed by Councilmember Muley.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Um I'm just gonna ask a few questions about the racial equity plans.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Uh I'm asking all the agencies these questions.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: We're super excited to have the prelim plans and want to make sure that as we talk about the budget that we have a lens of racial equity.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Um, what has ACS determined are the short and midterm goals to achieve in relation to the equity plan?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I started to say good afternoon, good morning, deputy speaker.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Uh it feels like afternoon already.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, I know I said this for another question, but I just want to say for the record that first deputy commissioners winette Saunders, who's leading this work for us, um, wanted to be here to be able to answer this question, but is not.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I'm here answering it for her, but um, she unfortunately couldn't make it today for our medical appointment.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We love her, it's okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So um on the short-term goals, we have launching school-based early support prevention, um, increasing community-based referrals to prevention, and those are the short term.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Do you want me to do the middle term?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yeah, just do it.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Middle term is continuing to invest in training ACS staff and providers to provide quality services and to foster a psychologically safe work environment that embraces equity and transparency.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And another one is investing in the development of community spaces that contribute to healthy child development and family strength, such as the FECs.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Another is to continue to decrease unnecessary child welfare reports by promoting a citywide culture shift among mandated reporters so that they're not calling the SER when there's a child not who doesn't have a safety reason.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And another is to strengthen restorative approaches to addressing conflict in juvenile justice settings.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And then do you want me to do the long term?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: No, the short and midterm is fine.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Nantasha Williams asks if any specific budgetary actions are required to achieve the identified racial equity goals within the current budget framework.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams Inquires About Budgetary Actions for Equity Goals
