utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, what if I'm happy to share the full plan with you?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I think it's public.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Yeah, it's definitely um what if any budgetary actions need to be taken to help achieve these goals?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Well, before before uh Deputy Commissioner Gindale jumps in, I just want to also just kind of chime in here.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg
utterances.text: It's a program that started in Brooklyn and is going to be expanded to Queens and the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsburg

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks about ACS's coordination with the Department of Correction to support children in foster care whose parents are incarcerated at Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Brewer?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Um I have a question about Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I go to Rikers a lot, and I have been a foster parent, and then now I have the migrant kids in my house, so I'm familiar with the with the crew.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My question is um people coming out of Rikers or coming out from upstate women in particular.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Are you in touch with Department of Correction?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Because we all know that maybe the kids are in foster care, they really want to go back with their parents, they can't go back till they have a place to live, blah blah blah.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I worry about that population because that population I know well you get mental health issues when your parents are in incarcerated.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So do you have that kind of connection at all, or is that something that you're focused on?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Either with Fortune Society correction or anybody else.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I just want to make sure we're clear.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Are you asking about children in foster care whose parents are incarcerated?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And I'm also concerned, it's not a big population.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I don't know, four or five hundred women at Rikers, but it is a population where young people can be damaged mentally if they don't get support.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: That's important to us now, Patrick.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: ACS has a team, it's called CHIP, Children of Incarcerated Parents.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And we work very closely with the foster care providers to ensure while children are in care and their parents are are incarcerated throughout the state or even in the country to facilitate visits.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And that team also, you know, works closely with agencies because case planning should continue.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And when fit when you know parents are released and they're moving back into the area, if the goal remains reunification, there's an expectation that the agencies are supporting those families to assess and you know make a decision about safe and timely reunification.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: To your question about like a formal like agreement or um partnership, we don't have that at now, but we can definitely take that and you know.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I would like to see, I think look at that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously, the new DOC commissioner is phenomenal, known him for 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And the fact of the matter is I think he would be interested in it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We're trying to reduce the population.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This would be one way to do it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You know, that you know the issues.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I I don't know if there's funding allocated to it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I don't know what the numbers are, but I'd love to see some data as to how many women could be reunited.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You can know where this is going so that the families are in better shape, the women are in better shape, and they get support.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is that something that you could get back to us on?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Number two would be trouble.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I get in trouble when I say I'll take look into that.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So, yes, I think so.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It's a really important population, and they're gonna end up in recidivism if that issue is not addressed, in my opinion.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Well, and I'll remove her getting in trouble and say yes, we'll absolutely follow up, and I look forward to meeting the commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.text: Um second issue quickly is um between Fairfair, a wonderful program, um, and the fact that uh true colors and others are trying to deal with young people coming out of foster care, becoming adults, etc.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Oh, agreed.

utterances.text: Um, absolutely.

utterances.text: So for calendar year 2024, we had 420 young people non permanency discharges.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: That number is down in calendar year 2025 to 383.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We work very closely with the foster care providers who do a wonderful job engaging young people and preparing them for transition.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So there are independent living skills that are provided prior.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have our fair futures, we work very closely to understand and assess the child's needs post um foster care.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Will they need supportive housing?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have we work, you know, with our supportive with HRA and and HPD to make sure that we, if they need supportive housing, they can go there.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We we work closely with Anthos, um, and we have Citif's vouchers, so young people get City FEPS vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Or Laura Lazarus, yes, keep going.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And the Anthos does aftercare, so they help with the stabilization after because we know that once you just get your keys, there's more to mean.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm aware, very aware.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Um, Fair Futures, I think you know, is up to 20 um six for those that decide to leave care prior to turning 21.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We have a team called Supervision of 21 that does their best to track these young people, um, providing them supports, connecting them to services, um, and then if need be, we'll hold a conference to see if they need to come back.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I say all that to say we do try as much as possible to have a net around these individuals to support them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So the organization that you mentioned will make sure that our providers are aware to connect the young people to that organization, and also the Center for Fair Futures as they're working with the coaches.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I guess my question would be not right now, but is there extra funding, are there extra resources, despite all the amazing work that you, Fair Futures, Laura and everybody else is doing?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: That would be my question.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: You always need more money, but in this case, this is a really again a really important thing.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I can take that back and look into that.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I would appreciate this population is recidivism if you don't address it now.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez

utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilman Rarua.

utterances.text: Um, I'm gonna do a lightning round just because I I know that there's a couple of folks who still want to ask a few questions.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So um, and I can be corrected.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We have not notified in writing yet.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Um, I did have an opportunity to touch face with each of the organization's leadership um yesterday, and um, we are waiting for um kind of final uh approval on the budget, and then we'll move forward with written um communication and then the timeline of the 90 days as stated earlier.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about the current number of vacancies within the child protective services division and whether any positions were eliminated as part of savings.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: The executive plan includes a budget headcount of 300 385 for protective service areas.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: How many vacancies are there currently in this program?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Um, I'm gonna do a couple of questions.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Were any vacancies in this program area limited as part of the mayor's savings?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: If so, how many and why?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm gonna turn that over to Deputy Commissioner Linares.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Hi again.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, so we have the contract capacity for family support uh model again is 4,512.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, know that that's about 44% of our total capacity, which is 10,637.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um in April, again, we had a total of three 3,347 families that were participating in this services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we have over a thousand contractors slot capacity uh to serve families in the family support programs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That doesn't account uh for the other programs that we have available in the boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We do not anticipate that any of the families will be wait listed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And we've until there's a different reasons why there are some vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we've taken steps to increase utilization.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have worked closely with uh DHS and NYCHA to make sure that we are in the community.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We do things like Family Day and invite prevention providers to the table so that they can connect with families in an effort to make sure that their utilization is increased.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Hence I think we have you can see an increase in our community referrals where we've gone up to about uh from last year, the same time about 20%.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Were you also asking?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Sorry.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about the onboarding timeline, background checks, and simulation training at the Satterwhite Academy for newly hired Child Protective Specialists.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Were you also asking about child protective?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Sorry, I misunderstood.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so the plan doesn't have any new savings there.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We currently have 191 vacancies in the division of child protection.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um CPS, the actual child protective specialist um budget position is 1,855, and there are 108 that are vacant.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: 108 that vegan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, what is the civil timeline for frontline staff such as um child protection specialists?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Does ACS perform its own background checks on candidates?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If so, long, how long does it take, and if not, how um does those background checks take place?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: There is a background check um prac policy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, it varies by position, what's required, and so it would vary how long it takes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Do you have like an average?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Six weeks.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's about six weeks.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Six weeks.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, expertise.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How does the onboarding typically take place um and try um train a child protective specialist?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And what is the percentage of CPS candidates per um provided job offers actually complete the hiring process and onboarding?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What difficulties has ACS face in hiring those workers and what is ACS doing to address those issues?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we have a very extensive training program for new CPS.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um they spend several months at the Satter White Academy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and that also includes um we have a simulation site where part of the job is practicing what it's like to go to court, what it's like to go to home.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and then they go into a training unit in their offices where they have a small caseload and less complicated cases, and then they go back to training in the academy because they'll have had a better sense of what the jobs like when they've done a few cases and they go back to the academy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it takes several months, um, and I think about 75, like route three quarters complete.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Closer to 70 percent, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, those are all the questions I have now.

chapters.summary: Council Member Rita Joseph asks for the current census of Crossroads and Horizon detention centers compared to the total capacity they were built to accommodate.
chapters.title: Council Member Joseph Questions Detention Center Capacity

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I still have a number of questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We'll send them to you um in writing because I know we are kind of pressed for time, and so just want to just remind our colleagues and you guys to try to be as succinct as possible because DYCD is in the other room waiting, please.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, round two.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We got Councilmember Joseph, followed by Councilmember Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, just wanted to ask a quick question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How many um young people we have at Crossroads and how many are at the horizon?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Real quick.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: 181 at horizon, 186 at crossroads.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What is the capacity the the facilities were built for?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: All right, so bedrooms.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're gonna hold that right there.

chapters.summary: Council Member Rita Joseph asks about ACS's collaboration with New York City Public Schools to provide reliable transportation and car services for students placed in foster care.
chapters.title: Council Member Joseph Questions School Transportation for Foster Youth

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, the city has a unique obligation for students in foster care who are under the city's care and custody and must do more to support their education, including ensuring that they get to school in the first place.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: These are feasible options.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Has ACS worked with New York City Public Schools to identify and implement a solution that allows students to get to school as soon as they are placed in foster care or change foster care homes as utilizing ACS vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, yes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So in 2022, in October, November, we worked with New York City Public Schools, the foster care office and the transportation office, and we released uh reimbursement plan and guidelines around transportation, and it outlined that as soon as children come into care, the school should be notified, and then um providers will be reimbursed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: At the time it was up to 200 a day if they had to cover the transportation because the providers are the foster parent.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and we've had that plan in place.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We made some revisions, I believe, last school year, and then New York City public schools, after for some additional conversations and hearing from the community, um, increased the 200 a day.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So that that is a new um facet.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we're in the process of re revising the transition plan, and also have kind of extended who could apply.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it's children that are going to school in the city or out of the city, and then I believe those that live out of the city that need to come into the city, and then also they will reimburse providers if they use other entities that have there's other types of car service, which wasn't part of the original plan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we're in the process of revising the current policy, and so hopefully we'll have that online for the start of the next school year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: For children at the children's center, when you think about it, they are in ACS's custody.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we are now the responsible for getting them to, and when all of the children are in our custody, but like they're in our house, if you will.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we are responsible.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have an added interest to make sure they go to school.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we use our ACS transportation, and our uh deputy commissioner of administration coordinates with the Children's Center team to make sure those cars are available and they have a plan and they get the children to school.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And the ones that are in agency service this fall, you will have a plan to make sure they get to and from school because they have the highest absenteeism rate entering into care.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we're hoping that, and we'll, you know, we'll reiterate with the foster care providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're hoping that with this revised policy, they're gonna get reimbursed for more money per day.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um they'll be able to use other services like kids' car, that should increase the attendance and the the and be reduce the challenges that they've experienced.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lynn Schulman asks if there are data-sharing agreements in place to help public school staff identify student needs and match them with prevention services.
chapters.title: Council Member Schulman Questions Data Sharing for Prevention Services

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, council member Shulman, followed by Councilmember Gutierrez.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I have two two questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: One, are there currently any tools and data sharing agreements in place that make it easier for public school staff to understand student needs and support matching with prevention services?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Hi, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, um, as you may know, because of social service laws, we will need um for prevention services, we need uh consent forms to get information uh about the education of the children.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so we don't have a data agreement where we can exchange uh data aggregately.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: However, if a provider is uh in need of specific information about a specific child, uh as long as they have a consent form, we are able to work with them and get them the information that they need.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um but unfortunately, because of service laws, uh we we need a consent.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So there's been some conversations uh in turn to try to figure out how to do that, but great.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We will continue those conversation and try to figure out how to do it in a way that uh obviously keep the confidentiality of the family uh safe.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Now keep us informed.

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Nantasha Williams asks if ACS still intends to expand Fair Futures to justice-involved youth under the racial equity plan despite recent budget reductions.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams Questions Fair Futures Expansion for Justice-Involved Youth

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And the other is that should these cuts go through for the preventive services?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I would like a briefing on how that's gonna affect particularly the um the agency in my district.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, that's a yes, okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, Deputy Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, I'm back.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, back to the racial equity plans.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So ACS pledged to expand fair futures to provide justice involved youth with coaches, housing specialists, and educational services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Given the reduction in funding in fiscals 2026 through 2028 for fair Futures, that was included as a savings in the exec plan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Does ACS still intend to achieve this expansion?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Will any slots that were earmarked for the pledges in the racial equity plan be eliminated due to this reduction in funding?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And how many additional youth does ACS hope to serve if it does expand the program?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: When can we expect to see this expansion and how will it be funded?

chapters.summary: Council Member Rita Joseph asks for data on college enrollment among youth aging out of foster care and the academic and career re-engagement supports available to them.
chapters.title: Council Member Joseph Questions College Enrollment and Career Re-Engagement for Foster Youth

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We are continuing to expand Fair Futures.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and it is in both on the juvenile justice side and on child welfare.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The budget action slows the growth, but the growth that by 20 by fiscal 2029 we achieved the same end number of 5400, which was the goal when we got to fiscal 2029.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If we feel that's not sufficient, obviously we can have conversations about where to go from there.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And back to councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, real quick.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, two more questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Over the past five years, how many youth have aged out of foster care annually and what percentage of those youth are enrolled in college?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I don't have the past five years.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I can give you um we had in council in calendar year 2024, 420 non permanency discharges.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So those are youth leaving foster care.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And in calendar 2025, we had 383.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I'll have to get the other information and then also crosswalk that with our college choice data.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We do know that when College Choice launched in 2022, we had 200 young people, and I believe the fall the spring semester of 2026, we had 471 youth in college.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But I will have to crosswalk the exits with who was in college at the time.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: No problem.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: For foster youth with foster care, um, for students with foster care experience who did not complete college, what academic career re-engagement supports are available for them for re-enrolling or transition into employment pathways.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So for young people that are in college specifically, um, we work very closely with New York Foundling.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They do our college support services program, and they provide um in addition to supporting around the financial issues, the wraparound academic advisement.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So for those that are maybe struggling, let's say they're on probation, they're providing additional support.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If they do have to leave school, we still they still, if they're in care, still have their case planning team, they'll have their fair futures coach, and then we have our team at ACS.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we do re work to re-engage.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: There have been young people that have been in the college choice program, discontinued, and now are looking at our career choice program, which launched this fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So maybe they're gonna take a different path.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee convenes the second part of the joint hearing, introducing the Department of Youth and Community Development (DYCD) proposed $1.62 billion budget and highlighting key changes since the preliminary plan.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Opens DYCD Executive Budget Hearing

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I think we are good.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thank you so much for joining us.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're just gonna take a quick break.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Wait, how was it?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh, were we nice?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You survived?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It was great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'll be back, I'll be back.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh no, I really don't know.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Just a reminder, everyone, please do not approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Please do not approach a dais at any point.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: No food or beverages are allowed in this room.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Just a reminder no one may approach the day is at any time, and no food or beverages are allowed in this room.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I have my snacks.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Waiting for okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, I think we can go ahead and get started.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, so good afternoon and welcome to today's second hearing uh about the FY27 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I'm Councilmember Linda Lee, and today we will hear from Department of Youth and Community Development.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I'm pleased to be joined again by my colleague, uh, Councilmember and Chair Althea Stevens.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And we have been joined by the Deputy Speaker, Councilmember Maloney, Councilmember Epstein, Councilmember Wong, and Councilmember Felder.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and welcome, Commissioner uh and your team, and thank you so much for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I just want to note it's really cool that the three of us have all this uh former nonprofit experience, and I think there need to be more of us in government spaces, so I'll just say that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I will just say that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um May 12th, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY26 through 2030 with a proposed fiscal 27 budget of 124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The Department of Youth and Community Development's proposed FY27 budget of 1.62 billion represents 1.3% of the administration's proposed fiscal 27 budget in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This is a decrease of 14.2 million or less than 1% from the $1.63 billion originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This decrease results from several actions, including savings the agency found through elimination of certain consulting contracts, vacancy reduction, cola adjustments, and underspending in community resources for employment and development and development and the ONS programs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This was slightly offset by addition of funding to expand services for the crisis management system program and the supporting wraparound services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As of April 26th, DYCD's actual headcount of 626 is 71 less than their fiscal 26 budgeted headcount of 697.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In the council's preliminary budget response, we called on the mayor to add 4.14.2 million to DYCD's budget to invest in expense funding for programs related to runaway and homeless youth, peer navigators, and runaway homeless youth housing navigators.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Although these requests were not included in the plan, we remain hopeful that today's hearing will give us the opportunity to discuss a path forward to secure funding for these programs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And now I want to turn it over to my co-chair for the hearing, um, Chair Stevens.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens delivers her opening statement on the DYCD budget, highlighting investments in the Crisis Management System (CMS) and expressing concern over the lack of baseline funding for Runaway and Homeless Youth (RHY) navigators.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Delivers Opening Statement on DYCD Budget and RHY Funding

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Good afternoon, and welcome to our second half of the fiscal 2027 executive budget hearing for the Department of Youth and Community Development.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I am Councilmember Althea Stevens, Chair of the Committee on Children and Youth.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Today we are will be hearing from DYCD, our new commissioner, Sandra Eskadiz, along with our first deputy and our financial officers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you for joining us today.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're here to discuss the agency recent release of executive plan, which includes a budget of $1.6 billion for fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This funding includes $66.2 million in personnel services to support and a budget headcount for 704 positions and a total of 1.55 billion other than personnel.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The current fiscal 2027 budget is 105.3 million or 7% more than the fiscal 26 adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: However, DYCD's fiscal 2027's budget decreased by a 14.2 million from the 1.63 billion budgeted in the fiscal 2027 Flynn Rafe Financial Plan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: To note a few changes in the executive plan, there's an additional $14.8 million to expand services for the crisis management, CMS programs, and supporting wraparound services and fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In fiscal 2028 in the outer years, the plan includes a baseline funding of 19.6 million in the Office of Community Safety so further support the programs in the new pro in the new office.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: A plan also added $8.8 million for the Office of Economic Opportunity Adjustments for the Advanced in Earn Program that helped to train and employ young people ages 16 through 25.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The plan also includes the savings agencies found to meet the targets set by the Office of Management and Budget that included 6.3 million for over budgeting in the community resources for employment and development cred and ONS programs or surplus costs of cost of living adjustment resulting in a $31 million after assessing that providers district distribution funding needed to provide the three percent increase for human service contract.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I want to ensure that the coal adjustment does not impact programming and provides adequate funding to the providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm concerned that the executive plan includes baseline city fund savings of three million from the elimination of 27 vacant positions beginning in fiscal 2026.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Again, I want to ensure that actions don't impact programming for youth.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The executive budget did not include fiscal 2026, one um one chat funding for RHY funding and peer navigators, which is something that I've been fighting consistently with to make sure that we're funding because we do not want to forget about our young people.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I do hope the administration will provide baseline funding and the adopted budget for the navigators because that was a program that we lost, and then we got in the council brought back.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so we want to make sure that this is something that's credible because the young people and providers have said it's a critical service need.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This gives them a sense of belonging, a sense of understanding.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The housing navigators assess in navigating the young people through transition and a safe dependable housing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The services for RHY youth does not stop at the door, but it begins at the door.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens continues her opening statement, praising the release of the new Compass RFP but raising concerns about the negative financial impact on providers who were not awarded contracts.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Raises Concerns Over Compass RFP Outcomes

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm pleased that after 10 years, it's a long time.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: A new compass RFP for elementary and middle school students was um was released, and I do appreciate the dedication and hard work by DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I really want to give it up to you guys because everyone knows that I was so critical that they put this on you guys in the turnaround.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it it was done.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I knew it was gonna get done.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: No one, I mean, Dan, you are a little grayer, I'm not gonna lie, but you still look good.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They aged you, I see it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um but you guys worked really hard to get it done and work it through.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I'm really happy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: However, you know, with new RFPs, there are always these mitigating factors that come in, and including myself and everyone here, and and councilman Williams just came to me and said she's also being spammed about the compass RFP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We are getting so many calls about a lot of worried providers who have to fold as a result of the outcome of the deficit of services that they will be able to provide to the communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and I'm concerned, and I know as our new commissioners come in, she is being so thoughtful and having a perspective of a former provider of trying to figure out the best way forward and make sure that everyone is whole, including the providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, you know, I think sometimes we talk about it as if the providers are not a workforce as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And you know, this council has spent a lot of time and energy talking about the workforce, but for some reason we forget to keep fighting for human service workers, and I think that is just as important.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so when we do these contracts, we have to, and I know you guys have been thoughtful.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think as a council, we have to think about like how do we protect them as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and that's something that I'm dedicated and have been dedicated in doing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: There are other DYCD programs that are overdue for the RFP, like my new fight, the Beacons and Cornerstones.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I want the agency to take this opportunity to further evaluate the conference RFP, examine every process, every methodology, and matrix used to identify changes that can be made for the next RFP, so that it has a fair and more and equitable process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And honestly, I I think that if any we we probably need to get all the RFPs done while Sandra's here, I'ma just say it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um because I just have a feeling but with that provider background, um, it'll it'll look a lot different than this one did.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I'm gonna be pushing for a lot more RFPs, just so we're clear, so we could have a nice stable programs so that when when I'm gone, we'll be in good hands.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so I'm excited about that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so I'll be pushing for that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Everyone here today in fiscal 2027 executive budget hearing has a responsibility with the objective of providing the best services to the youth of the city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It is my expect expectation from DYCD, you guys will be responsive to questions and concerns of the council members.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It is my hope that fiscal 2027 adopted budget meets the goals set forth by council.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We want to ensure that every youth and adult program and provides the essentials necessities, including adequate funding to meet the everyday needs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I would like to recognize I've recognized most of my staff um already, but I want to recognize the financial, the fiscal staff, Sandra Gray, our senior fiscal analyst, uh Leah Aliy Alia, Assistant Director, and I don't see Isha on here, but also Isha.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Shout out to her because I've been driving her crazy as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So with that, I will turn it over to Chair Lee.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel administers the oath to DYCD Commissioner Sandra Escamilla Davies and her executive staff, who affirm to tell the truth before the committees.
chapters.title: DYCD Witnesses Sworn In

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and just as a reminder, really quickly, um, we're having all of our public testimony for all the agencies on Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 9 30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 9 30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: is when we're having a long all day.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're gonna have so much fun all day of public testimony hearings.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So please uh make sure to sign up for those.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um okay, and then without oh wait, hold on.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Councilmember Jay Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Everyone's gonna start thinking your first name, his first name is Jay, Justin Sanchez, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um Councilmember Ressler, Councilmember Wilson, welcome our newest council member, and Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and with that, I'll turn it over to committee counsel to swear in the witnesses for their testimony.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions, commissioner Askanilla Davies, Deputy Commissioner Haskell, Deputy Commissioner Mulligan, Chief Financial Officer Bailey, Chief Accountability Officer Tadowini, and Associate Commissioner Gehan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: DYCD Commissioner Sandra Escamilla Davies delivers her opening testimony, outlining her assessment of the agency's internal infrastructure and detailing the $1.619 billion FY27 Executive Budget.
chapters.title: DYCD Commissioner Sandra Escamilla Davies Details FY27 Budget and Savings

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And Lee and members of the Finance and Children and Youth Committee.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I am Commissioner Sandra Scamilla Davies, and I am joined by Chief Financial Officer Navida Bailey and other DYC staff who are here and available to answer your questions today.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to discuss DYCD's fiscal year 2027 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Since becoming commissioner, I have spent the past few three months listening and learning from staff, providers, and partners across the city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What has become clear is that DYCD is powered by extraordinary dedicated workforce and a provider network that continues to deliver critical services for children, youth, and families every day.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As the agency has grown significantly over the years, both in scale and complexity, my team and I are taking a thoughtful look at how our internal infrastructures, staffing, operational systems support that work.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This includes examining areas such as procurement, contracting, payments, program operations, and performance management so that DYCD can continue to operate in a way that is responsive, effective, and aligned with the evolving needs of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: At the same time, we are engaging providers and community partners to better understand both operational challenges and opportunities for innovation across our programs and services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: These conversations are helpful to inform how we strengthen and broad and broaden the ecosystem that supports young people and families across this great city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The executive budget presented today reflects Mayor Mamdani's vision for a more affordable, equitable, and safer New York City and demonstrates a continued commitment to the children, youth families, and communities that DYCD serves every day.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Despite broader fiscal pressures facing the city, the budget protects DYCD's core programming and continues to invest in services and opportunities that support young people and strengthen communities across all five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: DYCD proposed a series of savings totaling 10.9 million in the current fiscal year, 34.8 million next year, and 32.7 million in the outer years.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This includes savings from conducting audit functions in-house, along with savings from readjustments to cost estimates.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The budget stands at 1.619 billion compared to 1.633 billion in fiscal 2027 preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And the breakdown is as follows: 1.5 billion in tax levy funds, 105.4 million in federal funds, 18.4 million or about 1.13% in state funding, and 5.2 million in intra-city funds.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: These resources support a wide range of investments across the agency's portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: While not an exhaustive list, I want to highlight several key initiatives that reflect how these funds strengthen services for young people, families, and communities across the city.

chapters.summary: DYCD Commissioner Sandra Escamilla Davies details the outcomes of the COMPASS RFP, highlighting the addition of 20,000 new elementary school seats and the inclusion of 52 new community-based organizations.
chapters.title: DYCD Commissioner Highlights COMPASS RFP Awards and Modernization

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: First, I'll start with the COMPAS request for proposals.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: COMPASS is the nation's largest afterschool system serving elementary and middle school students across New York City through COMPASS Elementary and schools out New York City.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The release of the COMPASS Center Base and School Based Awards marks the very first major reprocurement and modernization of after-school contracts in more than a decade, and that is just so exciting.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This investment reflects what families, schools, and providers have consistently called for: expanded access, stronger program, quality, workforce stability, and greater equity across the afterschool system.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: For fiscal year 2027, the city committed to $727 million in COMPASS funding.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This investment supports the additional 20,000 new elementary school seats over three school years and increased the price per participant rate to better support provider stability staffing and high quality program for children and families.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: DYCD announced, and I want to highlight some of the things from this RFP, 121 center-based awards representing 7,840 seats, 806 school-based awards representing 95,841 seats, expansions into 71 new elementary schools across 32 high-need community districts, 8,621 additional citywide seats, and 52 new community-based organizations joining the COMPASS portfolio, including at least one in every borough.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This RFP also introduced an updated program model designed to better reflect the evolving needs of young people and families, including strong expectations around social emotional learning, career exploration and future readiness, expanded inclusion supports, enhanced program quality standards, and greater alignment between the after school programming and broader youth development goals.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The increased investment in COMPASS represents an investment in stability for the sector by supporting stronger staffing structures, better wages, more sustainable programs, models, and expanding access for children and families across New York City.

chapters.summary: DYCD Commissioner Sandra Escamilla Davies discusses the expansion of Summer Rising 2026 to 379 sites and highlights the mayoral commitment to continue funding housing and peer navigators for runaway and homeless youth.
chapters.title: DYCD Commissioner Details Summer Rising 2026 and RHY Support

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We are proud of this expanded access, strengthening program models and new investments reflected in these awards.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It really is noteworthy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yet at the same time, I want to recognize that there were providers who were not awarded contracts through this process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: For some organizations, this transition may have significant financial and operational impacts, particularly for providers that have served children, families, and school communities for many years.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We do not take that lightly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I do not take that lightly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Those contributions, relationships, and years of service are meaningful and worthy of recognition and respect.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And yet it is also true that this was a competitive procurement process guided by clearly defined evaluation criteria, including program quality, organizational capacity, and operational readiness.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In partnership with New York City Public Schools, the process also incorporated incorporated principal input for school-based programs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As implementation moves forward, we are committed to focus on supporting thoughtful transitions, maintaining continuity for young people and families, and working closely with schools, outgoing providers, incoming providers, and communities to help open strong in the fall.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The second program that I want to highlight is summarizing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The summarizing 2026 application portal opened on March 3rd and closed on March 27th, during which time more than 150,000 applications were submitted by families across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Using a data-driven understanding of enrollment, patterns, and demand, the city extended approximately 127,000 offers to families.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And this is an increase of 3,500 offers compared to last summer.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Available seats will continue to be offered to families throughout the summer.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: To support expanded access, New York City Public Schools opened 19 additional buildings, bringing the total of summer rising sites citywide to 379.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: DYCD and New York City Public Schools continue to work closely together to strengthen and expand high-quality summer enrichment opportunities for young people.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This includes deeper collaboration between principals and community-based organizations, strengthening supports for students with disability, and enhancing program opportunities such as field trips, enhanced activities, and career exploration.

chapters.summary: DYCD Commissioner Sandra Escamilla Davies concludes her testimony by detailing the $246 million investment in the Summer Youth Employment Program (SYEP) and highlighting the achievements of the Fatherhood Program.
chapters.title: DYCD Commissioner Outlines SYEP and Fatherhood Program Initiatives

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The next program I'd like to highlight is Runaway Homeless Youth.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We are so thankful for the early mayoral commitment to continue the critical role of housing navigation, peer navigators, and financial coaches as part of the services offered youth in our RHY drop-in center and FY27.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The budget process is ongoing, but we are pleased that the city recognizes the importance of these supports for runaway homeless youth, and providers have the opportunity now to better plan the delivery of service for the upcoming fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're in the thick of summer youth employment.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Mayor Manzani officially launched the 2026 Summer Youth Employment on January 20th, marking the earliest application opening in program's history.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Funded at 246 million, SYP will once again serve more than 100,000 youth this summer through paid work experiences, career exploration, skill building, and supportive opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As one of the nation's largest youth employment programs, SYP continues to be a critical pathway to economic opportunity and future readiness ready for young New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And this year, DYCD is expanding career ready programs to 260 schools, up from 230 in 2025, while continuing high school credit and career ready CUNY reach so that more students can earn high school or college credit through participation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The program also continues to prioritize young people facing barriers to employment, including NYCHA residents and youth living in priority precinct communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In addition, DYCD is continuing initiatives launched in previous years, including SYP Pride in partnership with the Mayor's Office of Equity and Racial Justice and the New York City Commission on Human Rights, which offers providers and employers training, supportive work experience, and professional development opportunities for participants.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And finally, the Fatherhood Program.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: To date, DYCD Fatherhood Program has engaged more than 500 fathers, supporting them as they navigate co-parenting, custody, and other complex family challenges while strengthening parent skills, family stability, and long-term father engagement.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Through workshops, trainings, and supportive resources, these programs help fathers build connections with their children, families, and communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Last month, DYCD hosted its second annual fatherhood policy symposium, convening more than 100 leaders from government, academia, and community organizations to highlight the critical role fathers play in wellbeing of children and families and to advance father-friendly policies and practices across the city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: On June 25th, DYCD will host its fifth annual affirming father convening at John Jay College of Criminal Justice to further elevate the importance of father engagement, leadership, and community connection while continuing conversations about how systems and institutions can better support fathers and family across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And if I had more time, I'll I would tout all of the other wonderful programs that this investment will yield for us, as well as this extraordinary staff that I have behind me and working on the field right now that are doing everything they can to provide quality support to the network of providers that serve young people every day.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks about the $107.6 million reduction in community development programs and whether these funds were shifted to other areas of the DYCD portfolio.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions DYCD on Contractual Service Reductions

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you again for the opportunity to discuss the fiscal 2027 executive budget, and we're looking forward to answering any questions you may have.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Councilmember Brooks Powers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I'm just gonna dive right.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh Councilmember Wong.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh, wait, I think I said it the first time, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, Councilmember Felder.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: For the second such a riot.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I'm gonna just dive right in so I can pass it off to my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um for DYCD's FY27 budget um in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, when comparing FY27 budget in the executive plan to FY26 adopted budget, we see that there's a reduction of 107.6 million in community development pro to community development programs, of which 103.9 million is contractual services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, why is there a reduction in contractual services for community development programs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And also uh what is the reason for the reduction of 1.6 million in full-time salary positions?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Nice to see you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Nice to see you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So the original reduction that you're referencing regarding um is within the city portfolio, and that's adult literacy, and that may be attributed to the one-time funding that we get annually for their portfolio, the restoration.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, so uh and then was the funding moved to other programs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: No, because it's a one-time restoration that we typically get from the council for the C portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, okay, got it.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks which programs will see a headcount reduction as part of the 27 vacant positions eliminated under the mayor's savings plan.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions DYCD on Vacancy Reductions

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and then in terms of the vacancy reduction, a reduction of three million in FY26 to FY30 for a headcount of 27 in each fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, which programs will see a headcount reduction and what is the amount for each?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: At this time we have not identified the locations where we'll have the reductions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We were just notified of it, and as you know, the agency has all our hands full with the implementation of these new initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so we intend to in short order start to look at where we will um apply these reductions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If you could keep us updated on that, that'd be awesome.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um is this just a reduced vacancy for each of the programs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If so, uh, what is the remaining vacancy for each of the programs and the overall percentage?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I know you're still working this out, but just some of the questions in terms of the alignment of where we're going.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and then also with this headcount reduction impact services provided by the agency, hopefully the answer is no.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The answer is no.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um ultimately, as the commission has indicated in our first hearing, um, she's doing an assessment of the organization, and so at this time we have not necessarily identified the total impact of it, but the ultimate goal is to ensure that there's no disruption to services, and so we are confident that with the remaining vacancies that we have and our existing staff that we'll do our best to make sure that you um do appropriate oversight um in the program areas and um ensure contracts are registered and paid.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks about additional savings identified under Executive Order 12 and requests the budget breakdown for school-based versus center-based COMPASS programs.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Inquires About Executive Order 12 Savings and COMPASS Budget

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And what other savings, if any, were identified in the executive budget due to the mayor's executive twelve order?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Sure, and so as part of the executive 12 order, we looked at the new organization to see where we can find efficiencies and um how funding is um utilized, and we identified surplus as well as unobligated funding in a number of program areas, and with that we submitted that, and um that was accepted, and as a result, we did not do any service cuts for any of our programs, and so we were just prioritizing that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and then just really quickly moving over to COMPAS because I know you all have been putting a lot of effort into the new RFPs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I gotta say, I I I started my I did my first year social work degree at a beacon program, so I just want to plus one to what you're saying.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um but um I know you said there was 727 million total for the RFPs, and so what's the breakdown with the school based versus the center-based budgets?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I don't have that offering, I can get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I don't want to give you wrong information, but I can give that to you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks if DYCD has enough time to process and register the new COMPASS contracts before the start of the upcoming school year to prevent cash flow issues for providers.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions COMPASS Contract Registration Timeline

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and now that you have provided the list of new providers who will be entering the compensation, does the agency feel there's enough time to I think I process the contracts by the beginning of the FY27 school year?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I know that's a challenge that you guys are trying to push through.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So just wondering how if you could speak to that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We are committed to ensuring that the contracts are registered on time.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have a team that's dedicated to moving with the providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, we've had sessions with the providers to ensure that they are clear on the documents that are needed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're working very closely with Mox as well as the controller's office to expedite registration.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We also know that the timeline is very, very tight.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and we're planning to, you know, the biggest thing for providers is cash flow, so we are making sure that we have a plan in place for those that may not be registered on time.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we are definitely committed to getting them registered as quickly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And you got to include OMB in that conversation too, and the uh controller's office, because we got to get those contracts registered quickly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um what is the target date for completing the new contracts?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And does the agency foresee any obstacles in meeting the target date?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I know you just mentioned some of those things, but um what's like the final sort of deadline that you guys are hoping to complete?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So currently for our timely registration, we have about at least 50% of our contracts already on our PASA primary for registration for compass for center base.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: About 30% are already getting processed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we're confident that we're moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and in the next week or so, our school based awardees will start getting their contracts in the next couple of weeks.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we're doing what we can.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and we're hopeful that by um month or so, all providers will have their contracts and we'll be moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks for the total number of COMPASS applications received, the percentage of providers awarded contracts, and the criteria used to score applicants.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions COMPASS Application Volume and Scoring Criteria

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And what was the final number of applications received for both the center-based and the school-based providers?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm gonna defer it to our youth services team to provide that information.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then, sort of the second part to that question, which I'll just ask is what percentage of the center-based providers who submitted an application received an award.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So the first part is just total number of applications received for both.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think it was roughly 209 center-based proposals.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Forgive me if I'm a little off.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And we had proposals from roughly 3,500, if you consider each of the individual schools.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because we had a community district competition, it was many fewer than that, and we can get back to.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then okay, so 209, and then how many of those that submitted were awarded for the center-based?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Right, 121.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, out of the 209.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And what percentage of school-based providers uh received an award?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So 3,500 applicants, and then it was sorry, what was the number?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We made 806 awards in the school-based competition.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then there were as you said, 3,500 that applied, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: 3,500 individual school proposals, but many fewer if we count them by the community district proposals.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Just to add, sorry, it's for school based, it was 228 providers, unique providers that applied, 147 were awarded.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: For school base.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: For school base.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then what was the criteria used to score the Compass RFP applicant and what was deemed as an unviable score?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So the criteria was organizational experience, service requirement, staffing, community partnership, approach, and budget.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Those were the criteria that we looked at, and the minimum score was a 70.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Minimum was 170, okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I know there's going to be a lot more questions on that, so I will leave it to my colleagues.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks how DYCD is supporting long-standing providers who were not awarded contracts under the competitive COMPASS RFP process.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Support for Non-Awarded COMPASS Providers

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, Chair Stevens?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I will jump right into it, and I know we are like kind of pressed with time, so I just ask that the answers be succinct uh because we want to make sure we get to as many people.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so I'll I'll stay with the compass theme and just kind of just thinking about um just the impact of like organizations, and and as we know, uh these decisions aren't being made easily for you guys, but just thinking about um because of the these this this RFP, we know that some of these organizations that didn't get contracts, this is actually going to be a huge impact on their agencies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I'm wanted to know how is the agencies responding.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, how are you guys responding to the providers?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What supports have you been giving them?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What what has this what has this transition been looking like, especially for those we know that is going to have big impacts where they didn't get any awards?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we're we're in the protest phase for the school base, but so I'm gonna speak more generally.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: First, I want to say that the non-awardees, I want to acknowledge that they have financial and operational impacts that are gonna force them to make really difficult decisions, and we don't take that lightly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, and as I mentioned before, and this was a procurement process that followed all of the guidelines of procurement uh law, and yet uh we are gonna try to stand in the gap for these providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're conducting briefings for them.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We've extended based on their feedback the protest uh uh period so that everybody can get a briefing before the date before they send a protest letter, but also we are trying to figure out um about having conversations about what this is gonna look like and if there are other things that we could do to support them, and then really leaning into the transition um periods and figuring out what does transition look like from summer to September.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How do we help transition providers in ways that hold their dignity intact while entering new providers and making the way for good providers to also stand in that gap and support children, youth, and families?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.
