chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about the close-out process for Summer Rising and how DYCD is coordinating transitions between outgoing and incoming CBOs.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Inquires About Summer Rising Close-Out and Transition

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And just even thinking about the summer, because I know you brought up summer.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So as we're thinking about Summarizing, just going to record again, everybody knows I don't like that program.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Anyway, I just always have to say it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But um anyway, wanting to think about the providers who would be running and operating Summerizing and then not having a whole site going back and moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um what's the plan around what?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Does DYCD plan to support those providers who programs are closing out like right after that?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And and also just kind of considering what the morale for some of these programs are going to be for this summer and closing out.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I I think it uh thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh it's a good question because there's two parts to this, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: There is the contracting close out process, but we want to make this process as humane as possible.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So our teams will be coordinating with those CBOs to have conversations on the process itself from the contracting side from the financial side, and also that transition between CBO to CBO, uh, coordinating general transition um meetings between CBOs, connecting with school leaders and community to again really emphasize like this is not just about the contract, it's about the people on the site as well.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks how DYCD is preparing schools, families, and local council members for the transition to new COMPASS providers in the fall.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Transition Details and Council Member Engagement

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, could you talk to me a little bit about what the transition processes look like?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I know um that's one of my biggest things.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Like, like how are we working with organizations on this transition process and what is that gonna look like?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, you know, especially where you know there's cases where they might not have gotten that site, but they have another site, and so yes, we would love for those folks who work in the program to stay, but if some people are gonna go to the new sites, then I just think like this reshuffling is going to cause a little bit of a chaos.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so just trying to understand like how are we preparing um not only the providers but schools and families around this transition and what it's gonna look like.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Do we have like documents?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Are we like working on like toolkits?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and then also how will we be involving the council members who I know a lot of them are already getting a lot of calls and pushback and want to make sure that the local council members are part of those conversations with these transition processes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So uh that's a great uh segue because part of this process is a real coordinated effort between all those stakeholders for again uh the best transition as possible because the timeline is difficult and there's a lot of concerns about incoming change, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So the first part is there's core components of the plan that focus on transition meetings between all the different stakeholders, um, knowledge transfer of the transition meetings already start to take place, yeah, starting to take place, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're you know, also keeping in mind that we are in a process and appeal period, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So trying to make sure that we're coordinating all those uh engagements as well, being thoughtful about them in that process uh and in a respectful way, thinking about you know what staff retention may look like, connecting with the school leaders, um, family and school communication and coordinating with New York City Public Schools centrally and doing district level engagement and principal level engagement because some instances it's a good change that they're really anticipating for and they've been working for a long time towards, and then what our role is really kind of that central point to lift and escalate if there are some challenging conversations along the way and what we'll be tracking along that path.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So one thing that we already started is engaging with those new CBOs and welcoming them in and really thinking about like readiness check.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Where are they as an organization?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Where are they in terms of the staffing supports, coordination, what types of activities are looking towards doing, connecting them with the school leader?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So every single principal as part of the announcement was provided as who the CBO was and the contact information and coordinating with public schools where there are some instances where there's some disconnect.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So that's what we want to ensure throughout the and I know it is a is a fast turnaround time, but again, um I just want to make sure that we are tapping in um the local council members to be a part of those conversations so that when we so we could all be on the same page because it starts to get disconjoined when you have parents reaching out and the schools and all these things.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I want to make sure that local council members are part of the conversations and being brought in to be as supportive as possible with these transitions because I think you know a lot of folks are not happy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and you know, we are in a place where some of these decisions are final, and so thinking about how do we work together to get not only the communities acclimated but also the council members with some of these new organizations that will be coming into their communities.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks how DYCD will determine the location of the 5,000 additional COMPASS slots planned for fall 2027 and their impact on existing programs.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Location of Additional COMPASS Slots

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This is a community effort, so all stakeholders involved.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If um, you know, we'll definitely be connecting with you guys, and if there's any questions or flags, feel like absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, so according to the APS School Expansion Plan, 5,000 additional compass slots will be added in fall 2027 or fiscal 2028, in addition to the slots are in this RFP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How will you determine where those slots will be located?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What is the timeline to determining providers and beginning those slots?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, we're in the process of making strategic decisions about how to leverage that those slots, specifically to continue to stabilize the ecosystem and strengthening it as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Will there be an impact on providers who are currently providing after school program services?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thinking about as you're thinking about those expansions and even thinking about some of the mitigating factors now, how are we looking to leverage some of the losses that we have now, like moving forward?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is there going to be another RFP?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Like, what does that look like?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I would say in way we are gonna move in ways that honor the current procurement uh process, but also that we are gonna be opportunistic about ways to leverage um uh the current slots or the future slots in ways that help to mitigate some of the impact.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens questions the appeal process for center-based providers and expresses concern over the potential closure of vital community centers.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Center-Based Appeal Process and Closures

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Post-award announcement there are 10-day appeal process with the submission date of April 24th for the Center based providers that may not have been viable to receive awards.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Has there been any changes in this appeal process?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh we extended the appeal process, I believe, by two weeks in order to have uh providers be able to receive a briefing before they made their appeal.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and I know with like the the community base, it's it's it's a little different than it is for the um the school base, and and one of my biggest issues with the community base was that it was we were going to be closing programs, and in a time where we are seeing young people having lack of programs, I'm like, well, why would we be closing any programs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, so can we talk about what that process was?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I know um you guys have been really thoughtful about thinking about creative ways of like trying to um work with different providers, but like especially with community-based, I think, in a moment of trying to close any center in this moment sounds crazy to me, especially going into the summer.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I'm just trying to get a better understanding where we are with that and how do we move forward.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, we're in the process of looking at the impact and making strategic decisions about how we move forward because we're in the process of uh investigating that right now and looking into it deeply.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I I don't have a formal strategy to share right now.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Has there been any additional funding for OMB to um fill the gaps for these community centers?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think that we're in ongoing conversations that the commissioner just mentioned, um, recognizing the impact of the most recent awards, and we are looking into how do we can appropriate um respond to the concerns of all stakeholders.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so we're on ongoing conversations with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, I'll I'll I'll join those ongoing conversations because again, I I just wanna just say, like, you know, community-based everybody knows community-based programs have a special place in my heart, and that's because they do a different type of work, and the same students that show up at a school-based program typically does not show up at uh a community center, and those are the those are a lot of times are the kids we need to be reaching, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And this the community center staff are the ones that are typically out from the community, out on the ground and in there, and so for me in this moment, these community-based programs has to be a priority, and that has been one of the biggest things that I've been pushing for.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I think like as we're thinking about where we need to save, and I and I and I understand that savings, we cannot do it on the back of young people, and that is one thing that I'm just not okay with.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I've been saying this, my theme today is like if we're gonna have an affordable city, it needs to be affordable for young people.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And these are the things that we need, especially when we had ACS before you guys, and we have 362 young people in secure detention currently.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And that is abysmal because when I got into office, we were at 150.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so those numbers have tripled at this point.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And closing community centers just does not sound like that is something we should be doing in this moment.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I'm gonna continue to advocate and push because community-based programs need to be open, and we should be expanding that work and not um and not closing any programs.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about the appeal process for school-based providers and how DYCD is working to address the 12 schools that did not receive any viable proposals.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions School-Based Appeal Process and Capacity

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I have a couple of questions about the appeal process for the school-based providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um that um is the appeal process the same for the school-based providers?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um that they may um, if they were not deemed viable, do the is it still 10 days as well for them as well?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, so anyone that was not selected for an award, whether center base or school base has the right to appeal.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And it's the same process as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Did and was there an extension for them as well?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Well, for center base, there wasn't an extension for the school base.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We gave the extension of the volume.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And a question, even around the contracts.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Has every contract been awarded already?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So for school base, the majority of the contracts were awarded.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: There were 12 schools right now that currently were not awarded that we're working on on getting uh what is that process look like and why weren't they awarded?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, with that within that competition, there was either no viable proposal or no one was submitted uh proposal for that particular school.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we're right now in conversations to finalize the plan for the 12th.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so, what does that plan look like and how is that gonna work?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is that is it gonna go out to procure is it gonna go back out to procurement?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Are we looking at some of the providers that are already in the pool?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm just trying to get an understanding of like how do we then like what is the mitigation for that looks like?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's still under review for consideration because our ultimate priority is to ensure that services are happening for the fall, we want to make sure that there's no laps, so we are looking to see what's the best interest of the city to get services started in the fall.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, because I I just want to go on record for saying there was a lot of providers who got multiple contracts, and so if they are the people getting additional contracts, I'm going to be upset because I think capacity is one of the things that you know we know that, and I we know that a lot of these folks will say they have the capacity, then when it's time to produce, it starts to get warded down.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I want us to really look at some of these groups who might not have like been able to get awarded because there was a lot of competition in certain areas and things like that to kind of like mitigate that, but thinking about capacity and being able to trying to keep some of these providers whole should and I would like to see that be a priority.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks how DYCD is assessing and supporting new providers who do not currently hold School-Age Child Care (SACC) licenses from DOHMH.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions School-Age Child Care Licensing

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um how's the agency assessing providers who do not have school age child care licenses?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because that's another question, especially for the new providers that's coming in that's gonna be in these places.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um about uh when the RFP came out was just like, are we giving additional supports to DOHMH?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because they are a huge part in this process that we kind of forget about.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so I'm trying to understand, especially with these new programs coming in, how do we expect to get all these people cleared?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because even if you were cleared at another site, you gotta do the process over.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And with the SAC requirements, I'm really concerned about folks being able to start and being able to get um up and going by what is August?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What is it?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: September.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: September.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That's a great question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, at the start of the RP planning process, we've been in full engagement with the OHMH and New York City Public Schools because there's a whole coordinated process that happens behind the scenes outside of the application process, which includes inspections, the facilities itself.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we wanted to really focus on the more trouble spots areas and then on the application side work with those new and existing CBOs to ensure that they are fully understanding the process itself, that we have support and technical assistance that we can provide on our end as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What's been great is that there's only a small subset of these programs starting the fall that actually require a new license itself.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we're uh right now we're looking at just over 200.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So that's a share.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And again, it's it's also you said 200, like it was like 10.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But it's it's but let's let's be clear 200 out of the total 900 plus programs we have.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We've done this in the past when we did the sonic expansion.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I and I was there and it was not smooth.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It was it was not smooth.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Smoother, and right and I was there, it was not smooth, and it was a mess.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And you're 100% correct on that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And what's been great since then is that there actually is an online portal, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We had to go from paper-based to online since then.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So there's been a lot of progress.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And that is the process of what we do here at DYCD to make sure that we can make those troublesome spots as comfortable as possible, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think this is an opportunity for us to really think about these processes across multiple agencies and how we're tracking them and ensuring that we are escalating as we speak right now.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have these teams coordinating and meeting to discuss where um additional supports are going to be needed and really coordinating with public schools that identifying the classrooms so the OHMH can go out and do the inspections in a quick manner and engaging with CBOs that still need support in that process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is there a consensus plan if they are not able to get their SAC license and up and running?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, there will be plans in place to do that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So you have that plan already?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, so you'll share that with me.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: All right, great.

chapters.summary: Council Member Christopher Marte raises concerns on behalf of parents and principals regarding the discontinuation of Manhattan Youth's after-school programs at several local schools.
chapters.title: Council Member Marte Questions Discontinuation of Manhattan Youth

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, can the agency provide the city council with a list of current school-based providers who did not um get awards a newer RFP, a list of school-based programs who did not get awarded the new RFP with the old funded um amount lines, um, a list of non-viable center-based providers, and a list of DYCD um funding groups for both center-based and school-based awards and their amounts.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You don't have to go through it if you guys can just get back to us.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, we can spot it from.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I have a lot of questions, but I'm gonna pass it off.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I I do I didn't even get to any of the other sections, so I'll be back.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: She will be back for round two.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so we're gonna move to council member questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh councilmember Maloney, followed by Deputy Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, on behalf of countless parents and principals across my district, and you're gonna be hearing from other districts in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I have concerns about the new Compass RFP and in particular Manhattan Youth, which was discontinued at some schools but not all schools, with limited transparency about why and limited engagement with the parents and the principals on the ground to council member Stevens's point earlier.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This is a program that had a 94% attendance rate, positive audits, and was recommended uh first by many principals.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So the question is why why were they deemed insufficient?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And if their program truly was insufficient, then why were they awarded for some contracts and not others, including a case where there's a co-located school where uh Manhattan Youth was awarded for one school and not the other?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I think we get to say that you know, I want to speak more broadly about the selection process, and I'm gonna hand it over to Dana.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But I do want to talk about this in terms of transparency and the narratives that are out there.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This was a procurement process that was highly transparent.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The concept paper was co-developed by the field.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And there, in any procurement, there are winners and non-winners.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And my heart goes out to all of the agencies that were not awarded.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But I am really proud of the rigorous process that we engaged in and the level of care that DYCD took in making these awards.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I could I I want to speak about that, uh, but I also want to give you the technical answer, which I'm gonna hand over to Dana.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I also want to say that every single competition in every single region was competing with other providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so they were ranked in that geographical area accordingly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So there were some providers that in some areas they were rewarded, awarded their specific, uh they were re awarded, and in other areas they were not.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And and I can tell you on the provider side that that is as someone who's been a provider that is devastating, and yet it is part of the procurement process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think the thing I want to highlight, because this has not been procured for over 10 years, there is a sense of ownership that I think is really felt in terms of relationships, in terms of continuity, and yet what we know to be true is that procurement is necessary because it allows for innovation, for equity, and for accountability.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I think more than one thing could be true about this process, but again, I I share the deep uh empathy and regard for the staff for the families and the children youth that uh that are going to have a new provider.

chapters.summary: Council Member Christopher Marte questions the transparency of the COMPASS scoring process and asks why principal rankings were seemingly overruled in certain school competitions.
chapters.title: Council Member Marte Questions COMPASS Procurement Transparency

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But I'm also really excited about the providers that were selected that are high quality, high caliber, and that offer um really great services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, to add, I definitely want to push back a little on the fact that the procurement wasn't transparent, it was extremely transparent.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We had made selections based on a basis for award where there was criteria outlined on how we were making the awards.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, in the case of Manhattan Youth, they did apply to multiple competitions, and each competition is handled on its own merit.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So you could have one school which was considered a competition be more competitive than others, and we made selections based on where scores, ranking, provided diversity.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: There were many um areas that we looked at when making those selections, but it stood behind the basis for award that was released in the RFP, and that was a procurement document that was made public.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: One of them was was around budget.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can you share more details on how Manhattan Youth scored against those criteria?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: At this time, we can't give specifics because we are in the protest period.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And also they will be awarded a briefing, so they will get not only their scores and how they fared in relationship to other people, but they will get also specific feedback on their RFP of areas of that were highlighted by the evaluator.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And are there instances where a school and a uh principal did rank Manhattan Youth as excellent and it was still not awarded again?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And if if that's the case, there's a process where the principals on the ground are being overruled in a closed-door process, if you could speak to that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It wasn't a closed-door process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It was actually a transparent process that was guided by a contribution that was made by the principal.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So print principals selections was weighed, but the primary selections, as Dana mentioned, were the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so I'm proud to say that over 80% of principals got their first or second award.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And with principals that did not get their first ranking, we're gonna be working to help that transition on the ground to really think about ways to be a bridge and to make sure that they are centering this choice around children, youth, and families.

chapters.summary: Council Member Christopher Marte asks what recourse is available if new providers fail to meet community needs and requests a detailed debriefing on the scoring of local programs.
chapters.title: Council Member Marte Inquires About Recourse and Transition Support

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And my last question, I know I'm out of time here, is if there's a chance to re-evaluate these decisions, and we're signing up for a six-year RFP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So if these programs are not meeting the needs of the community, for example, uh in this case, there are scopes of services like sports, arts, or academic enrichment that the prior provider did offer that the new provider does not, which can be very disruptive for the youth and the families, and that's who I'm representing today.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What's the recourse on the line?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I want to say two things.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The first is that I know how resilient young people are and how adaptable they are to change.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I also know how uh responsive providers are to children, youth, and families, and how they will tailor their services to meet the needs of young people.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, it is a new process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, there will be an on-ramp, but I believe that at the end of this that there will be responsive services that center young people, and yet if they do not, like any other process at DYCD, we will take the necessary course of action.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um just really quickly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I know you guys said that you're gonna be doing a briefing with the providers um and a debrief around the folks who weren't viable.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can you please make sure you share that information with us um and also let us know when that's happening so um we can possibly participate in support?

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Nantasha Williams asks if any of the goals identified in DYCD's preliminary racial equity plan require specific budgetary actions or allocations.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams Questions DYCD on Racial Equity Plan Budget

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, deputy speaker, followed by council member Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um thank you, Chair Oh wait, uh oh, Deputy Speaker first, followed by thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I'm gonna ask some questions about the racial equity plans.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um recently the administration released a preliminary report on the plan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The plan included input from many city agencies, including your own.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um could you just share with us if any of the goals that you have identified have any um budgetary um actions?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um tell the truth, the whole truth and nothing but the truth before this committee and respond honestly to council member questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm Denise Ramirez and I serve as the chief equity inclusion officer here at DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, yes, there are budget implications for our uh goals and our strategies, but the way we've approached this is to incorporate it into our operations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um we have a goal on capacity building, that's part of the budget for this year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have the equity methodology, which is will be integrated into the RFPs as we move forward.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it is integrated into how we approach the work.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Nantasha Williams questions why it will take until 2034 to fully apply the equitable investment methodology to all city tax levy-funded programs.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams Questions Equitable Investment Methodology Timeline

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm happy you mentioned the um the methodology.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So in 2021, you begin developing an equitable investment methodology that prioritizes communities with the highest concentrations of poverty.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: However, the city's racial equity plan states that this methodology will not be fully applied to all city tax levy funded programs until 2034.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Why will it take more than a decade to fully implement this approach and what barriers are preventing faster implementation?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The timeline of 2034 is to uh align with what we expect to be the cycle of RFPs coming um online.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I will say I think the the hurdles are what we've been talking about is any time that we are implementing change, um, it will take time to um iterate, adapt, and integrate um you know the methodology.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it's part of the process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I just think that's very long to do, especially considering you guys started this in 2021.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so one of your long term goals is to apply this investment um methodology to 100% of the funded programs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What percentage of the city tax levy funded programs currently use this methodology?

chapters.summary: Deputy Speaker Nantasha Williams asks how the equitable investment methodology weighs poverty, demographics, and community violence indicators when allocating program slots.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams Inquires About Methodology Data Metrics

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It was applied to the literacy RFP in 2024.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And an equity approach was applied to the COMPASS RFP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So those are the two so far.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can you explain how this methodology weighs poverty data, demographic data, community violence indicators, and existing service gaps when allocating program slots?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The primary uh driver for the methodology is poverty, um, looking at poverty data.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So you don't use any other metrics of data, only poverty?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's the driver.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So poverty makes up at least 70%.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um in some cases, like the illiteracy RFP, we took into account the program specific needs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So looking at data on uh limited English proficiency or looking at data on adults without a high school diploma.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we will look at other factors, but poverty is the primary driver.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But when do you look at other factors?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, yes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: No, but I'm saying when.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: When do you say let's look at other factors?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As part of the process, so um we'll look at in the compass RP, we looked at um students with disabilities, students in temporary housing, we looked at English language learners.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, as part of that process, we also looked at the priority precincts with the highest levels of gun violence.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, as part of that process, we will look at that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, so you do look at other data metrics, but primarily poverty.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, um, and just the last question will DYCD publicly release the methodology and underlying data used to determine which neighborhoods receive priority investments?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So one of the strategies that we included in this long-term goal is to conduct outreach and engagement to get input on the methodology.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So as part of that process, we will be sharing as we do that outreach, sharing what's been done and soliciting feedback.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Harvey Epstein asks how DYCD plans to support center-based providers like Henry Street Settlement that lost funding, ensuring families do not lose critical services.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein Questions Support for Henry Street Settlement

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um Councilmember Epstein, followed by Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I just want to focus first focus on center-based providers that lost their funding.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I know we've talked a lot about this, especially, you know, Henry Street in my district.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's got a hundred and fifty-year history providing services, has a boys and girls republic center that now you know what how are we going to make them whole to ensure families who have been relying on that place for decades are gonna be able to have services for their children there?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're in the process of evaluating a strategy to really think about strengthening stability and continuity, and so we're in the thick of that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So what's our timeline to get to for them to hear back from you?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I believe that the protesting period ends um June 4th.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, June 4th, and everybody gets a briefing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thereafter, we hope to be able to um identify kind of some next steps.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, because they have staff, and so they have staffing needs and they're making some decisions we need to be able to do that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then families also have to make decisions based on that as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So you think soon after June 5th, you'll be able to get back to them?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Absolutely.

chapters.summary: Council Member Harvey Epstein questions why parent input and satisfaction were not directly factored into the COMPASS RFP evaluation process alongside principal rankings.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein Questions Parent Engagement in COMPASS RFP

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, and just just on the other providers, the school-based providers, uh, I want to follow up with uh Councilmember Maloney.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In some of these determinations, yeah, how much did family input play into this factor?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How much did principal input play in?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How much did services like someone has a sports program, someone doesn't have a sports program?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Like all those factors, cost, how are these all factored in?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because you know, from the families we're hearing in hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of families, you know, they were very happy with the provider.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I understand that you went through a process and I respect that, but it feels like they're not feeling like their input has been heard.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Dana, would you like to so as part of the procurement process, the principals did have an opportunity to weigh in through their ranking?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and when we made those selections, we did take other factors into consideration.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The parents at the time of the RFP, that was not taken into consideration.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: However, that will be done as part of the school partnership agreement process when we're bringing the new providers in.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That's when the opportunity for the parents to speak about what programming they want to ensure that the new provider is um providing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I appreciate that, but I don't I'm not sure why after the fact you would take parents' consideration in, like why not have that involved in in the application process and parent engagement and are they happy?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I appreciate the principals and the three schools that lost Manhattan Youth in my district, all rate rated rank them as number one.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So they've already said they want them to continue.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The parents said they want them to continue, but not really listening to the parents.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The second question I'll ask because I know I'm almost out of time, is you committed to coming out to the school to talk to parents, or your team will be?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We'd like to be able to sketch that set up before the end of the school year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I want to make a commitment now to make sure that happens in the next couple of weeks.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm committed to speak to whoever wants to speak to me about this process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, just on the Yeah, I also just want to add in terms of parent engagement.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We also, prior to the RFP being released, as part of our stakeholder engagement, we did engage families to build that new compass model that we made it into the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So parents were engaged before, but during the actual RFP process, it was really between the school principals and DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I'll just say I think that's a mistake, honestly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think parents are the recipients of these services, and they're the ones to best judge beyond the paper what happens day to day in those schools.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I think taking their consideration out of the mix as a as an instrumental part of what we're doing, I think unfortunately was a mistake, and I think it'll have some consequences in our district, and we're hearing directly from it.

chapters.summary: Council Member Harvey Epstein urges DYCD to facilitate transition meetings with parents and explore ways to retain experienced after-school staff under new providers.
chapters.title: Council Member Epstein Urges Transition Planning and Staff Retention

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I would, you know, would would love to figure that out.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I appreciate your big willingness to come talk to parents and to hear directly from them, but also hearing directly from the community about the jobs and the staffing and making sure people who you know are working for Manhattan Youth, maybe they can, you know, either stay on with the new provider or whatever we can do to fix this problem.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We really do need to fix it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I just I wanted to add uh something to that to the principal engagement and to how extensively through this process the superintendents were involved and the principals were involved, but where we are right now, having just announced these awards and and finalizing the protest period is precisely the time when families and young people and principals and superintendents can express what it is that they love about their current provider and how those programs and services could be implemented in the fall.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What they haven't had that they'd like to see.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We love at DYCD, you won't get any arguments when a school community loves their after school provider, that is like the best thing for us.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And we really appreciate the advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If that energy could be brought to um to the to help plan and coordinate with the new provider, this is what our kids want.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have a whole group who wants theater.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We can't live without soccer here.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can you hire with that in mind?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Like this is the time for the school specific, student-specific activities to be negotiated.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And that's what Daniel and the team are going to be engaging with providers in now.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I hope that the adults working with young people around this will take that opportunity to engage with the new providers to get things off to a good start in the fall.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I I know my time is up, Chair, so I'll come back for a second round.

chapters.summary: Council Member Rita Joseph asks for the total Summer Rising budget and requests an update on outstanding FY25 payments owed to after-school providers.
chapters.title: Council Member Joseph Questions Summer Rising Budget and Outstanding Payments

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I will add you to the list.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, Councilmember Joseph, followed by Councilmember Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I hope young people are also at the table when you're making these decisions and their voices are centered.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, you know what my favorite topic is?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What is the total budget for Summarizing program?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And can you provide the status update on the number of providers for the summarizing program remaining outstanding or not receiving payments for FY25 service?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know the city love to owe people.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How are we doing on that?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Well, hello.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So summarizing our budget for FY27 is uh 151 million.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're grateful that the most recent administration did it based on restoration and the JAN plan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so we know for this upcoming summer that funding is um restored and they'll have continuity of services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um what was your other question?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're hearing from providers that haven't been paid from FY25.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: All of our FY26 amendments, um, inclusive of summarizing, are pretty much registered.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I'll just give you exact number.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But for the most part, um for FY27, 98% of our um registr um amendments associated with all types of um increases to funding um have been moved and about and this is FY25.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They wanted to know why is the delays and providers have been a form of why there's a delay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Do you inform the providers when there's a delay in payments?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What do you say to them?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: For payments, um, usually we inform them that there's a delay, but currently there has not been any substantial delay in payments.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think what they're referencing is some maybe saying they have not received their funding, additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, if that's the case, I ask that they reach out to our help desk and we can look into it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But to my knowledge, all implementations for prior summers for summarising were um included in a release to providers, and so we can always talk offline and let you know who the provider is.

chapters.summary: Council Member Rita Joseph asks what strategies DYCD is implementing to pay providers faster and resolve technical issues with the invoicing help desk.
chapters.title: Council Member Joseph Inquires About Faster Payment Strategies

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um what strategies are being implemented or for paid providers faster in the FY26?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, what is the what is the timeline to get funding out the door right away for providers?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Well, currently, most of our providers, when we get contracts registered, we do a 50% advance to help with cash flow.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: After that, we essentially um do reimbursements based on provider invoice submission.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Now, in full transparency, we have not had as much invoice um submissions, and so that may be impacting the provider's ability to maintain cash flow.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But currently we have less than one percent of invoices under 30 days, and I mean over 30 days, so we know that there's a pretty much fast turnaround for um invoice submissions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But again, if someone is having specific issues, I I encourage you, please reach out and we'll look into it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But right now, about one percent of our invoices are over 30 days, and I think when I came in and looked, it was about three, and they're at 40, and I said work on them.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I know that they're already working on them right now, and so um we're prior to the case.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Wonderful to hear.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And if a provider is having um trouble accessing the system, do you provide technical support for that provider?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, we have a help desk, and a lot of our staff are doing phone calls all the time.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And we work closely with Mox.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, our payment team and budget team have weekly calls with Mox um troubleshooting any issues they're having technically.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And um, if they're providers are having issues, we always um raise those concerns to them during those meetings.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And again, we kind of provide our system.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have a help desk to return to any um respond to any increase.

chapters.summary: Council Member Rita Joseph asks about the number of Summer Rising applications and how DYCD is coordinating with public schools to accommodate students with IEPs.
chapters.title: Council Member Joseph Questions Summer Rising Special Needs Accommodations

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How many students applied for summarizing today?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And what provision are you putting in place for students with special needs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Students with IEPs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm gonna defer to the youth services team to respond to that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So uh this past year we received over 150,000 uh applications.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we're really excited about that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, in regarding um providing supports for youth and disabilities, uh, we've been working with the Dow team in New York City Public Schools to ensure that we have accommodation plans for every single child that is required to receive those services and coordinating with school leadership and the CBOs as well to ensure that all those supports in place for all those services throughout the um program period.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And a student who needs a one-to-one power in summarizing, yes, one will be provided and you will accommodate what's on the IP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Correct, correct?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler raises concerns about the concentration of COMPASS contracts among a small number of large providers, citing a specific case in Williamsburg.
chapters.title: Council Member Restler Questions Concentration of COMPASS Contracts

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you so much to the esteemed and impressive co-chairs of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Really appreciate your great leadership.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and congratulations again, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I really enjoyed sitting down with you some weeks ago and checking in on some of the compass concerns that we've been hearing from our communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And uh, you know, I just think it's a it's a great thing to have somebody with such deep expertise in youth development leading the agency.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um we know there's a really strong bench uh uh supporting you uh at DYCD and look forward to working with you and your team.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh I continue to be concerned about uh kind of the compass process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I I fully appreciate that you know, whenever we put out a new RFP, I mean part of the reason we haven't put out this RFP for 10 years is because they're winners and losers, and it's an incredibly bumpy process to go through.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so the last administration handed you this, you know, um hot potato, I guess I'll call it for the purposes of being diplomatic.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um but you know, as I've shared with you, we have just to give one example, a provider that serves the the Hasidic community in Williamsburg that's been an excellent provider for many, many years.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They had hundreds of seats, they now have dozens, um, and they were the one provider that served that community.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And they scored really well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh I know you're not gonna speak to an individual provider, but I'm gonna just give you the specific context for my district to make this real.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so I think we want to I it would help me to understand like how could one provider score excellently at one location and above the threshold, but not at a different location when it's the same team providing the same excellent services across the board.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler questions how a single provider can be evaluated as excellent at one location but fail to meet the threshold at another nearby site.
chapters.title: Council Member Restler Questions Disparities in Provider Scoring

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So within each competition, there's evaluators that are are reading, and the evaluating team could be different across the board, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it's all objective based on how the provider has demonstrated that they can provide these services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And the other part too is that depending on how to say objective, but isn't that inherently subjective, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Like that is the precise definition of a subjective process is that it's different readers, and some readers are determining that this organization is excellent, and two blocks away where they have a different site, they're determining they're not.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That is, I mean, sorry to interrupt you, but no, no, no, it's fine.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And but I would also just add that it really depends on what other providers competed in that competition, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If if the pro if the program was if sorry, if the competition was extremely competitive, that's where you're gonna see the disparity in the world.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I can appreciate that that you could have different uh that at a given location, some other organization applied and is even more excellent than the group that's been there for 30 years, and they get selected because they did even better.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What I struggle to understand is how one organization can be reviewed as excellent at this location, but at these other locations in the neighborhood where they've been doing an excellent job for 30 years and always scored well on all of their DYCD contracts and all of their performance reviews, how they could be found to have such a different score based on who's reading it, it just that is what really pains me and is paining my community.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I I wanna I appreciate what you're saying, and I do think this is very nuanced.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And what I would say is I just I think there's assumption in some of the questions, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The assumption is that they did poorly in one or in one section and good in another section.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And the truth is that they could have done really well, but there was greater competition in one, and there are more providers that were invited or that came to that proposal.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So if you have 10 in one section and you have five in another, and you rank number seven, and there's six slots, so I wanna just offer that nuance, but I don't want to take away from what you spoke about earlier.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, so it's not like that they that they did poorly, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If we give that example, and then better in another one, it's really that they might have done really well in both, but there was greater competition in the world.

chapters.summary: Council Member Lincoln Restler asks about the threshold scores used to determine provider viability and questions the inclusion of charter schools in the COMPASS RFP.
chapters.title: Council Member Restler Questions Threshold Scores and Charter Schools

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I uh you know, from our conversation, previous conversation, Commissioner, you had explained that there was a threshold level that if organizations did above, then you could try to figure out a path to work with them because they scored well enough on the on the thing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And if they scored below that threshold level, then your hands are kind of tied because they didn't, you know, and you have an object objective, subjective, whatever word we wanna use.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You have a process, it was determined.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um that's what I'm speaking to in my question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is that on some locations they didn't score above that?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm just using this as an example, but there are other providers, and I you know was on a tour this morning of one site with a city agency assistant commissioner who wanted me to ask questions about their school.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So everyone in Manhattan Youth, if Virginia's still here, um, but uh everybody's concerned about I think a similar thing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I just am using this as an example that this organization scores above the threshold, you know, here doesn't across the street, it's confusing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so I I can I ask one more question, or you gonna kill me?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I can't do round two, but I it's okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I will text you the question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's about charter schools getting selected for the Compass RFP, and that is what I'm concerned about.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But I will save that for out the Chair Stevens will ask it in a much more effective way.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Sylvina Brooks Powers, followed by Councilmember Brewer.

chapters.summary: Council Member Selvena Brooks-Powers asks how the $14.8 million increase for the Crisis Management System (CMS) will be allocated to reduce gun violence among youth.
chapters.title: Council Member Brooks-Powers Questions Crisis Management System Funding

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and hello, Commissioner, and to the DYCD team.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The increase of 14.8 million dollars of city funding in fiscal 27 to expand services for the crisis management system program is welcome news.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: My community has seen a surge in gun violence in the last few months, including in a neighboring district where the deputy speaker represents with the tragic shooting of 15-year-old Jaden Pierre in Southeast Queens.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How will this new funding allocation help reduce gun violence, particularly among our youth?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What CMS groups will see additional funding thanks to this increase, and how will the transition of CMS from DYCD to the Office of Community Safety Impact Programming and the city's administration of these programs because we know when it shifted from Mach J to DYCD, it was a whole dynamic there.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then also for my CMS providers, they're often going above and beyond, going beyond the small territory they're responsible for, and is the funding that they have right now is absolutely not sufficient.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And the last question I'll leave you with, and I can repeat whatever you need me to.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: SYEP meets a critical gap for youth to seek gainful employment and stay busy during the summer months.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But we have heard that youth need similar employment opportunities throughout the academic school year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What is DYCD doing to meet this need?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Council Member Selvena Brooks-Powers advocates for expanding CMS to Hamels and asks what DYCD is doing to provide year-round employment opportunities similar to SYEP.
chapters.title: Council Member Brooks-Powers Questions CMS Expansion and Year-Round Youth Jobs

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm gonna start.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you for that question, um, council member.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm gonna start with the transition of ONS from DYCD to the Office of Community Safety, and then I'll pass it over to my colleague Daryl.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, the first thing I want to say is that I am really excited about the deputy mayor uh Renita Francois and her commitment to um really seeing this program through and to making it a priority for the whole city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so the transition is gonna take place in two phases.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The first is the programmatic and strategic transition has already occurred.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it is already reporting to the new commissioner and to the new to the deputy mayor, and that has been seamless, and it has been happening in tandem with the support of my team.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The operational aspects of it, the contracts and some of the operations are gonna be transitioned over the last year, uh over the next year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What I can say is that because we had the experience of transitioning from Mock J to DYCD, we have uh experience in what this could look like in ways that uh really keep programming intact and then do not disrupt services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so we're all committed to doing that and to continue to partner with um the deputy mayor as well as the new commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then Daryl, you can speak uh specifically to the other question.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks about subcontracting limits under the new COMPASS contracts and raises concerns about school principals lacking contact information for new providers.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Questions Subcontracting Limits and Provider Communication

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, and I wouldn't be sweated.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember, and it's great to see you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You too, Darrell.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, so we we are working closely with the Office of Community Safety on the additional investment of 14.8 million for the Office of Neighborhood Safety.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, we're working out details now on both how that's going to be um either amended or procured and which groups that it's going to, but it will support the place-based work, the precision work um that that team is doing on the ground.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So supporting the crisis management system, supporting the wrap around services, support supporting the HFIP hospital program as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I just want to put a plug in for Hamill's an expansion to Hamill's because right now I'm using discretionary funding, which is fine, but they have not been designated an official site.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and so we have King of Kings that is stretching pretty much most of the per peninsula, and then the supplemental funding I give to Queen's Royal Priesthood is like a stopgap.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we need to expand those as especially as the summertime comes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then the question on SYEP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I'm really excited about it because we we agree.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, we have our work learn and grow program, which um was created due to advocacy from the council many years ago.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That program serves 7,000 young people uh who were in SYEP and are able to extend their experiences through the school year, and we've found that like the impact of that program really um like is exponential on top of what they get through SYEBK SYEP because we're able to engage them throughout the school year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're able to offer college credits.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: A lot of the young people who go through work learn and grow have received a college credit, um, over 20,000 of them uh in the past five years, and we have another a thousand people with our learn and earn program, which is also a full-year program.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so I would say we definitely appreciate your advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: There's tremendous, tremendous interest from young people for work learn and grow slots.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and so it's definitely a place that as an agency we're looking to continue investing in and looking at how we can do more.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I hope it is expanded.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: My very first on the books job was summer youth employment program, and then I also worked through the school year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It wasn't back then called what it's called now, but um, it definitely was helpful to get money in my own pocket.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I advocate for that, and I know our amazing chair Stevens does as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Brewer, followed by Wong.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks how DYCD measures improvements in contract processing and how many contract delays are escalated to the Mayor's Office of Nonprofit Services.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Questions Contract Processing Metrics and Escalations

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I appreciate the call yesterday that we discussed the after school programs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I sent you a letter, I assume you got it, um, and we hope that that can be changed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is it true that we could do sub uh contracting if nothing else works?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The contract does allow for 30% subcontracting.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, that's not not everything then.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Also, just so you know, the people, uh principals do not have a contact for the other uh provider.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They don't know how to reach them.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I obviously know, so I made the contact, but they did not have a contact, just so you know.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They contacted you to get a contact.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So um I hope that can be changed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's really causing a lot of challenges.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Also, PS 145 is not happy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They want LEAP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I assume you know that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They also contacted me.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Everybody contacts me.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so they too are upset.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So you've got a lot of upset people, just FYI.
