chapters.summary: Council Member Phil Wong questions how DYCD manages Summer Rising attendance and waitlists when enrolled children fail to show up during the first week.
chapters.title: Council Member Wong Questions Summer Rising Attendance and Waitlists

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, in terms of contracting, um, we work, um, we have wonderful staff, we work constantly, really almost with a lot of agencies in Manhattan, not just in my district.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And the issue is constantly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I wonder what is the benchmarks and what metrics do you use to evaluate whether the contracting process it has improved, and what do those metrics show compared to previous years?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And also, how many do you do you keep track of how many get escalated to the mayor's office of nonprofit services, which is what we do?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We call them and then work with them to try to get contracting addressed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is that something that do you keep track of that?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, so we do work very closely with the Mayor's Office of Contract Services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We meet with them weekly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have our own tracking monitoring system where we are able to track where the contract is within the life cycle that's outside of Passport.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we do have we are able to pull data from our own internal systems as well as from Passport on where things fare in the contracting process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So how do you measure that you're that it's working better?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we have also weekly meetings.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have contracting weeklies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have a program operations team that meets with us to ensure that we're meeting our um regular goals and targets.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Three years.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we do um we are actively monitoring and also working on improving our um lifetime cycle for registration.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, and then um I guess there's a three-year, I remember when the three-year contract process went into effect.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We are hearing that people don't find it as more.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I thought it would really be much better, but they don't feel that it is as productive, even more so than it was in the past.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Do you know how many providers chose not to apply or express confusion about the new model?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I don't have those numbers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can you get them to us?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, we'll have to also work with the mayor's office of contract services because although there are three-year contract term, it still has to go through the cleared list process as well as the annual um application that providers have to file with council.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because they feel like there's not enough communication to so that they understand the process, just so you know.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm I'm getting these because we get everything.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: People contacting us.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Wong, followed by Councilmember Wilson.

chapters.summary: Council Member Phil Wong asks how quickly the Summer Rising waitlist moves and questions the ratio of city to state funding for the Summer Youth Employment Program.
chapters.title: Council Member Wong Inquires About Waitlist Movement and SYEP Funding

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um my question re uh my first question is regards to the attendance for the summer rising.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um okay, so we have over 150,000 applicants uh was submitted from March third to March 27th.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh but my my experience with summarizing is that quite often some kids don't show up and then meanwhile there are other kids in the waiting list, they they're expecting a phone call.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, but who then they never get the call.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So my question is who who does the outreach?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: DYCD or is the is the school's job?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because uh uh when I call to school, I get the run around.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So uh thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, you know, I think it's important to know that this is a collaborative program model between New York City Public Schools and DYCD and ensuring that there are supports and services for all young people.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and also summer is a very different model in itself, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It is not a requirement to show up every single day.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have families that utilize space and weeks and days differently than you would say after school, which is a year-round component.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um another thing is that the wait list is also factored in with other components such as ensuring that we are prioritizing youth um that are special needs, uh students in temporary housing, you know, families that will be able to have opportunity to have those seats that they probably wouldn't have never used before.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And it is an extremely popular model, so we are gonna see robust wait lists in certain areas.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um then, you know, from one year to the next.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So every year we do focus on looking at specific locations and sites where we can open up more programs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And actually this year we actually opened up more programs to kind of lighten the load on wait lists for specific schools.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This past year we opened up 40 new elementary programs, but we know the demand has increased.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But our families in the waiting list are they being contacted and say, okay, we have an opening, please come.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because uh my impression is that they don't get these phone calls.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm I'm glad you asked that question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And as my colleague was mentioning, like every year we listen to feedback and we make improvements.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We had roughly amount the same amount of applications this year as last year, but we made significantly more offers, thousands more offers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: At this time, in comparison to the same time last year, we also have thousands more of those offers accepted.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we're we're above in by the thousands, the numbers that we had um last year, and and the wait list is actually gotten smaller because of those efforts.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So as the commissioner said in her testimony, looking at the attendance patterns, as you mentioned, enrollment patterns, we've been able to get more young people pushed into the program than we ever have before, being able to anticipate some of those trends, and I think we'll see changes to the experience you had last summer.

chapters.summary: Council Member Erik Bottcher asks if DYCD will consider reinstating Manhattan Youth and how the agency plans to address the funding gap left at Hudson Guild in Chelsea.
chapters.title: Council Member Bottcher Questions Manhattan Youth Reinstatement and Hudson Guild Support

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh quite back to my problem my question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If if kids don't show up, say after the first week, should the next person move up?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh is that happening?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's how automatically and does DYCD reaches out or the school reaches out.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh so both the school and CBO have access to the same wait list.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So either school or CBO will be doing some engagement as the list moves.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So if they're not receiving a call, unfortunately, that may mean that the list hasn't moved up to the next person.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, there's also that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, quick question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Quick city or state, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The two hundred and forty-six million for SYEP, is that primarily city funds or states.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's uh primarily city funding, um, with a small allocation of TANEF funding from the state.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because uh I as I recoil SYEP is historically gets state funding or am I wrong here?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The scale of the state funding is very small in comparison to the amount of city tax levy that we have funding the program.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay I see it's it's so successful I think it should the size of SYEB should increase and I haven't seen I have I don't see that happening so please look into that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay Councilmember Wilson followed by Banks.

chapters.summary: Council Member Erik Bottcher asks how DYCD measures educational outcomes for high-need students and how the New Visions portal can help track program impact.
chapters.title: Council Member Bottcher Questions High-Need Student Outcomes and New Visions Portal

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you chairs I would also like to add my voice with that of my colleagues Maloney and Epstein regarding Manhattan youth I've received an overwhelming correspondence to my office over 400 parents and students and teachers concerned about that about the change and you know they received positive audits and built decades long relationships with the school communities and parent communities are going to look to incorporate the same staff or programs that existed so my question is if we're looking to keep what was built by Manhattan youth why switch providers because procurement processes allow for competition which is necessary for the ecosystem of New York City and so while again I want to just say that I appreciate the advocacy and my heart goes out to the children youth and families of those communities I also want to say that I have I have uh confidence that the new provider will be able to meet the needs of the communities as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The CEC uh two in my district also gave put out a resolution asking for them to be reinstated.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And you know, given that response from the parents and and the school board, would would there be any consideration to reinstating uh Manhattan Youth?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, what I would say is that the school procurement process was different than the center-based uh process because the school procurement process allowed for continuity of the slots, the number of seats in a building.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so what is being asked of us is essentially would we give the contract to another provider that won it through the competition?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, unless we found something egregious in the RFP process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And believe me, I am reading a lot of these close calls to make sure.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, but uh but it's really the continuity of the provide of the slots and not the provider.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I I also I want to ask about the CBO selection.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I want to acknowledge that you know Hudson Guild is here with us today, which is a piv a crucial provider in Council District 3 centered in Chelsea, right next to two very important uh NYCHA developments and families that they serve.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, you know, they were a reciprocal of one of these uh the cuts, and I I'm just this leaves a huge gap in in my in the district for after school programming with the provider on the like northern edge.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, that's really that's not really gonna work for for parents and and community and and for the families, especially those in the night in NYCHA that really rely on this.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So how are we gonna deal with that gap?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And how is um Hudson Guild going to make sure that they have the continued resources they need to serve uh Manhattan and my council district?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, I would say that we're in the process of thinking about continuity of services and stability of the ecosystem uh for center-based uh awards, and we are hopeful, as I mentioned earlier, to uh uh to share where we land after June 6th.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can I ask one more question?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh the DY uh DYCD funds uh programs explicitly designed to improve educational outcomes for high need students.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um what are the challenges DYCD faces in understanding whether those programs are reaching the students who need them the most and whether they're working and uh how can the uh the new visions portal potentially help address those changes?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I want to understand more which programs you're talking about at GYCD.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Are you talking about?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I mean, on the on the one hand, all of our programs are designed to help young people to reach their goals and to thrive.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I'm not sure which particular program you're talking about.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So let me know.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But I can answer a portion of that, and I think Valerie, if you can chime in as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think one of the things that New Visions offers, the portal is access to data that community-based organizations would ordinarily not have to assess impact.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Right now, currently, all of our contracts are designed to um check enrollment and participation, but with the support of uh New Visions for Public Schools portals, we're able to look at um how the that correlates to academic gains.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, so um I oversee our workforce program, so primarily young people between the ages of 14 and 24.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and we have been in really amazing conversations with the New Visions team about way we can leverage their tool because we are always looking at ways to demonstrate like the specific impacts, especially of the Summer Youth Employment Program and Work Learn Grow, which I talked about a little earlier.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so we've been looking at things, we already do this, but we've been looking at expanding like uh for a school.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can they see a young person's participation in summer youth employment programs so that they can take that into consideration as they're developing what a high school experience is going to look like for that young person?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then in return, can we look at how that young person did throughout the program now that we have that data in that portal and can see comprehensively how their experience was in a public school?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so I'm really proud over the last many years we've been building that partnership with public schools, um, and we're now in conversation with New Visions about how we can leverage their tool.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I just want to add that that's super critical in terms of allowing providers to use data to inform their practice, and so we are hopeful to continue to be working with them and expand our work with New Visions in the coming years.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Great Councilmember Rings.

chapters.summary: Council Member Kamillah Hanks questions the concentration of COMPASS contracts among large providers and asks what capacity-building supports exist for smaller community-based organizations.
chapters.title: Council Member Hanks Questions COMPASS Contract Concentration and Small CBO Support

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Well, good to see you again, Commissioner, and to your team.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, one of the uh obviously topics of discussion and contention has been the uh COMPAS program or RFP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, one of the major concerns raised following the most recent COMPASS RFP process involves the uh concentration of contracts amongst uh smaller number of large providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In some instances, a single organization reportedly oversees uh north of 80 schools or sites under the DYCD funded COMPAS program.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What is the agency's rationale for following or you know, for allowing one provider to manage such a large portfolio of school-based contracts so at time award selection we really did take that into consideration um the provider that you're referencing they did submit a lot of proposals across the entire procurement um and weren't selected for everything that they um proposed for and they do make up only 10% of all the awards that were made we did um not award them in multiple competitions just to ensure that we were reaching other but that's a that's a large concentration of contracts to one organization.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They are an existing provider and when we were making rewards we wanted to ensure that we were making them thoughtful and not um overgrowing the the system and um we really did make an effort to ensure that we were um reaching other providers within the different competitions so well that particular providers actually the providers actually uh they received contracts in I mean areas that they have no track record in as far as community relations and they so how how do you rationalize that yeah within their response for the RFP they demonstrated to be the highest proposer within those competitions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But I think I think your point is well taken and I want to I want to also just say there's 52 new providers as a result of that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so in prior iterations we had higher concentrations of what you're speaking of and this has really opened up the aperture and created a ecosystems that's more balanced that that takes into account small, medium, and large organizations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Well, many of the uh smaller community-based organizations feel that they are being shut out of the opportunities within the compass uh ecosystem despite years of service and strong relationships within their communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What opportunities currently exists for the smaller nonprofits to grow within the compass, and um and and what opportunity and uh broader DYCD and the broader DYCD uh ecosystem and complete uh compete for additional sites or contracts over time, including programs such as Saturday Night, uh Saturday Night Lights and other DYCD funded uh active initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What are being done to support those groups or give them opportunities?

chapters.summary: Council Member Kamillah Hanks urges DYCD to improve direct communication with small nonprofits regarding discretionary contract processing and missing documentation.
chapters.title: Council Member Hanks Urges Proactive Communication on Discretionary Allocations

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As the commissioner mentioned, we have 52 new providers as a result of this compass RFP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and in terms of small CBOs, 17, which are considered those that have less than 100,000 um in revenue.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We do have a whole host of capacity building services to help those providers along the way as they enter into the compass uh portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um in terms of new procurements at this time, we don't have anything um for them to apply to, but they do have the opportunities also to serve as subcontractors that help them get into these compass programs and start building their ability to I have one there's one organization that's been in the school for close to 10 years or so long, long track record in that community, and they've been surviving off of cost of monies that we've given them, um, and then whatever other monies that they've raised, they competed for a compass uh or you know, they submitted for the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and and they're actually they got approved that the principal supported them.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They the consideration was given, but they didn't get the contract.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and and and and that to me means something because having a relationship with the community, having a relationship with the students means a lot.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I know uh the commissioner uh had mentioned uh earlier, I think it was a commissioner, uh, the need uh to support those new organizations that are coming in and figure out ways that you know they can uh the community can now instruct them or give them ideas of things that they want to see.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But a relationship is important, and and and when that is that chemistry is broken up, it can do damage to the community.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I know you you're looking at uh you know uh numbers, you have your scorecards, but those relationships mean a lot.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And um, I don't want I don't want our community to be uh negatively impacted because of uh, you know, a score chart uh that really doesn't take into the the human side of the impact of those relationships.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh so uh I know this is ongoing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I know a lot of these organizations have put in appeals, um, and we're hoping that strong consideration is taken, um uh given uh to those organizations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, council member.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And can I just ask one more question, Madam Chair?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Very quickly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This is and then I'll pass on those other questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You thank you for your indulgence.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks what steps DYCD is taking to prevent debit card fraud and ensure timely payments for Summer Youth Employment Program participants.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions SYEP Debit Card Fraud Prevention

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh when it comes to contract processing and staff reimbursement, this has been a major issue with a lot of the uh discretionary allocations to a lot of the small organizations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh what is being done?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, you know, my office, I think every council office acts as a medium between uh the DYCD and those organizations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But there are many a times when DYCD should just directly call those organizations if they're issues that occur or documents are missing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, there's sometimes there's like a major lapse between communications between DYC and those organizations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What we want to do is see how we can strengthen that with DYCD is directly, it's being more proactive and reaching out to those organizations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, trust me, my office, we can be dealing with a whole host of other issues uh instead of having to be this medium or this go-between between NYGEN and nonprofit.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So if we could improve that, and that would go a long way.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I'm sure the commissioner will take that into consideration and add that to the million list of things to do.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I I'm just gonna do a quick lightning round around some things uh because I know we do have to wrap very quickly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: My my first question is just around SYP.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Hey, Val, there you go.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, just thinking about as we prepare into the summer and go into the summer and thinking about the issue we had last summer with the debit cards and things like that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I just want to get a clear understanding of how are we moving forward this summer to ensure that that doesn't take place again and ensuring that um you know young people are getting paid in a timely manner and and uh and then other things are happening succinctly I can assure you that the team at DYCD is doing everything within our control to prevent it from happening again.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And the short I mean, I can talk about this for a very long time because it's been our focus since the day it happened last year, but high level, we're doing everything from engaging young people in more and more financial literacy training.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Our providers have been convened at DYCD to make sure they're focused on educating their young people on the risks of participating in fraud, and we're working with the banks, the law department, DOI, uh, the the payroll processor, and all of those folks to understand what they're doing to prevent it from happening too.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I know we have been encompassed crazy, but I will call you so we can have a deeper briefing to go over uh how do we prevent and what does that look like moving forward?

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks if DYCD is discussing the $20 million Education for Integration and Equity proposal with OMB to support immigrant adult literacy students.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Adult Literacy Funding and Integration Proposal

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, really quickly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, this might be Bob over here, so get ready.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This is adult literacy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, just have some questions around there.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I know who works for what.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The New York City Coalition of Adult Um Literacy has responded to the threats with a 20 million proposal for the mayoral administrators to create a new fund um stream for immigrant adult literacy students called the education for integration equality.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Are those conversations with OMB um to add this new funding stream into the adopted budget?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Have you guys started having those conversations?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh, we didn't swear them in.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Bobba, for being honest.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You may get I do, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm sorry, could you restate the last part of the I was asking um, you know, the adult coalition has been asking about this 20 million dollars proposed for additional funding for um for uh um immigrant adult literacy students?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um have you guys started to have those conversations with OMB already around this funding?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh uh there hasn't been a pointed conversation with OMB at this time, as you're aware the administration had to deal with a significant uh budget crisis, but we are having internal conversations and talking with LAC about the nature of the proposal.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The adult literacy um forward initiative is currently funded by council, which was created in partnership with the um expertise of providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It focuses on meeting the community needs with the with an innovation and holistic approach to adult literacy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is DYCD open to adopting aspects of this program for DYCD's RFP adult literacy program?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're happy to continue to support the council's pilot.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As you'll recall.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: There was a prior um iteration before adult literacy Forward.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And for our part, we believe that we learned from that model as well as a pilot that we did.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That's why we cre increased uh to a significant sent the price per participant under the current literacy model.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we currently like and abide by the model that we have, and we'd be happy to continue to receive support from the councils we have in prior years to expand the number of slots made available under the model.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks for the current budget and borough-wide provider distribution for the DYCD Fatherhood Initiative.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Inquires About Fatherhood Initiative Budget and Providers

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um the next question I have is just around the father's initiative.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What is the current budget for the father initiative?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We did the budget.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh deferred to um Navita to answer that question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And who were the providers that received the awards?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And what boroughs and districts?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Chair Stevens, I don't have all that detail for that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh, okay, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, but for the budget, I can tell you that the budget for fatherhood at FY27.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Sorry, is 8.2 million.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In terms of the providers, uh, there are 14 providers operating 17 contracts, six are in the Bronx, four in Brooklyn, three in Manhattan, two in Queens, and two in Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We can follow up with later with you after the Oh, don't you worry?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You guys are gonna be following up a whole lot because I still have a whole bunch of questions I'm gonna get through, and I'm trying to be really quickly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They told me I got into 215, so I have one more question.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about the actual spending and distribution tracking of the $11 million budgeted for OMNY cards for youth program participants.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions OMNY Card Spending and Distribution

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It was a it's about these dog on oh, here it goes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, so my next question is just around um what is the actual spending for uh the Omni cards um budgeted at 11 million for the army cards and how many providers and you've received the cards?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh, I'm sorry, I should have had you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We were really trying not to do uh shirt like the shuffling, it didn't work out the way we can you repeat that if you're asking about um what was the actual what was this actual spending for FY26 budgeted for the 11 million for the omni cards?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How many providers and you've received those cards and does the agency receives any reports from providers on a distribution of those cards?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I was I will let um Valerie speak more to the distribution and providers and receive, but I can tell you that in last year we um spent about $4 million towards the OmniCards.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, and that funding went to uh about 80,000 cards.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Participants need multiple cards to get through the summer, so it was for about 40 participants, 40,000 participants that we purchased cards for, and they were distributed to 90 providers, and we have very tight policies around the providers' responsibility of tracking which participants get the cars, how many cars they've distributed, where they store them, and they report all of that back to us on a regular basis, and then we use that information to take into account the need uh for each following program cycle.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um this is wait.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh, it's 216.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'll stop.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, so we are short for time and I don't want to go over any more, but we I still have a ton of questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We'll be sending them over to you guys.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We want to make sure that we know that we're going into negotiations, but please do not let that hold up getting these questions because we really want to get through it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and so we'll be sending them over as soon as possible.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I know there was even colleagues who have questions, so we'll be sending those over, and please make sure that we're working in tandem to get this done and not let negotiations get in the way of that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, thank you guys for being here today, and I'll pass it back over to Cherry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I mean, you basically said it all.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Very efficient time.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I just want to thank you all again for being such great partners.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, I think thank you so much.

utterances.text: Um, okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So good afternoon, and welcome to today's third hearing uh about the uh FY27 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, Councilmember Linda Lee, and I'm glad to be joined today.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh, I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Glad to be joined today by my colleague, uh, Chair Elsie Encarnacion, uh, Chair of the Committee on Immigration.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and we have been joined by our Deputy Speaker Williams, Councilmember Brewer, Councilmember Joseph, Councilmember Wong, and Councilmember Aviles.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, welcome to a familiar face, Commissioner Faiza Ali and your team.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: On May twelfth, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY26 to 30 with a proposed FY27 budget of 124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs proposed fiscal 27 budget of $781,962 represents.00063.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I feel like that's important to note with such a sizable immigration immigrant population in our city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But of the administration's proposed FY27 budget and the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And this remains unchanged from the originally budgeted amount in the fiscal 27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As of April 26, Moya's headcount was 31 less or three less, I'm sorry, than their fiscal 26 budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In the council's preliminary budget response, we called on the mayor to ensure the critical and essential services we provide to our immigrant population be upheld and that we safeguard programs that may be at risk with any possible federal funding cuts.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And my questions are largely going to focus on a bunch of the services around the AIPI community funding, the consolidation of all immigrant services into a single agency, of course, contracts, payment delays, which is one of our favorite topics, prioritizing access to mental health and health services, the legal support hotline, city preparing for the 2030 census, and also Moya's role in preparing for the 26 FIFA World Cup.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I now want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Chair Incarnacion, for her opening statement.

chapters.summary: Committee on Immigration Chair Alexa Avilés delivers her opening statement, condemning recent ICE enforcement actions and calling for a historic $30 million increase in city funding for immigration legal services.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Delivers Opening Statement on MOIA Budget and ICE Arrests

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Good afternoon to Commissioner Ali and her team.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Before we get started, I want to take a quick moment to thank the entire Council Finance Division and Committee staff for their efforts in preparing for today's hearings and all of the hearings alongside the finance chair.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm sending her all of my reserved energy to continue on through this budget process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I also want to start off by condemning the horrific shooting that occurred at the Islamic Center of San Diego on May 18th and stand in solidarity with our Muslim brothers and sisters to say hate and violence against our vibrant immigrant communities will never be tolerated.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We offer our condolences to the families of the victims whose lives were taken.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Our immigrant communities continue to be under attack.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Last fall, we saw an uptick in street arrests by ICE, and recently the news publication The City reported that 800 New Yorkers were arrested by ICE between October 2025 and March 2026.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And they were not even the intended targets of immigration officers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Last year, the council fought long and hard to secure a historic level of over 50 million for immigration legal services, doubling the city's investment to safeguard immigrant communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The council values the partnership through task force meetings it has formed since last year with Moya, the Office of Civil Justice, and DOE Project Open Arms in prioritizing funding and resources for our immigrant neighbors.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: For fiscal 2027, the council is urging the restoration of last year's budget amount and an additional 30 million to further protect and enhance our city's immigration legal services in this time of great need I'll be repeating that number over and over again so you don't have to write it down no worries Commissioner Lee you played a critical role in securing that funding last year and we're asking you to continue your advocacy within the administration we look forward to an open and honest conversation today's hearing and I will now turn it back over to Chair Lee.

chapters.summary: MOIA Commissioner Faiza Ali delivers her opening testimony, sharing her personal background as the daughter of Pakistani immigrants and reaffirming the city's commitment to protecting all New Yorkers regardless of status.
chapters.title: MOIA Commissioner Faiza Ali Shares Personal Background and Sanctuary Commitment

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay great so just before we start just a couple of housekeeping rules so um as a reminder for this year's executive budget because uh we have a shorter timeline uh we're doing all of the public testimony on one day on Wednesday June 10th beginning at 9 30 a.m so please make sure to sign up on June for June 10th Wednesday 9 30 a.m we'll try to make it as fun and exciting all day as possible so please come join us that day um and also I because we're pressed for time because we have to be out of the space and we only have the translators for a certain number of hours um if members could keep their questions brief and if you guys could keep the responses as brief as possible so we can get through everything that'd be great um and if we can keep the openings to 10 minutes if it goes over it's okay but yes okay awesome thank you so much and oh turn over to council for swearing in good afternoon to your affirm to tell the truth the whole truth and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council members commissioner ally deputy commissioner Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And Deputy Commissioner Lucero.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Good afternoon, and thank you to Chair of Encarnacion and members of the Committee on Immigration, as well as Chairs Lee, Deputy Speaker Dr.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Natasha Williams, and members of the Committee on Finance for this opportunity to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: My name is Faiza Ali, and I'm the Commissioner for the New York City Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs, or Moya, and I'm joined today by my colleagues Lorena Lucero, Deputy Commissioner of Programs and Policy, as well as Jesnea Sanchez, Acting Chief of Staff and Deputy Commissioner of Administration and Strategic Initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As the daughter of immigrants from Azad Kashmir, Pakistan, I understand deeply the role that local government must play in ensuring communities are treated with fairness, dignity, and respect.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Across the country, aggressive ICE enforcement operations have sown fear and uncertainty in immigrant communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And in this moment, New York City must be clear.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We remain committed to protecting all New Yorkers, regardless of their immigration status.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: At a time when immigrant families are increasingly being met with disdain or contempt and hostility, local government must be a store a source of stability, clarity, and protection.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That requires investment, operational capacity, and sustained support to meet our responsibility to immigrant New Yorkers.

chapters.summary: MOIA Commissioner Faiza Ali discusses the initial audit of city agency policies under Executive Order 13 and the ongoing work of the Interagency Response Committee to comply with local sanctuary laws.
chapters.title: MOIA Commissioner Details Executive Order 13 Audit and Interagency Response

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Today I will provide a brief update on Moya and highlight six key areas of our work immigration legal services, the immigration legal support hotline, the Haitian Response Initiative, Rapid Response Collaborative, Language Justice and English Language Learning, and the Community Engagement Programs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: During a recent visit to 26 Federal Plaza, I watched immigrant New Yorkers arrive for a routine immigration proceedings, only to encounter an atmosphere of intimidation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Hallways were filled with families lawfully seeking status while enforcement agents moved through the building in masks.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It was deeply unsettling and a stark reminder of what is at stake for the communities that we all serve.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: At Moya's preliminary budget hearing, I outlined the challenges immigrant communities are facing under the federal administration's aggressive detention and deportation campaigns.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: ICE's recent presence at the at Wycloff Medical Center in Brooklyn drew community protests and a highly visible NYPD response.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Consistent with Executive Order 13's directive to continuously review agency policies and protocols, NYPD in partnership with the Interagency Response Committee will assess whether there are any updates to the policy and procedure to be made in light of that incident.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Pursuant to EO 13, the agencies have completed their initial audit of policies and protocols with support from the Interagency Response Committee on interactions with local immigration enforcement and is updating its training guidelines and materials accordingly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Moya remains an active participant in the ongoing work of the IRC and is firmly committed to upholding and complying with local laws.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This is a moment when immigrant New Yorkers need reassurance that their local government will stand with them and protect them.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Mayor Mamdani was elected in part because New Yorkers recognize the growing instability and anxiety many immigrant families face about their place in our society.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It is Moya's responsibility to help restore trust, reinforce that commitment, and ensure that there is continued access to schools, healthcare, housing, and city services for all New Yorkers.

chapters.summary: MOIA Commissioner Faiza Ali provides an overview of the $34.2 million FY27 MOIA budget, highlighting contract management and the opening of 10 new staff positions.
chapters.title: MOIA Commissioner Outlines FY27 Budget and Staffing Vacancies

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In the interest of time, I'm gonna skip around in the testimony, but you do have my full statement as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: With a fiscal year 27 budget of approximately $34.2 million, Moya maintains more than 70 contracts with nonprofit organizations serving immigrant communities across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have already communicating funding levels to all contracted providers so that they can plan accordingly for the next fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Over the past few years, a few weeks, it feels like it's been several several months already at Moya, but over the past few weeks, I have met directly with our providers to better understand how the city can be a stronger partner.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They are meeting this moment with urgency, compassion, and a deep community trust doing critical work on the front lines.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Now more than ever, the city must provide stability in both funding and vision so providers can continue delivering urgently needed services to immigrant New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: At the same time, Moya is continuing to build a team capable of advancing Mayor Mamdani's bold agenda.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Ten new positions at Moya are currently open, and we expect to move forward with hiring in the coming weeks.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I appreciate the council's continued attention to Moya's staffing levels and look forward to sharing additional updates soon.

chapters.summary: MOIA Commissioner Faiza Ali highlights the achievements of the Legal Support Centers (LSEs) and details the expansion of the immigration legal support hotline operated by Catholic Charities.
chapters.title: MOIA Commissioner Details Legal Support Centers and Hotline Expansion

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Over the past year, Moya has strengthened its legal service infrastructure during a period of dramatically increased need.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Moya's immigration legal programs include legal support centers, the immigration legal support hotline, the legal technical mentorship program, and the Rapid Response Legal collaborative.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Together, these initiatives expand access to trusted immigration legal support at a time when the demand continues to grow.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Our immigration legal service network includes 29 providers serving communities across the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Last summer, Moya launched the immigration legal support centers, supported by more than $18 million in investments over three years to strengthen community-based organizations providing culturally and linguistically responsive immigration legal assistance.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Since the program's launch in July 2025, the LSEs have conducted more than 12,000 comprehensive legal screenings, helping immigrants arrange address a wide range of legal needs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: These investments are critical not only to expand services, but to strengthen the long-term capacity of the organizations immigrant New Yorkers rely on the most.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Another critical resource for immigrant New Yorkers is Moya's immigration legal support hotline that's operated by Catholic charities, which helps New Yorkers connect to comprehensive immigration legal screenings and support.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So far this year, the hotline has received more than 19,800 calls.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In FY26, Moya increased funding to expand the hotline's accessibility and responsiveness as callers face increasingly urgent and complex immigration challenges.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This April, we partnered with Catholic charities to host a family preparedness food bank, responding to concerns from parents and caregivers about family separation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In collaboration with the New York State Office of New Americans, the Mexican Consulate, and the New York Legal Assistance Group, 56 volunteers fielded 225 calls.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As part of this expansion, the hotline increased staffing capacity, including an additional hiring additional counselors and a staff members specifically designated to respond to time-sensitive rapid response needs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The hours of operation were also extended beyond the previously Monday through Friday 9 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: to 6 p.m.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: schedule so that callers have more opportunities to reach the support outside of a standard work hour day.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The hotline also launched WhatsApp communications, expanded multilingual reporting from four to ten languages, and added three new interactive voice response language prompts.

chapters.summary: MOIA Commissioner Faiza Ali details the baselining of the Haitian Response Initiative (HRI) at $1.7 million and the expansion of the Rapid Response Legal Collaborative in schools.
chapters.title: MOIA Commissioner Highlights Haitian Response and Rapid Response Collaborative

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Moving on to the Haitian Response Initiative, Moya coordinates HRI an innovative program designed to support both newly arrived and long-standing Haitian residents.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The initiative funds seven Haitian-led community-based organizations to provide case management and social services, while Catholic charities delivers immigration legal services and capacity building trainings.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Established in FY22, HRI was baselined last year at nearly 1.7 million dollars.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: HRI also partners, HRI partners have also organized legal clinics across the city, helping hundreds of Haitian New Yorkers access legal assistance related to deportation protections, legal remedies, and work authorization.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Catholic charities regularly conduct conducts train the trainer workshops to equip community organizations with real time information needed to support residents navigating immigration related issues independently.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This work remains especially urgent as we know amid growing instability in Haiti and a pending Supreme Court decision that will determine the future of TPS.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In FY26 alone, HRI has completed six legal clinics with an additional asylum application review clinic scheduled today, May 28, 2026, at Brooklyn Borough Hall.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I will skip now to and talk a little bit about the Rapid Response Legal Collaborative.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Moya has significantly expanded our Rapid Response Legal Collaborative, which provides legal assistance to immigrant New Yorkers who are detained or at imminent risk of detention or deportation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: To meet the growing demand, Moya increased our investment in rapid response legal efforts by $3 million this fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Moya also coordinates rapid response efforts in schools to ensure that immigrant families receive timely information and support when immigrant and when immigration enforcement activity occurs in or near their communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It includes connecting schools, families, and community partners to legal resources, newyer rights information, and direct services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Referrals are centralized through Project Open Arms, a dedicated team within New York City public schools that coordinates across public schools, including charter schools, District 75, and District 79 schools as well, to support immigrant families and help ensure access to a full range of services.

chapters.summary: MOIA Commissioner Faiza Ali discusses MOIA's role in monitoring language access compliance across 46 city agencies and highlights the work of the in-house language services team.
chapters.title: MOIA Commissioner Details Language Access Coordination and In-House Translation

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I also wanted to provide an update on Moya's language access work, an area that impacts nearly every aspect of city government.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Moya oversees and monitors and provides technical assistance to more than 46 city agencies in implementing language access services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Language justice is not simply a compliance obligation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It is a core equity and public trust issue.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: New Yorkers cannot fully access critical services or exercise their rights or meaningfully engage with city government if language remains a barrier to information, to care or participation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This work impacts approximately 1.8 million New Yorkers with limited English proficiency and is essential to ensuring equitable access to city services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: On April 17th, Moya convened all of the city's language access coordinators for a historic citywide meeting and launched working groups focused on strengthening relationships and with language service providers, multilingual strategic communication and engagement, bilingual talent and language access training, as well as language access and data and evaluation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In addition, Moya has built an in-house language services team that provides translation and interpretation support to mayoral offices and city hall.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thus far in FY26, the team has delivered translation and interpretation services to 37 mayoral offices and agencies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The LS team has also provided on-site language support at migrant centers and played a critical role in rapidly translating updated shelter protocol for recent arrivals.

chapters.summary: MOIA Commissioner Faiza Ali concludes her testimony by detailing community engagement workshops and ethnic media roundtables, emphasizing that immigrant New Yorkers are central to the city's story.
chapters.title: MOIA Commissioner Outlines Community Engagement and Ethnic Media Roundtables

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: On community engagement, finally, I wanted to share that Moya's community engagement work helps immigrant New Yorkers access services, understand their rights, and stay connected to trusted information during a time of heightened fear and uncertainty.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Through our immigrant rights workshops, Moya staff deliver presentations that connect community organizations and their members to free, high-quality immigration legal services and know your rights resources.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In FY26, the program delivered over 300 presentations, reaching over 7,000 people citywide, continuing our critical work, reaching diverse communities where they are.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Moya also continues to strengthen relationships with trusted ethnic and community media outlets that serve as immigrant New Yorkers that serve immigrant New Yorkers in multiple languages.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In the past year, Moya has led 10 community and ethnic media roundtables to share critical information through hyperlocal and television newspapers and television stations that immigrant communities rely on for timely and accurate information.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So far during my tenure, we have convened three such round tables with additional sessions scheduled for this summer.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In conclusion, Moya was created to ensure New Yorkers can fully, that immigrant New Yorkers can fully participate in the civic, economic, and cultural life of the city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And that mission is especially important and critical during periods of rapid policy change and growing uncertainty for immigrant communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Meeting this moment requires strong coordination across agencies, deep partnership with community based organizations, and continued investment in the systems that help immigrant New Yorkers access services, understand their rights, and remain connected to their neighborhoods and opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As the needs facing immigrant communities continue to evolve, Moya will remain focused on building trust, on strengthening that coordination, and ensuring New York City remains a place where immigrant families can live with dignity, safety, and opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Finally, I want to thank the city's dedicated public servants, many who are in this room and make these hearings run, and our community partners for their continued support and responding every day to the needs of both recently arrived and long-standing immigrant New Yorkers.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks about the operational challenges and limitations of MOIA being structured as a mayoral office rather than a standalone city agency.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions MOIA on Standalone Agency Structure

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Immigrant New Yorkers are not separate from the story of this city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They are central to it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They are workers, parents, students, business owners, neighbors, and caregivers who sustain the city every day.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I look forward to working with the council to ensure that our policies and investments and our institutions reflect that reality while continuing to strengthen the communities that are in fact the backbone of our city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I just want to say thank you to you also because even when you were back in the day in the council, when I was at KCS, you were so instrumental in helping a lot of our immigrant communities and nonprofit organizations be that bridge to us accessing folks in government.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I think it's very crucial.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's a very crucial role that you played and still play even more so now in this role.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, so I'll go through my questions quickly because I know we have others that need to ask questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So since Moya is a mayoral office technically and not a full-fledged city agency, what specific challenges and limitations does this structure present in terms of funding, operational capacity, and policy implementation?

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks about the frequency and effectiveness of the quarterly interagency task force meetings coordinated by MOIA across city government.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions MOIA on Interagency Coordination

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because I know you have to go across a lot of different city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, I this is a great question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think it's actually a really important conversation that should be had, and it does, you know, I think that it deserves more nuance than sort of a like binary answer of like should Moya be an agency or not.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, you know, right now Moya's structure as an office, it does create certain limitations because we are, like you said, fundamentally a coordinating office rather than a standalone operational agency, um, and much of our work does uh require sustained partnership across city government.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But at the same time, I think that um there are some real strengths to the current structure that we should take into consideration.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Being housed within City Hall, for example, gives Moya the direct proximity to executive decision making on issues um related to immigrants and immigrant inclusion.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It really allows us to also elevate um our concerns as a citywide priority rather than being siloed um into a standalone agency.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But I say all this to say that you know, I think it's really important for me, and I'm glad you raised the question the the issue of input and engagement with advocates.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think this is a conversation that needs to be had more broadly with other stakeholders to fully understand uh you know what will the impact be, in fact, if we were to transition into an agency or not.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And is there um are there regular meetings or task force groups or conversations?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm assuming the answer is yes, but if you could speak to that a little bit because um between Moya, HRA, DYCD, SBS, DOHMH, like there's a lot of agencies I feel like that you touch, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so how has that been in terms of I mean the last few weeks?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Well, I mean, there is a long-standing inter-agency task force that Moya um is responsible for it in it brings to the table all 46 city agencies for the very purpose of making sure that we're sharing updates and information, you know, uh that can potentially impact immigrant New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so those those meetings happen quarterly, but I'll go a step further, you know, um and just tap into my organizing background.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's really important for me as the leader of this office to also engage commissioners at at some of the leading agencies where immigrant New Yorkers seek services and make sure that we're building um across as commissioners as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so I've been, you know, um focused on um having those meetings, having those conversations, and making sure that we're elevating those issues even outside of the interagency task force meetings.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks how MOIA is working to increase access to legal services and address systemic language access gaps specifically for the AAPI community.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions MOIA on AAPI Legal Services and Language Access

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and then moving now to the AAPI community support, um, I know that there was uh an analysis done by Stop AAPI Hate, and it found that a lot of the ICE arrests um actually have gone up seven times in the Asian community, and then um there have been over fourteen hundred arrests of individuals with Asian citizenship in New York compared to two hundred and five arrests of Asian immigrants during a similar period.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and in April, the LDEF Asian American Legal Defense Fund released a report highlighting system systemic gaps in language access, outreach, funding structure, and institutional partnerships.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so, um, how has your office or will your office increase access to legal services um specifically for the AAPI community?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And what are some challenges you've had connecting to the population to legal services, which I'm sure a lot of is language and other things, but yeah, I think first it's really important to sort of highlight that Moya meets the needs of AAPI immigrant communities through sustained engagement with stakeholders and advocates, different organizations, and that includes ongoing meetings with advocates and community organizations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have close partnership with AAPI serving organizations, and many of them are receive funding and are run our legal support centers or our immigrant rights workshops.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think that one of the biggest challenges to answer your question more directly is language access.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And while many of the legal services hotlines right now primarily operate in English and Spanish, and so for many AAPI immigrants, the legal system can seem inaccessible.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But language is only part of the challenge.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think that AAPI communities is incredibly diverse as well, with different immigration histories and cultural contexts and levels of familiarity, frankly, with the you know the systems.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I think uh like you know, typically we tend to lean on a one size fits all approach to outreach, but in this instance it just is not something that would work.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, there are real gaps when it comes to uh culturally and linguistically competent legal services, particularly for uh some of the lesser common um languages that are spoken.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, I think that's primarily a reason why MOIA has focused heavily on our partnerships, um, especially when we're doing our multilingual know your rights outreach.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We've relied heavily on our ethnic media engagement.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, the community media is where many of the Asian communities seek you know trusted information from.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so that has been really important for us to forge those relationships with ethnic media and to get the information out.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I think this is across all communities in general, and something we've heard in our roundtable conversations where there really is such a shortage of culturally competent attorneys that are available.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks how local AAPI-serving community-based organizations can partner with MOIA to build community-led legal infrastructures.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Inquires About AAPI Community Partnerships

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so what are some ways that and you sort of alluded to this with the partnership aspect, but what are some ways our local AAPI serving community-based organizations can partner with you to bridge this gap?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, especially for our AAPI communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, I think meeting those requirements requires sustained investment in community-led legal infrastructures.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, one specific way that Moya is already working on this is really through capacity building initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, I mentioned in my testimony uh the legal technical mentorship program that's administered by IARC, making sure that our AAPIs led and serving organizations have access to that capacity building tool is really critical.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks about the targeted multilingual media campaign funded by the council to promote the immigration legal support hotline in Asian and African communities.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions Legal Support Hotline Outreach

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, and then moving to the legal support hotline, um, because I know that you were saying that you received 14,700 calls in FY26.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and I know the council also supported this with an additional 500,000 in FY26 to further expand the hotline.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think um, and outreach to the Asian and African communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so just wanted to get a sense of um how you sort of and you sort of mentioned this already, but how you were able to partner with ethnic media or local community-based groups to do the outreach for the hotline specifically.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I'll share a little bit about the council's support.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Also, you know, we appreciate the additional investment um in FY26 to help just bring more awareness to the hotline and and specifically do outreach to these particular communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Right now, um, Moya is currently finalizing a targeted multilingual know your rights media campaign that's supported through this um additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We anticipate to launch that in mid-June.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, the campaign is being designed specifically to reach New Yorkers, um, some of the more hard-to-reach New York communities, specifically in our Asian and African communities through our ethnic media platforms, um, and our outreach will um be expanded to include radio and TV and print and um digital media and also other other platforms, frankly, that um more of these communities utilize, like YouTube, Facebook, and and Instagram.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
