utterances.text: Um, the campaign, and we can share additional um breakdown, but there are a number of languages that will be included in in this as part of um the outreach campaign in Wooff in Tibetan and Korean and Bengali, um, and we can provide the council with a full list.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks how many of the 19,800 hotline callers were successfully connected to legal services and other community supports.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions Hotline Referral Outcomes

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then how many of the total 14,700 callers were effectively connected to legal services, legal legal services providers, and how many of them actually received legal support if you have that?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, so just to clarify on the on the numbers, um, from FY uh from July 1st, 2025 to May 15, 2026, the hotline received 19,000 calls.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, when a caller calls a hotline, we do not collect uh PII information, so personal identifying information.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We do direct them to our legal support centers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Overall, the legal support centers have served over 12,000 people.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, and okay, so that but that doesn't just include the legal services, but maybe referring them to different community organizations for other types of support, or is this specifically just for the legal the hotline itself um provides um referrals to primarily our legal support centers for legal services, that's sort of the why it was created, but it also provides um connections to other agencies and things to that extent.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um okay, great.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks how MOIA is working with OMB and MOX to address extreme contract processing delays and payment timelines for legal services providers.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions Contract Processing Delays for Legal Providers

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then let me just skip because I know we're for contract and payment delays, moving on a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um we know that there's um you guys are you know the legal services groups are dealing with an unprecedented attack on um immigrants' rights, and one of the biggest issues that providers expressed during um our round table that we had was the extreme delays in getting paid by the city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um one provider explained that the city owes them 15 million, um, and they have now spent thousands of dollars on interest payments uh from private loans just to make payroll, and this is a common sort of theme that we hear amongst our nonprofit providers, and so just wondering um how Moya, if if you can advocate for this administration to dedicate more funding to processing the contracts and talking to Mox, ensuring that providers have timely access to invoicing and reimbursements.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If you want to speak to that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I can start and then I'll turn it over to my colleague if she has anything more to add.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I mean, this is an area that you all know I care very deeply about.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I did what during my time at the council.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Moya strongly supports continued investment in contract processing capacity and reforms to improve payment timelines for our providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We recognize that a lot of our immigrant serving organizations are operating under significant financial pressure.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think it's important for me to just kind of elevate that last session the council did enact major procurement reforms, including laws establishing advanced payments and quarterly invoice invoicing and stronger accountability.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Those reforms were really an important step forward, but implementation across the agencies is something that's critical.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're continuing to partner with our agencies and talking to mocks and providers to identify any operational bottlenecks and find out, you know, figure out ways to together how do we improve that consistency and transparency.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I would just add that at least on the Moya and we have a close partnership with both Mox and the Minor's Office of Nonprofit Services, who are a great resources, uh a great resource for all nonprofits, especially folks that are new to uh you know uh the invoicing or contracting uh process over um, you know, here uh in the city, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh so we definitely at least want these to get it elevated to us.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We definitely work closely with providers to point them in the right direction.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks about MOIA's collaboration with DOHMH to provide mental health and trauma-informed support to immigrants following federal detention.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions Post-Detention Mental Health Support

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I think um you answered my question, Commissioner, already about I think I think HRA and DSS are probably some of the ones that we hear most about from the um agencies, and so whatever you guys can do to help push those contracts through would be I think immensely helpful.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um moving on to health and mental health.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um how so I know that aside from the traditional um mental health and health services, I know that more specifically there's been a lot of um trauma around the federal immigration detention that's being that's been happening across the city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so do you support um immigrants post-detention with their mental health needs or other services and what does that look like?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Moya's been working really closely with agency partners, um, including DOHMH to really understand better um the kinds of support that and mental health support um that we can uh provide to immigrants following detention.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: There um this includes coordination with organizations um such as the Envision Freedom Fund, which is a veil and bond support nonprofit organization to strengthen connections between community-based organizations and the relevant agencies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um proud to say is in the preliminary stages of research to assess the mental health impacts that immigration post-detention and to identify where are the gaps um and and services and support that is needed, and we'll continue to engage our partners on uh this process and share those additional findings once we have them.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks if there is additional funding in the executive plan for wraparound immigration services, highlighting the work of the Haitian Response Initiative.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Inquires About Wraparound Service Funding

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And will there be, I mean, given that we're looking at federal cuts that are obviously going to hurt the refugee asylum seeker services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Have you been having conversations with the administration, OMB?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Do you know if there's any additional funding for immigration services in the executive plan that go towards specifically funding wraparound services for this population?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The administration continues to evaluate community needs across legal services and case management and other stabilization support.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And you know, at Moya, we strongly recognize the importance of holistic and trauma-informed approaches, particularly for vulnerable populations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so that while there may not be an additional funding in the executive plan for wraparound immigration services, we do want to take a moment to highlight again the work of the Haitian Response Initiative because they have done incredible work in this area.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think we have specific data, but the HRI has completed about 610 intakes so far this fiscal year and has completed a certain number of trainings as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So there is an example of folks within Moya's network where they have offered and do offer this trauma-informed care.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks how MOIA is advocating for funding to retain immigration attorneys and ensure flexible use of funds for legal services providers.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions Attorney Retention and Flexible Funding

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And two things really quickly I just want to uplift is that during our roundtable discussions with a lot of the uh providers, uh definitely ways to retain immigration attorneys, and if there's anything you all can do on your side to really emphasize to admin, OMB that we need extra funds to make sure that we're able to retain the attorneys with higher salaries and pay, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then, of course, purpose of funds, making sure that the funding can be flexible because there's a lot of different types of services that they need to provide.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so those are two things I just wanted to highlight from our round table conversations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and I'm gonna pause there and I have a couple questions, but let me move on to our to my co-chair, uh, Chair R Enconnacion.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks which city agencies house the 10 new open positions at MOIA and whether they include the three vacancies discussed during the preliminary budget.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions MOIA Staffing and Open Positions

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Hello again.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so I'm gonna jump right in because we are uh pressed for time.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, but I wanted to talk about the budget, Moya's budget and your head count.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, you know, it's always challenging to try to conduct oversight because of the programs they're spread throughout multiple agencies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I was already discussed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I know that you highlighted some strengths and some challenges that we may have in calling for an independent agency.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm gonna continue to call for that uh because I really feel that there are some uh major wins that we can get there, but I I I appreciate the uh want and need for for the communication around that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um in your testimony, you mentioned 10 new positions um at Moya that they are currently open.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Under which agencies are those 10 new positions housed, and does that include the three vacant positions that we had spoken about during the prelim budget?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Or is it in addition to those those three?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um yes, that that does include uh those uh those uh three uh vacancies that we spoke about in the prelim.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and these vacancies are spread uh through the mayor's office, uh DCAS, uh DSS, and SBS.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What was the first one before DCAS?

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés questions the structure of having MOIA staff lines housed under other agencies' budgets while executing MOIA's core mission.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions MOIA Staff Lines Housed Under Other Agencies

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm sorry?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh DSS.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Mayor's office.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The mayor's office, okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, got it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And this is all still with the same baseline budget of 781.962.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Well, um, so um our lines overall they're spread throughout um, you know, they're housed at uh different agencies over at the mayor's office.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have uh five lines that are currently housed there, and those uh in the three vacancies that we spoke about, that's where those are, and that's the 700.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um sorry, the um, yeah, the 780 uh thousand dollar budget is specifically uh for the mayor's office, but we do have other lines that are housed under other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: While that's the case, uh the employees uh do sit within our office, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And do carry out uh Moya's uh you know work uh you know, but they're essentially uh you know on payroll on other um agencies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This is where it gets confusing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: This is where I'm not really understanding.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we'll get into that because I'm I'm trying to understand, like um, so then as far as directives, mission who tells them what they should be doing on a day-to-day basis.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is that coming from a different agency?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is it coming from your vision?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um if it's coming from your vision, then it makes sense that it was it's your staff, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If it's coming from DSS or DCAS, then it's it's their staff.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, chair, you raise a very great question, and and we're trying to demystify it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I apologize if if we're adding to the confusion.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The the staff that are currently at Moya, um, even if their budget comes from a different agency, they are executing the work and the mission of Moya.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They are physically in Moya's offices.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I I see them, I interact with them.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Their core responsibilities are to um you know uh implement our programs, our policies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so it uh the total PS budget, you know, in across the agencies, including the the mayor's office, is about 5.3 million for staffing that that implement our program and our work.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks which MOIA programs are currently RFP'd and the timeline for future competitive procurements to avoid service breaks.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions MOIA RFP Cycles and Future Procurements

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That is actually really helpful.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I just wonder why the hesitancy to just call it Moya's budget in that way, uh, because then I can I can see growth there and I can see the importance behind the mayor's office of immigrant affairs when I'm we're talking about the millions of dollars for a budget versus seven barely a million dollars for the the office, but we'll move on.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So under um which Moya programs are RFP'd and which are not RFP'd?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so most immediately for FY27, uh, we're not uh RFPing any new programs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh we um especially with the added baseline funding, our focus has been to um to extend contracts, right, to avoid break in services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh we're still um in the process of making a a determination and uh an assessment on uh whether to RP programs in the future.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're still in that process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And once we make that decision and we move forward, if we do move forward right with that decision, we'll definitely ensure that community-based organizations and all the information is public with as much with plenty of time for folks to be able to participate as part of that procurement process.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks what metrics MOIA uses to evaluate current programs and how provider feedback is incorporated into future planning.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Program Evaluation Metrics and Provider Feedback

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can you talk about some of the metrics that you're going to use to evaluate those programs briefly?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Like what are you looking for?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How do you see success and extending those programs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: On the current programs that we have, I can well, there is a variety of things.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But I so I'll try to answer the RFP question and then I think the to the programs that we currently have.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think that's a again conversations that we will have as the commissioner has been mentioning.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have been actively meeting with all of our providers to hear the good, the bad, and the ugly, and those conversations will continue in regards to what's working and what's not.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What we have pride ourselves with, and I've heard this from Chair Lee as well, is we have tried to maintain our programs, at least some of them very nimble, so providers can respond in real time.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's a combination of how how many folks have been served, um, the types of folks that have been served, languages spoken, things to that extent.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But it varies for we I think the numbers at 11 right now of different of the different initiatives that we have.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, but it's also a point of um that we're open to having conversations in regards to anything that you might be hearing from providers yourself or things that you're learning on the ground um in regards to what we should be thinking about as well.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks about the contract cycles and renewal status of key initiatives like the Legal Support Centers and the Haitian Response Initiative.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Contract Cycles and Renewal Status

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And are they currently in a um contract cycle and what year are they in most of these RFPs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I can start.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I don't know if you want me to walk you through the different programs that we have for but the legal support centers, they were part of an RFP that happened last last year, and this is their first year of a three-year contract.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So that's one bucket.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have HRI partners that are in their a renewal cycle now and are starting again this year, same contracted partners.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have our immigrant rights workshop, same thing, as well as our hotline, which would continue with Catholic charities, the rapid response to your collaborative, as the commissioner mentioned, there was an enhancement there, but that contract is also uh moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Our English language support centers as well, those contracts continue.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I think I got all of them, but and uh I think I got all of them, but um the only one I think that you can think of within the RFP cycle would be our um like our legal support centers, which are locked into a three-year contract.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks how the $33 million in baseline funding for immigration legal services will be allocated across different programs.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Allocation of Baseline Legal Services Funding

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Got it, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, speaking of immigration legal services, um, you know, uh we're very excited to see roughly 33 million um comprised of 11.6 million added in the prelim uh for legal support centers and 21.3 million added for the expansion of immigrant legal services baseline starting in fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh but there seemed to be and there was no additional funded added in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I wanted to know what exactly will this additional funding added in pre-limb be used for.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, for example, which existing programs will be expanded, will there be new programs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The additional funding is going to allow to us to continue the current programming while also expanding key support initiatives that we know the immigrant New Yorkers are depending on as part of the funding that was baselined.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The city increase in baseline funding for the RLC by three million dollars.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is that this the because of the fast changing federal policies?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It forces and and the changes in in law as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It forces the immigration attorneys to have to sort of chase all of those changes and be nimble.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And we're really um we're proud that we were able to baseline some of the support, get additional resources in FY26 to make sure that that they have what they need to actually be nimble in that space.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it's an expansion, it's not creating new programs.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks if any new programs will be created with the baseline funding and the process for community engagement in program design.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Inquires About New Program Creation

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Will any new programs be created?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, we're absolutely looking forward to you know having those conversations in the foreseeable future about you know, but I think the biggest important thing for me has been, you know, we've mentioned meeting with all of our providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It was like a week-long marathon meeting of hundreds of providers, specifically to understand not just what the needs are, but like are the programs that we are currently administering having an impact in the communities, and how are we measuring that?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So those conversations for future programming are important for us to have.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It just requires a little bit more community engagement and stakeholder input.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks about the specific types of cases covered by the legal services funding, highlighting the $14.6 million directed to deportation defense.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Specific Case Coverage and Deportation Defense

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so what specific immigration cases or clients is the $33 million anticipated to cover.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Sorry, I didn't cut that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You said the $33 million?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're we're making significant in investment in deportation defense.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um Moya is directing $14.6 million of that to go to the RLC to our legal support centers and to support uh you know New Yorkers who are facing deportation cases.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think that level of investment really in in shows the urgency, and my colleague just passed a note that we do want to uplift that there is addition, there is additional resources that will be going to our habeas project, and we can provide details on the breakdown of that particular funding.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks how MOIA is planning for potential federal clawbacks of legal service funding and whether any emergency budget reserves exist.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Planning for Federal Clawbacks and Reserves

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That would be very helpful.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And and do you know of any of this new funding will be allocated directly to vendors?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: All of it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: All of it?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And have you thought about, and I'm sure you have thought about this, so that was that part is rhetorical.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, but how are you thinking about and planning for potential federal clawbacks of legal service funding or restrictions on who can be served?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, it's one of the things that keeps you up at night.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, but I think it's really important to highlight that our legal services are funded by the city, uh, which shields them from direct federal clawbacks.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: However, we are actively you know tracking what the what can be trickle-down effects from federal funding cuts and restrictions on on whom our organizations and providers can serve.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We remain, um, like I said, in in close communication with our agency partners regarding federal policy changes and any threats to accessing services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, specifically, we've been talking to our partners and agencies around the mixed uh status rule change that's being proposed by HUD.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and and of course, you know, staying in close communication with our providers who they flag uh you know emerging issues that they see on the ground regularly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Do you know if there would be like budget reserves for those kind of emergency situations or something that would happen, you know, it could be overnight with this federal administration.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um are there any kind of reserves specifically for uh Moya?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, that's that's something that I would have to circle back with you on.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm I'm unsure.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks about MOIA's advocacy with the Office of New Americans (ONA) and state leaders to coordinate funding for legal services.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Advocacy with State Leaders and ONA

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I know uh can you talk a little bit about some of the advocacy you've done with uh the Office of New Americans and the state leaders uh to advocate for the some of the services and the funding for legal services specifically, but anywhere you've done uh some of that advocacy.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, this is a a priority that we're that we're actively working to address.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, um I mentioned earlier uh most recently, Moya coordinated with ONA on our family preparedness uh phone bank, and we're going to continue to build on that partnership to really um you know identify and respond to whatever additional needs may um arise.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think for us, the priority is to make sure that we're preventing any sort of gaps in services that that may arise and and are looking forward to continuing that partnership with ONA.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks for the number of individuals served through the Rapid Response Legal Collaborative in schools during the current fiscal year.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Rapid Response Legal Collaborative Outcomes

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then the Rapid Um Response Legal Collaborative, you've you've talked a little bit about that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How many?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can you tell me how many people have been served through the work for fiscal 2026 thus far?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can we once I'm just gonna get the numbers?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We came with numbers just in case.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Love that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So through uh 20 um this year to date, I can tell you that on some of the KYR efforts that we've done, there have been 383 students supported, grades nine through ten.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In quarter two and quarter three of FY26, the collaborative screened about 90 individuals under the expanded DOE scope and provided legal services to 50 of them.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I'm gonna circle back on the other bucket because there's two buckets of work that I just want to have in front of me.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The one that I walked you through is connected to the rapid response through your collaborative and schools.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks about the challenges of virtual and in-person event turnout for rapid response and how MOIA is working to improve outreach.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Rapid Response Event Turnout Challenges

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, it seems pretty low the numbers, and maybe I just uh in skewing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So can you talk about maybe whatever you've heard are some of the biggest challenges in supporting our students at that level?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, I think one of the biggest challenges right now is is turnout for virtual and and those in-person events.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, we're working really closely with our partners to look into how do we churn out for some of those future events?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What does outreach look like?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How can we better ensure that individuals are receiving access to the information that they need?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, we've been we've we've struggled with that with the know-your rights as well, so that rings very true.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks about the authorship of the Executive Order 13 audit report and MOIA's role on the Interagency Response Committee.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Executive Order 13 Audit Report Authorship

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I'll I'm gonna get into the uh city's sanctuary policies across the agencies, executive order 13 and what happened there, and then I'm gonna um allow uh my colleagues to ask some questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh for many of you who who may not know yet, on uh February 6th, the mayor issued Executive Order 13 to strengthen the city's sanctuary protections, and the report for this executive order audit um and its findings was published online just recently on May 22nd.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I want to thank the administration for giving uh the council a briefing on this before it was uh put online.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It was extremely helpful to hear about it before actually reading it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um but I uh so I had some questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, who in the administration authored the report and what was your role throughout the the fight before the findings came out?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, the report was uh prepared by the interagency response committee, which as you mentioned, it was created by um executive order and EO13, it's chaired by the FDM, it includes direct consultation from Moya, our chief counsel's office, and the special advisor for strategic coordination.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um that's who prepared the report.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks MOIA to describe the significant gaps identified in city agency reporting on interactions with federal immigration authorities.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Significant Gaps in Agency ICE Reporting

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um we meet um and have met throughout this process in leading up to the release of the audit quite regularly to you know discuss um how are we going to roll out this information.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm I was pleased to see that we had such great participation from our partners in the council for the briefing as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, it was great, and then moving forward, um will Moya be kind of like the lead of those conversations and the ongoing um audits for lack of a better word here?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, Moya is a a member of the IRC in a leadership capacity, so we will be continuing um our role in that process.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, um, and in that report, there uh the audit reports and talks about significant gaps in city agency reporting on interactions with federal immigration authorities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Can you describe in a little bit of detail what those significant gaps were?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, the audit revealed that um many interactions between city agencies and federal immigration authorities were not reported at all, um, either because agencies weren't effectively tracking um those interactions, because incidents surfaced maybe after relevant reports had already been published, or because the existing guidance um didn't require reporting interactions such as subpoenas and warrants and and other instances where federal agents forcefully entered city property.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and I think to to um directly address um these gaps, the mayor has uh updated and approved um additional guidance that will be issued by the IRC.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And in this um guidance, reporting requirements have been strengthened and clarified, um, including by expanding the types of interactions that are subject to that reporting.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In short, um, you know, agencies are now directed to be over inclusive um in what they report to Moya.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks if MOIA identified any additional gaps from their own data analysis that were missing from the official audit report.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Gaps Identified from MOIA's Data Analysis

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And did you see any gaps on from your own data analysis that maybe weren't in the um audit, the official audit, or that you may have put in there from your own analysis or just recognize we're missing from the report?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, um, you know, I think until now the reports that were issued by Moya, um, they categorize like other instances as as any types of interaction between city agencies and federal immigration agencies that were not otherwise subject to reporting.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So that you know, as I mentioned earlier, was something that we also has had identified as as a gap.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés questions the NYPD recommendation to notify operations of ICE presence and asks for clarification on the role of a 'patrol executive' sent to the scene.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions NYPD Recommendation and 'Patrol Executive' Role

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And one of the recommendations submitted by the NYPD is to establish a protocol requiring that the communications division notify operations of any 911 calls related to the presence of federal immigration authorities or immigration matters.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And the recommendation says that a patrol executive will be sent to the scene.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: What is a patrol executive?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, that's a great question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That is information that we can share after we have more discussion with the NYPD and other IRC partners.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But do you do you know it as a uniform officer?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is that it's is it do you know that it's gonna well you don't know that it's gonna be a separate person?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I the wording is interesting.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, that's something I'll have to get back to you on.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um so the I'll I'll save those follow-up questions for this patrol executive for uh that time.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks about the timeline for city agencies to post their ICE interaction policies online and how compliance is monitored.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Timeline for Posting ICE Interaction Policies

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and then I know that accord that EO 13 the six agencies had to make their policies and protocols regarding interactions with ICE available on their websites.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Do you know if that they have done so, if all of them have done so?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Now that the mayor has received the audit and adopted the recommendations for changes to the policies and protocols, agencies are beginning to implement those changes and will complete that process in the next in the coming months.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The IRC is going to continue in our in our role as leaders as leadership to work with the agencies as they undertake this process, which also includes a public engagement component.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But there's no like set timeline of when I mean promptly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: There's no deadline, but promptly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then who monitors the compliance is you said it's the IRC IRC.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Alexa Avilés asks if there is a process to adjust rates or colas for providers in the absence of new RFPs to address rising operational costs.
chapters.title: Chair Avilés Questions Adjusting Provider Rates and Colas

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm gonna take a break so I can let some of my colleagues um ask some questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'll have Linda uh actually before that, I I just had a really really quick clarifying question on the RFP question that the chair just asked, because um while I know there's pros and cons to not issuing out new RFPs, I guess my main concern, and correct me if I'm wrong, is that it also doesn't allow for new rates and increases, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I'm just wondering if if there's something baked in or a process where um colas or the rates per case and those types of things will be increased or looked at.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um for that, um, we'll we're gonna have to circle back with you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're gonna have to consult with our contracting agency uh to get more details on that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because if there's a way where we could address that issue, um, even if there's no new RFP, I think that'd be great because I know that that could be a way to increase some of the funds that they're getting.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

chapters.summary: Council Member Rita Joseph asks about expanding the Haitian Response Initiative and partnering with council member offices to host legal clinics.
chapters.title: Council Member Joseph Questions Haitian Response Expansion and Council Partnerships

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have Councilmember Joseph followed by Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Good to see you always, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Just a couple of follow-up questions.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I know you said you provided programming on the HRI.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Number of people serve 610 for 2026 thus far.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That's correct.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Will there be an expansion of the HRI initiative?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is that something you're looking for?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Currently, the providers will be remaining the same at the same funding level.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I have another question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Will you are you considering maybe thinking about partnering with council member offices to provide those services in their offices?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That's something that was actually raised when we met with our HRI partners on how do we reach more deeper into communities so it's part of an ongoing conversation.

chapters.summary: Council Member Rita Joseph asks how MOIA is supporting asylum seekers with filing fees and communicating the availability of these funds to CBOs.
chapters.title: Council Member Joseph Questions Asylum Seeker Filing Fee Support

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, I think we should consider that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How are you supporting asylum seekers?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I noticed that they have a fee they have to pay by May 29, 2026.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How are you getting that word out to the communities?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: One to credit the council and also some of the work that we don't say, we don't get enough praises.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Go ahead though.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I have to be honest here, but um because of the partnership that we had last year during the budget negotiations, we were able to allocate funding for fees, including for including that it's um including the some of the filing fees that you're mentioning right now, which I think is a win overall.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have been working with our providers to ensure that they're aware that that's available to them, um, and we have been pretty successful of spending some of that funding down.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, how do how do communities or for example my office or non-for-profit get in touch with you to know that these fees are available for members?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because I think today was the deadline for some of them to apply for those fees for the for the for filing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, well, I mean, we are always available after this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'll send you an email, uh, council member, and then we're happy to make the connections with our legal service providers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I would love that.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks about home health aide training programs for asylum seekers and whether MOIA plans to scale this successful model.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Questions Home Health Aide Training for Asylum Seekers

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: First of all, Commissioner, thank you for showing up at Open Arms District 3.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: They very much appreciated that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Number two, um, as you know, Ruth Messenger is very involved with uh people who are here seeking asylum.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And she has managed, thanks to money that you and previous speaker Adrian Adams allocated to the uh Jewish hospital to have a teaching program for home health aids.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Really successful.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So my question is are you doing that elsewhere?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: In other words, it's my experience because I have folks who are seeking asylum in my house.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That the job situation is what they're looking for.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Obviously, people are delivering, but they would like something more secure.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Unfortunately, we're all getting older, and home health aids are desperately needed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, my question is are there other such programs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Will money be allocated?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is it something you're looking toward?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I actually had a very good um and lengthy discussion with Ruth.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm sure specifically about the success of this program because it was a pilot and talked a little bit about ways to expand that and into other um, you know, into other uh job opportunities as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it is part of an ongoing conversation that we are hoping to continue with Ruth um on that on that specific teaching hospital, but also just using that model to see if if it's something that we can scale up and support.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks for estimates on the number of asylum seekers in the city who could benefit from targeted employment programs.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Inquires About Asylum Seeker Population Estimates

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And is there a sense of I have no sense.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I mean, I wish that every single person who came here was able to stay who was seeking asylum.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um we need the schools, we need we need them all.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That's not the case with the person in in uh Washington.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So my question is do we have some sense of how many folks here are seeking asylum and who are obviously not everybody's in shelter, not everybody is able to be found.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You can't find the people in my house, I bet.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So my question is how do we have any sense of the numbers?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Many of whom could use these programs that Ruth is uh trying to establish.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I don't have that number handy, but we will get it and we can circle back with that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, because I think that would be helpful give some sense of where we're going in terms of those kinds of programs.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks for an update on English Language Learner (ELL) programs, highlighting the need to address extensive waiting lists.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Questions English Language Learner Programs

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The young people I know just got jobs as security officers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Very exciting, big deal, better than delivering, safer, etc.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: From their perspective, more steady.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Second question is everybody has to learn English if you want to get a job.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Some of them are learning it from YouTube.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: That seems to be a way.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But the adult literacy, can you bring us up to date on um cuts where you're looking?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um obviously the feds have been very unfortunately uh cutting, and I think this is as important as all the other topics that we've been talking about today.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, I can start.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I don't have the in regards to just overall cuts, we don't have that, but we are happy to say that um via the Moya English Language and Learning Support Centers, um, we have added um and have extended and baseline funding for the for our English language support centers at 500k per library system.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We do have uh what we call We Speak, which is uh which is a a way for folks who might not be ready to join an adult literacy class, might be a bit too shy to speak out or speak openly and learn to try to learn the language and encourages people to um communicate amongst each other, but also learn about New York City civic engagement life and what other offices and agencies are doing around the city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So it's a bit of a multifold way of of integrating new New Yorkers into our city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So the reason I ask you is it would be helpful because I know you bring information and agencies uh together to have a sense of where we are with in general the English L ELLs because it seems to me that if there are huge waiting lists, which there always have been, then that would be something that the council and the mayor should be looking at.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It is the one way that everybody can get jobs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I'd love to know again, it exists in different agencies, libraries, DOE, you, etc.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So is that something that you can work on?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Council Member Gale Brewer asks how MOIA coordinates ELL programs across libraries, DOE, and other agencies to ensure efficient resource allocation.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Questions ELL Program Coordination

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I mean, I I mean we know, and you kind of hit it on the nail with both of your questions that English learning is is the pathway for civic participation as Lorena mentioned, but ac economic mobility and and just long-term stability for immigrant communities in in our city through our English language um yeah English learning and support centers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We know they're designed in a way to strengthen language um skills, but really connect them immigrant New Yorkers to larger services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, you know, we see this as an important, it's a value, it's an important program that we uh administer at Moya um and we would love to continue those conversations with the council on how to provide additional support for adult literacy okay thank you very much thank you council member thank you thank you chairs um thank you all for being here I have a couple of scattershot questions um in terms of um in terms of the immigration legal support hotline um if I recall it sounds like the number of calls that you've received that there have been a good number of improvements to the hotline um do you have a sense of what of what the increase is in usage from the prior fiscal year.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about the increase in legal support hotline calls and the growing complexity and duration of cases handled by counselors.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Legal Support Hotline Call Volume and Complexity

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah um I'm about to get these numbers I know because they're watching um so um I think we've we've talked a little bit about this in years past but there was actually a decrease in some of the calls during the last administration um and I think it it was connected also to sort of the federal environment and things to that extent um um but that's just overall I know that now we have seen an increase in calls in other languages Haitian Creole to be one that is very different and that change really began January of this year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But I'll try to get you a number because I know that you might be interested in that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah no I I think you know our perpetual struggles to make sure that these hotlines are as effective as possible and I think you know, we want to recognize that there have been investments and work to improve those things.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I'm I'm glad to see that to hear that there are more languages incoming um that's a good sign.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think I was also heartened to hear about, I mean, the 12,000 of the 19,000 being particularly referrals for legal support, um, kind of leads me to my next question in terms of the legal support partners.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Obviously, they have um they have they're they're bound by contracts with certain outcomes, and the improvement in the hotline will potentially mean more volume for the legal support centers who are bound by specific contracts and service limits.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is there a consideration to um adjust support to the legal centers as that volume increases in referrals?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Because I guess what I'm getting at here is that I feel like the volume of referrals probably coming to the legal support centers has increased it uh from every direction, and uh the contracts are stagnant and not increasing with the same level.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Obviously, it's not a one-to-one.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We'd love that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, but do you have a sense of how that is kind of metting out at the end of the day?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, I can um and I also just want to clarify that um referrals are made to the legal support centers, the rapid response to your collaborative knife up, as well as um we were able to um also provide connections to ICAR as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So there's a bit more support with that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um on the legal support center side, as you know, um besides the three-year contracts, there's other providers as well who have a one-year contract, and as a commissioner mentioned, that funding has been renewed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So there's a bit of a of an expanded.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But what we have done internally is we've created a capacity tracker as a centralized tool to support the hotline referrals and the legal support centers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we're not just like sending people without consulting with the legal support centers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Weekly, we send out and ask them to provide us with a real-time snapshot of what their capacity looks like.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So they have that as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And part of our sort of ongoing work with the hotline and because we also recognize that you know we've been doing a ton of outreach and are about to roll out more outreach and with this hotline number, what does that mean for capacity?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think part of the improvements that we have with Catholic charities and and some of our legal service providers is understanding better how do we what does the flow of information look like?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How should legal service providers be receiving those calls?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think that there's been improvements, improvements being made to the coordination to just host also address the capacity concern.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Our intention is not to flood the legal service providers with all calls, but screen them so the appropriate calls are being sent to the appropriate organizations.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If I may add one thing, Councilmember, um the other note to make, just data-wise, is that the calls have, although we have received lesser calls, the call time and like duration of the call has increased.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So from FY25, when the average was about four minutes, and FY26, it's about seven, which talks about just the complexity of the calls that we're getting as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So just wanted to add that as well.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks how MOIA is adjusting support for legal centers as referral volume increases, highlighting the use of the capacity tracker.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Adjusting Support for Legal Centers

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: No, that's helpful.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I think I'm I'm heartened to hear about the capacity tracker because it is uh a concern.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think where we can be uh more targeted and precise in our understanding where the gaps are in the in the field, um, then I think we'll be able to kind of build on that, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I I'm heartened to hear that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: If I may one quick um I guess it just uh we can talk some more about about that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um I think uh we still have a whole uh three years um with Orange Chupacabra and uh complex system, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As we see every day, more and more changes are being made, which brings more complexity to the legal field.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I think there's just going to be an ongoing issue to make sure that we check in and be very precise with like how we're supporting the building of the capacity to find lawyers.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks how MOIA is working to build capacity for immigration lawyers to navigate rapidly changing federal policies.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Attorney Capacity and Changing Federal Policies

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um in terms of uh adult literacy, um, I'm sorry I didn't uh I think some of these questions probably for DYCD, but I'd like to know if Moya is advocating uh for the new proposal around the 20 million dollars uh for um the called the education for integration and equity program, um an important allocation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'd just like to know what those conversations have been like.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We have been in touch with and have received a proposal that was been put out for the 20 million.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I think you see some folks in the space who are here now.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and um we have touch space with DYCTs, but those on conversations are ongoing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I don't have anything like new to share, but happy to um we will be listening in to the DYCD hearing as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As you know, adult literacy is very important to Moya and connected to the language justice work that we're doing generally, um, but that's what I have to share right now.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about proactive planning to address potential federal cuts to adult literacy and English Language Learner programs.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Proactive Planning for Federal Literacy Cuts

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: No, I I think we we missed it, it was right before this one.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, but um I think I think what's important to note here is obviously um there is an RFP that has challenges that came out in 24.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're gonna continue to ask critical questions around re-looking at that RFP for what we knew were the consequences were gonna be, and in fact, played out that we continue to invest in adult literacy services.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So even with the expansion, it's still woefully underfunded and uh uh uh a critical service.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and I think uh we see that field is also under attack by the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So, what I would really like to see is like some proactive planning around understanding some of that money being uh swept away and our city being prepared to step in that gap.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee asks about MOIA's preparations for the 2030 Census and plans to collaborate with the newly established Office of Census.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions 2030 Census Preparations

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, okay, really quick question about the 2030 census.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, because as we know, it's gonna come up a lot faster than we realize, and uh, you know, in gearing up for that, knowing how important this is, um, just wanted to know uh what Moya has done in the past few years to prepare for the upcoming census, and then how are you planning to collaborate with the new Office of Census once it's established and sort of what the role of Moya will play?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, Moya is really clear.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Unfortunately, the planning for the next you know, for the previous cycle predates my time and most of our teams, but it's really clear to us that ensuring a full and accurate count of immigrant New Yorkers requires long-term sustained groundwork and not just outreach in in the year itself of the census.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So over the past several years, you know, Moya has worked around, you know, expanding language access and strengthening community partnerships, investing in multilingual public education and ethnic media engagement that we believe has helped build the infrastructure that is essential for reaching historically underaccounted communities.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um those same systems are critical for for the census outreach as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and looking forward, um, you know, we are uh uh looking forward to playing an active role in supporting the census efforts by working closely with our community-based organizations and sister agencies.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks if there are plans to translate the family preparedness flyer into additional languages like Russian and Urdu.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Family Preparedness Flyer Translations

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, chair.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, we're gonna do some rapid questions and responses, which we have till four o'clock, uh then we lose our translators, and we can't have that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh so for family preparedness and family separation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, during our round tables, uh, some of our CBOs shared, you know, that they are supporting community members preparing for their family separation, preparing families for possible separation.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And I know that Moya has a flyer posted online.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It is currently available, I think, online in eight languages.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Is there um is there are there plans to expand the amount of languages that it is available for in?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, so right now um I I think it's also available in Russian and we are working on um the Urdu uh translation right now so it could be available in its top 10 we're always um open to feedback as well if there are additional languages that you might be in encountering in your neighborhood and if I may chair on the RLC um because I just got that other number just I wanted to make the note that the data that I provided was until the end of March so there's more work that happened on the DOE side as well as with the rapid response to your collaborative one of the the the main contract um there have been about 137 screenings I will just note that although those numbers seem low there are those are very comprehensive complex cases that require um you know very dedicated and experienced attorneys just wanted to say that uh because they're very traumatic cases too that although they seem low they're actually pretty significant and in retrospect but thank you for letting me add that no I I appreciate that I want I want um and and it it was really about making sure and understanding that people knew about these services and knew how to get connected and that they were availing themselves when they needed support um and so other than the flyer and and things like that what other resources can CBOs look at loyal at Moya to for um family separation I can I can um take this commissioner um so part of the outreach campaign which we do do with our outreach team that's one bucket um the the commissioner mentioned this at the top, but we also uh created, we had a Moya um legal hotline bank with ONA um back in um April, I think April 21st, when we received about 227 calls.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about resources for family separation, including standby guardianship clinics and training provided by the Immigrant Justice Corps.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Family Separation Resources and Guardianship Clinics

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We understand that this is an issue that the community really cares about, and that was a sort of the way that we we provided that outreach.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We worked with ethnic media at the time to spread the word and awareness.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So that's another bucket.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We also have the rapid response in schools in which families are provided KYR on what standby guardianship and their and their options, as well as our legal support centers provided in FY26 at least 14 guardianship clinics of serving a total of 364 people.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then the last one that I'll mention is that our legal technical mentor, the immigrant justice corp, also delivered a five-part training series on special immigration juvenile status, although not directly on family separation, but it's also adjacent to the issue.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I wanted to uplift that work as well.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks how MOIA coordinates with ACS to address family separation issues and support affected children and caregivers.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Coordination with ACS on Family Separation

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And do you coordinate with ACS at all for that?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes, we we do, and we talk to them frequently.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I know that there are a lot of uh changing issues that happened.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So as the commissioner mentioned at the top, we have um through through our our interagency efforts, we do we do um connect with them as well on any you know federal changes and things to that extent.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks how MOIA is collaborating with council-funded interpreter banks and language service worker cooperatives to improve compliance.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Language Access Compliance and Interpreter Banks

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I want to get into language access.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: As you know, we have some of the strongest language access laws in the country.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and what we're seeing uh, you know, obviously it requires agencies to appoint language access coordinators and to translate.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um but in 2023 there was an evaluation by the city's language access uh like the secret shopper program, and it said that more than half of the centers evaluated were in violation of local law authority.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And you know, since then the council has been uh in fiscal 23 through 26.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: The council began investing in the creation of the um language service worker cooperatives um as well as the community interpreter bank.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh so can you talk to me a little bit about how uh can Moya increase collaboration and coordination with the council funded initiatives, the bank and the language service worker cooperatives?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We've had um conversations with the council funded um uh both the community interpretive bank or organizations and uh the community language workers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: On the community interpreter bank specifically, Moya organized a virtual convening this February where the NYIC has shared information about the services that are being offered by the community interpretive bank.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, 33 different agencies were in fact present.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and our engagement really doesn't end there.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, you know, Moya has also included information about their third-party language resources, including information about the language justice collaborative and other nonprofits in our monthly language access newsletter and agency uh language access partners.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And how do you measure uh compliance across city agencies when it comes to those uh kind of gaps that you see?

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks how MOIA monitors compliance across city agencies, referencing the secret shopper evaluations that identified significant gaps.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Monitoring Compliance and Secret Shopper Evaluations

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, Moya is uh, you know, committed to advancing language justice really as a core principle of how we do our work.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um language access is essential to ensuring that immigrant New Yorkers can fully participate in civic life and accessing critical services and engaging with agencies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, you know, the city's responsibility goes as I've said earlier, beyond um the comp but beyond compliance.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, Moya coordinates across the city agencies through a combination of oversight and technical assistance that we offer and uh specifically on with uh other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, we monitor this technical assistance to all of the local law third agencies and convene them on a regular basis.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, as I mentioned in my testimony just this April, we did um do our large convening of language access coordinators across agencies to not just underscore the importance of language access but um make sure that there's an opportunity for agencies to um share some of their best practices and talk a little bit about it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And that there's a law on it, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Like that they compliance is important, I think, in that sense because you know, the reasoning behind putting that law forward was that we we need to start closing those gaps between those agencies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
