utterances.text: So you know, I uh just a pinpoint that because I think that it is um and and you know that so you know and I think that that's where you're getting at with um making sure that there's a good relationship and collaboration there, but in terms of making sure that there's compliance, I want to just uh just to understand how we measure that and we fill those gaps and and hold them accountable to to making sure that they are providing those services.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about efforts to embed language access as a core government function, referencing a recent memo issued to city agencies.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Embedding Language Access in Government

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think part of it is also like having an ongoing uh you know, uh communication with these agencies.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, after that convening, DM Sue, um, Deputy Mary Julie Sue and I um actually issued a memo to all the local law authority agencies just reiterating not just compliance, I think that we we need to have a larger conversation about how do we achieve language justice.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and so we talked um uh in that um you know memo about how we're in embedding language access really as a core government function.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So to your point, it is absolutely the law, but we can go deeper than the spirit of the law if we really want to ensure equitable access for immigrant New Yorkers.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about protections for transnational workers and efforts to address the impacts of extreme heat and climate change on immigrant laborers.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Transnational Worker Protections and Climate Impacts

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Agreed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, and I know with the immigrant worker protections, because I know you just mentioned uh commission uh deputy mayor, sorry, Sue, uh there were a lot of discussions, especially in some of our roundtables, around being having concerns about workers who may live in the city but work outside of the city and having those protections applied to them.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So does the administration plan to connect with open with local jurisdictions to talk about how do we coordinate, further protect, um, and how do we do that, knowing that you know they they're not necessarily a sanctuary city like ours.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I mean, these are real concerns that affect people's willingness to move um safely for employment and opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Uh, we are open to coordinating uh with workers across the country.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, do you want to share about the recent round table?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, we are um taking this international.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We actually met with um the transnational worker advocates um with the premier sue, where advocates came from actually Latin America to talk about some of the issues that they have been having.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Another touch point we've had most recently has been with workers and in connection with the chief climate officer to talk about the impacts of heat and climate on workers as well.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So there have been a new touch points that we've made um with the workers specific um issues that you've lied.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you so much for that.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about anti-harassment outreach for immigrant tenants in collaboration with HPD to prevent unlawful evictions.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Anti-Harassment Outreach for Immigrant Tenants

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I love that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I think that that's that's great, and you know, it's that kind of innovation that we hope for when we're looking at how do we really support people on a real road in a real world way.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um in as you know, we we held a joint hearing um with the housing committee to talk about some of the housing issues that we were seeing and the and the the uh intersectionality around immigration and housing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Do you know if any of the increased funding for expansion of legal services and the executive plan will be involved in anti-harassment outreach in collaboration with HPD?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We don't have that information, but following that hearing, we have been um working with them more on some of the gaps that you're hearing um laid out um and further strengthening the coordination that we have on the ground.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thank you for hosting that because it was a very timely hearing.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks for an update on MOIA's collaboration with the Office of LGBTQIA Affairs to support TGNC and non-binary immigrants under Local Law 73.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Collaboration with Office of LGBTQIA Affairs

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, it was you know, I really want to make sure that we are uh keeping people housed.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I mean, it's one of the only ways that we can really uh make sure that and properly housed, right?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um trying to make sure I get through.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, I have two two two more things really quickly.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, one I really want to talk about our supporting our LGBTQ plus immigrants.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, you know, this is a subset of a subset that we we cannot forget about, and we have to keep uplifting um some of the questions there.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So can you please provide an update on your collaboration with Director Taylor Brown of the new Office of LGBTQIA affairs?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, specifically as it relates to how they meet the needs of our immigrant New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I think you said it best that it is government's responsibility to support the most vulnerable, and sometimes we have communities that are far more vulnerable than that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And so I I appreciate the question.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: You know, Moya has been meeting uh regularly with uh Director Taylor Brown of the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA plus affairs um to discuss next steps in implementing specifically local law 73, um, which was passed by the council last year in conjunction with the mayor's office of equality and the mayor's office of LGBTQIA affairs.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We are um through that local law uh there is a uh conducting uh a comprehensive assessment of the services that are currently available to um it uh TGNC um communities, um, specifically um transgender and gender non-conforming, non-binary um intersex immigrants, and the findings of this assessment um will directly inform a phased implementation plan really designed to address um and identify any of the service gaps across the city.

chapters.summary: Chair Althea Stevens asks about MOIA's partnership with the newly created Office of Mass Engagement to support outreach and information sharing.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens Questions Partnership with Office of Mass Engagement

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And just in the same vein as the last question, you know, I'm hoping that some of that uh translates into actual dollars there to support those programs, outreach, and things like that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So I know that you may not know similarly to the last one if it's there yet, but if we can continue advocacy for that.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And then lastly, I know the mayor's office of mass engagement was newly created this year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It received 2.8 million in baseline funding, started in fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, can you talk a little bit about your partnership with them?

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: How you may uh use that office to help support some of the programming that you have.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um yeah, yeah, I'm happy to say that I just spoke to them um again yesterday.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So we are um very much in close um communication and in lockstep in regards to us sharing information regarding immigrants as well as supporting them with um some forward facing information coordination and some outreach uh efforts that we've made um I know that we've partnered with them on on on a couple of things already and that partnership will continue.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We're also in the same building so we see each other on the elevators as well but we are working very closely with them and across teams not just one but you the whole office as a whole.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee concludes the hearing by asking about MOIA's role in the upcoming World Cup and the launch of the New York City Neighborhood Passport initiative.
chapters.title: Chair Lee Questions World Cup and Neighborhood Passport Initiative

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I want to be in that elevator.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Combining time talk and spirits of ideas I'm gonna stay stake out the elevators now.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: We will be in communication there's a lot more I had 11 pages of questions obviously um so you know I know um but you know it it is extremely complex as you know there's many layers to it many subdivisions and and um answers that I I really wanted to get in terms of the executive budget it's it's difficult to go through and make sure that we properly are supporting each and every aspect of it.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm gonna continue to fight for as much as I can on my end and I hope that internally you're for fighting for um our immigrant neighbors I know you are and I'm actually really really impressed with some of the stuff that I've seen so far and some of the answers to the question thank you for being so prepared.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I'm gonna give it over now to my co-chair.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I want to just say thank you to the chair for being a fighter um and and whatever questions I'm happy to um have a follow up conversation with you just so you can get the answers.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: All right, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Actually, um, since we have like two minutes, um, one final question that's more fun about the World Cup that's happening right now this year.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But um, just out of curiosity, because we have so many great small businesses, immigrant-owned parts of our city, especially Queens.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: I mean, I think the Queen's council members fight over who has the better food in their district.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: But um we we, you know, we know that this is coming up, and so just was curious to know.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um, I know that the mayor's office is launching New York City neighborhood passport, and so are you guys working with them at all to encourage folks to explore a lot of different neighborhoods in the city and get out there and I'm glad to end on a fun note.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Yeah, New York City Neighborhood Passport is a a citywide program it launches in on June eleventh.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um and the idea is to encourage New Yorkers to visit um and explore the five boroughs and many of the immigrant neighborhoods that make this city um so great.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Um participants are collecting uh artist design stamps um from hundreds of community organizations and cultural institutions and public events from dance performances and film screenings.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: It's really a great opportunity for the community to get involved and in and explore um our great city.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: And um if you want recommendations from all of us in terms of which restaurants are the best in our area, let us know.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Add it to a list.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Okay, so yeah, and I think with that we are done for the day.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thank you so much, Commissioner and everyone else for being here.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh just a reminder about the public hearing.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Oh, yeah, so just what la and again public hearing Wednesday, June tenth.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Make sure everyone signs up.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: So thank you so much, everyone, and that concludes our third day of hearings.

utterances.speaker: Luisa Linares
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

summary: The Committee on Finance, jointly with the Committee on Children and Youth and the Committee on Immigration, held day three of the FY27 Executive Budget Hearings, focusing on the Administration for Children's Services (ACS), the Department of Youth and Community Development (DYCD), and the Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs (MOIA).

Members expressed deep concerns regarding proposed savings measures, including the closure of four preventive service programs at ACS, the high census of youth in secure detention facilities, and the transition of the Office of Neighborhood Safety (ONS) from DYCD to the Office of Community Safety.

The committees extensively debated the outcomes of the newly released COMPASS after-school RFP by DYCD, questioning the concentration of contracts among large providers, the loss of funding for long-standing community-based organizations, and the lack of direct parent input in the selection process.
title: Committee on Finance
